header: Introduction to Algebraic Formulas and Functions
content:
Algebraic Formulas
Sometimes we are not given the values of a function in table form, rather we are given the values in an explicit formula. Formulas arise in many applications. For example, the area of a circle of radius r is given by the formula A(r) = πr². When an object is thrown upward from the ground with an initial velocity v ft/s, its height above the ground from the time it is thrown until it hits the ground is given by the formula s(t) = −16t² + vt. When P dollars are invested in an account at an annual interest rate r compounded continuously, the amount of money after t years is given by the formula A(t) = Pe^(rt). Algebraic formulas are important tools to calculate function values. Often we also represent these functions visually in graph form.
description: No figures or tables are included in this chunk.
summary: This section discusses the use of algebraic formulas to define functions in various real-world applications.

content: Given an algebraic formula for a function f, the graph of f is the set of points ⎛x, f(x)⎞, where x is in the domain of f and f(x) is in the range. To graph a function given by a formula, it is helpful to begin by using the formula to create a table of inputs and outputs. If the domain of f consists of an infinite number of values, we cannot list all of them, but because listing some of the inputs and outputs can be very useful, it is often a good way to begin.
summary: This section introduces the basic concepts of functions and their graphical representations.

content: When creating a table of inputs and outputs, we typically check to determine whether zero is an output. Those values of x where f(x)=0 are called the zeros of a function. For example, the zeros of f(x)= x² − 4 are x= ±2. The zeros determine where the graph of f intersects the x-axis, which gives us more information about the shape of the graph of the function. The graph of a function may never intersect the x-axis, or it may intersect multiple (or even infinitely many) times.
summary: This section discusses how to find zeros of a function and their significance in graphing.

content: Another point of interest is the y-intercept, if it exists. The y-intercept is given by ⎛0, f(0)⎞. Since a function has exactly one output for each input, the graph of a function can have, at most, one y-intercept. If x=0 is in the domain of a function f, then f has exactly one y-intercept. If x=0 is not in the domain of f, then f has no y-intercept. Similarly, for any real number c, if c is in the domain of f, there is exactly one output f(c), and the line x=c intersects the graph of f exactly once. On the other hand, if c is not in the domain of f, f(c) is not defined and the line x=c does not intersect the graph of f.
summary: This section explains how to determine the y-intercept and its relation to the domain of the function.

header: The Vertical Line Test: A Key Property of Functions
content: This property is summarized in the vertical line test. Rule: Vertical Line Test: Given a function f, every vertical line that may be drawn intersects the graph of f no more than once. If any vertical line intersects a set of points more than once, the set of points does not represent a function. We can use this test to determine whether a set of plotted points represents the graph of a function.
description: Figure 1.8 (a) The set of plotted points represents the graph of a function because every vertical line intersects the set of points, at most, once. (b) The set of plotted points does not represent the graph of a function because some vertical lines intersect the set of points more than once.
summary: This section introduces the vertical line test as a method to verify whether a graph represents a function.

header: Intervals of Increasing and Decreasing Functions
content: A function with this property is said to be increasing. It is important to note, however, that a function can be increasing on some interval or intervals and decreasing over a different interval or intervals. For example, using our temperature function in Figure 1.6, we can see that the function is decreasing on the interval (0, 4), increasing on the interval (4, 14), and then decreasing on the interval (14, 23).
description: Figure 1.6 demonstrates a temperature function with both increasing and decreasing intervals.
summary: The chunk elaborates on the concept that functions can exhibit both increasing and decreasing behavior across different intervals.

content:

summary: This chunk presents a formal definition of increasing functions based on their behavior over specified intervals.

content: We say that a function f is decreasing on the interval I if for all x1, x2 ∈ I, f(x1) ≥ f(x2) if x1 < x2. We say that a function f is strictly decreasing on the interval I if for all x1, x2 ∈ I, f(x1) > f(x2) if x1 < x2.
summary: This section defines what it means for a function to be decreasing and strictly decreasing over an interval.

header: Examples of Increasing and Decreasing Functions
content: For example, the function f(x) = 3x is increasing on the interval (−∞, ∞) because 3x < 3x whenever x1 < x2. On the other hand, the function f(x) = −x^3 is decreasing on the interval (−∞, ∞) because −x^3 > −x^3 whenever x1 < x2.
description: Figure 1.11 shows (a) the function f(x) = 3x which is increasing on the interval (−∞, ∞) and (b) the function f(x) = −x^3 which is decreasing on the interval (−∞, ∞).
summary: This section illustrates specific examples of increasing and decreasing functions.

content: Now that we have reviewed the basic characteristics of functions, we can see what happens to these properties when we combine functions in different ways, using basic mathematical operations to create new functions.
summary: This section introduces the concept of combining functions to create new functions.

content: For example, if the cost for a company to manufacture x items is described by the function C(x) and the revenue created by the sale of x items is described by the function R(x), then the profit on the manufacture and sale of x items is defined as P(x) = R(x) − C(x).
summary: This section describes how to define profit as the difference between revenue and cost functions.

header: Composite Functions through Composition of Two Functions
content: Alternatively, we can create a new function by composing two functions. For example, given the functions f(x) = x^2 and g(x) = 3x + 1, the composite function f∘g is defined such that (f∘g)(x) = f(g(x)) = (g(x))^2 = (3x + 1)^2.
summary: This section explains the concept of composite functions formed by the composition of two different functions.

content:
Example 1.9
Application Involving a Composite Function
A store is advertising a sale of 20% off all merchandise. Caroline has a coupon that entitles her to an additional 15% off any item, including sale merchandise. If Caroline decides to purchase an item with an original price of x dollars, how much will she end up paying if she applies her coupon to the sale price? Solve this problem by using a composite function.
Solution
Since the sale price is 20% off the original price, if an item is x dollars, its sale price is given by f(x)=0.80x. Since the coupon entitles an individual to 15% off the price of any item, if an item is y dollars, the price after applying the coupon is given by g(y)=0.85y. Therefore, if the price is originally x dollars, its sale price will be f(x)=0.80x and then its final price after the coupon will be g(f(x))=0.85(0.80x)=0.68x.
summary: This example demonstrates the application of composite functions in calculating discounts.

header: Exploring Final Price Calculations with Composite Functions
content: 1.6 If items are on sale for 10% off their original price, and a customer has a coupon for an additional 30% off, what will be the final price for an item that is originally x dollars, after applying the coupon to the sale price?
summary: This question asks how to determine the final price of an item after applying multiple discounts.

header: Symmetry Properties of Functions and Their Graphs
content:
Symmetry of Functions
The graphs of certain functions have symmetry properties that help us understand the function and the shape of its graph. For example, consider the function f(x)= x 4 −2x 2 −3 shown in Figure 1.13(a). If we take the part of the curve that lies to the right of the y-axis and flip it over the y-axis, it lays exactly on top of the curve to the left of the y-axis. In this case, we say the function has symmetry about the y-axis. On the other hand, consider the function f(x)= x 3 −4x shown in Figure 1.13(b). If we take the graph and rotate it 180° about the origin, the new graph will look exactly the same. In this case, we say the function has symmetry about the origin.
description: Figure 1.13(a) illustrates the symmetry about the y-axis for the function f(x)= x^4 - 2x^2 - 3. Figure 1.13(b) depicts the symmetry about the origin for the function f(x)= x^3 - 4x.
summary: This section discusses symmetry in functions and graph shapes.

header: Understanding Symmetry in Functions - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs
content:
Figure 1.13 (a) A graph that is symmetric about the y-axis. (b) A graph that is symmetric about the origin.
If we are given the graph of a function, it is easy to see whether the graph has one of these symmetry properties. But without a graph, how can we determine algebraically whether a function f has symmetry?
description: Figures illustrating graphs of functions with symmetry: (a) symmetric about the y-axis, (b) symmetric about the origin.
summary: The section introduces the concept of symmetry in functions and how to assess it algebraically.

header: Symmetry Properties of Functions - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs
content: Looking at Figure 1.14 again, we see that since f is symmetric about the y-axis, if the point (x, y) is on the graph, the point (−x, y) is on the graph. In other words, f(−x)= f(x). If a function f has this property, we say f is an even function, which has symmetry about the y-axis.
summary: This section explains how to identify even functions using their symmetry about the y-axis.

header: Example of an Even Function - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs
content: For example, f(x)= x² is even because f(−x)=(−x)² = x² = f(x).
summary: An example illustrates how the function f(x) = x² meets the criteria for being even.

header: Understanding Odd Functions - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs
content: In contrast, looking at Figure 1.14 again, if a function f is symmetric about the origin, then whenever the point (x, y) is on the graph, the point (−x, −y) is also on the graph. In other words, f(−x)=−f(x). If f has this property, we say f is an odd function, which has symmetry about the origin.
summary: This section defines odd functions based on their symmetry with respect to the origin.

header: Example of an Odd Function - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs

header: Definitions of Even and Odd Functions - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs
content:
Definition
If f(x)= f(−x) for all x in the domain of f, then f is an even function. An even function is symmetric about the y-axis.
If f(−x)=−f(x) for all x in the domain of f, then f is an odd function. An odd function is symmetric about the origin.
summary: This section provides the formal definitions of even and odd functions based on their symmetry properties.

header: Determining the Nature of Functions - Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs

Example 1.10
Even and Odd Functions
Determine whether each of the following functions is even, odd, or neither.
a. f(x)=−5x⁴ +7x² −2
summary: This example prompts the determination of the nature of specified functions, testing understanding of even and odd functions.

content: To determine whether a function is even or odd, we evaluate f(−x) and compare it to f(x) and −f(x).
summary: This section introduces the method to evaluate functions for evenness or oddness.

content: a. f(−x)=−5(−x)4 +7(−x)2 −2=−5x4 +7x2 −2= f(x). Therefore, f is even.
summary: Analysis of the first function confirms that it is even.

content: b. f(−x)=2(−x)5 −4(−x)+5=−2x5 +4x+5. Now, f(−x)≠ f(x). Furthermore, noting that −f(x)=−2x5 +4x−5, we see that f(−x)≠−f(x). Therefore, f is neither even nor odd.
summary: The second function is determined to be neither even nor odd.
The third function is determined to be odd.

content: One symmetric function that arises frequently is the absolute value function, written as |x|. The absolute value function is defined as ⎧−x, x<0 (1.2) f(x)=⎨ . ⎩ x, x≥0.
summary: Introduces and defines the absolute value function.

header: Characteristics of the Absolute Value Function
content: Some students describe this function by stating that it 'makes everything positive.' By the definition of the absolute value function, we see that if x<0, then |x|=−x>0, and if x>0, then |x|= x>0. However, for x=0, |x|=0. Therefore, it is more accurate to say that for all nonzero inputs, the output is positive, but if x=0, the output |x|=0.
summary: Discusses the behavior of the absolute value function across different ranges of x.

header: Range of the Absolute Value Function
content: We conclude that the range of the absolute value function is ⎧ ⎨y|y≥0⎫ ⎬. In Figure 1.14, we see that the absolute value function is symmetric about the y-axis and is therefore an even function.
description: Figure 1.14 illustrates the graph of f(x)=|x|, demonstrating its symmetry about the y-axis.
summary: Summaries the range of the absolute value function and its symmetry characteristics.

header: Graph of the Absolute Value Function
description: Figure 1.14 illustrates the graph of f(x)=|x|, demonstrating its symmetry about the y-axis.

content:
Example 1.11
Working with the Absolute Value Function
Find the domain and range of the function f(x)=2|x−3|+4.
Solution
Since the absolute value function is defined for all real numbers, the domain of this function is (−∞, ∞). Since |x−3|≥0 for all x, the function f(x)=2|x−3|+4≥4. Therefore, the range is, at most, the set {y | y≥4}.
summary: This section introduces the absolute value function and its domain and range characteristics.

content: To see that the range is, in fact, this whole set, we need to show that for y≥4 there exists a real number x such that 2|x−3|+4=y. A real number x satisfies this equation as long as |x−3|= (y−4). Since y≥4, we know y−4≥0, and thus the right-hand side of the equation is nonnegative, so it is possible that there is a solution.
summary: This part shows the mathematical reasoning for finding solutions to the equation involving the absolute value function.

header: Finding Solutions to the Absolute Value Equation
content: Furthermore, |x−3|={−(x−3) if x<3; x−3 if x≥3}. Therefore, we see there are two solutions: x= ± (y−4)+3.
summary: This segment outlines the two cases for the absolute value equation and derives the solutions.

header: Concluding the Range of the Function
content: The range of this function is {y | y≥4}.
summary: This final statement concludes the determination of the function's range.

header: Functions and Graphs Overview - Basic Classes of Functions
content:
Learning Objectives
1.2.1 Calculate the slope of a linear function and interpret its meaning.
1.2.2 Recognize the degree of a polynomial.
1.2.3 Find the roots of a quadratic polynomial.
1.2.4 Describe the graphs of basic odd and even polynomial functions.
1.2.5 Identify a rational function.
1.2.6 Describe the graphs of power and root functions.
1.2.7 Explain the difference between algebraic and transcendental functions.
1.2.8 Graph a piecewise-defined function.
1.2.9 Sketch the graph of a function that has been shifted, stretched, or reflected from its initial graph position.
summary: The section outlines the learning objectives for understanding various classes of functions.

content: We have studied the general characteristics of functions, so now let’s examine some specific classes of functions. We begin by reviewing the basic properties of linear and quadratic functions, and then generalize to include higher-degree polynomials. By combining root functions with polynomials, we can define general algebraic functions and distinguish them from the transcendental functions we examine later in this chapter. We finish this section with examples of piecewise-defined functions and take a look at how to sketch the graph of a function that has been shifted, stretched, or reflected from its initial form.
summary: This chunk introduces the analysis of specific classes of functions, focusing first on linear and quadratic types.

header: Linear Functions - Definition and Graph Behavior
content: The easiest type of function to consider is a linear function. Linear functions have the form f(x)=ax+b, where a and b are constants. In Figure 1.15, we see examples of linear functions when a is positive, negative, and zero. Note that if a>0, the graph of the line rises as x increases. In other words, f(x)=ax+b is increasing on (−∞, ∞). If a<0, the graph of the line falls as x increases. In this case, f(x)=ax+b is decreasing on (−∞, ∞). If a=0, the line is horizontal.
description: Figure 1.15 illustrates examples of linear functions with various slopes, showing cases where the slope is positive, negative, or zero.
summary: This chunk details the characteristics of linear functions, including their standard form and behavior based on the value of 'a'.

content: As suggested by Figure 1.15, the graph of any linear function is a line. One of the distinguishing features of a line is its slope. The slope is the change in y for each unit change in x. The slope measures both the steepness and the direction of a line. If the slope is positive, the line points upward when moving from left to right. If the slope is negative, the line points downward when moving from left to right. If the slope is zero, the line is horizontal. To calculate the slope of a line, we need to determine the ratio of the change in y versus the change in x. To do so, we choose any two points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) on the line and calculate (y2 - y1) / (x2 - x1). In Figure 1.16, we see this ratio is independent of the points chosen.
description: Figure 1.16 illustrates how to calculate the slope of a linear function using any two points on the line, highlighting the consistency of the slope value.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of slope in linear functions and how to calculate it using any two points.

header: Exploring the Slope of Linear Functions
content: Figure 1.16 For any linear function, the slope (y − y2)/(x2 − x1) is independent of the choice of points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) on the line.
description: An illustration showing that the slope remains constant regardless of the chosen points on a linear function.
summary: This figure demonstrates the consistency of slope in linear functions.

content: Consider line L passing through points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2). Let Δy = y2 - y1 and Δx = x2 - x1 denote the changes in y and x, respectively. The slope of the line is m = Δy/Δx.
summary: Definition of slope based on changes in coordinates of points on a line.

header: Relationship Between Slope and Linear Functions
content: We now examine the relationship between slope and the formula for a linear function. Consider the linear function given by the formula f(x) = ax + b. As discussed earlier, we know the graph of a linear function is given by a line.
summary: Introduction to how slope relates to the formula of linear functions.

content: We can use our definition of slope to calculate the slope of this line. As shown, we can determine the slope by calculating (y2 - y1)/(x2 - x1) for any points (x1, y1) and (x2, y2) on the line. Evaluating the function f at x = 0, we see that (0, b) is a point on this line. Evaluating this function at x = 1, we see that (1, a + b) is also a point on this line.
summary: This section details the steps to calculate the slope using specific points from the linear function.

header: Conclusion on Slope of Linear Functions
content: Therefore, the slope of this line is (a + b) - b / (1 - 0) = a. We have shown that the coefficient a is the slope of the line. We can conclude that the formula f(x) = ax + b describes a line with slope a.
summary: Final confirmation of the relationship between the coefficient a and the slope of the line.

header: Understanding the Y-Intercept of Linear Functions
content: Furthermore, because this line intersects the y-axis at the point (0, b), we see that the y-intercept for this linear function is (0, b). We conclude that the formula f(x) = ax + b tells us the slope, a, and the y-intercept, (0, b), for this line.
summary: Discussion on the y-intercept in relation to the linear function's formula.

content: Since we often use the symbol m to denote the slope of a line, we can write f(x) = mx + b to denote the slope-intercept form of a linear function. Sometimes it is convenient to express a linear function in different ways.
summary: Exploration of alternative forms for representing linear functions.

header: Equation of a Linear Function Passing Through a Point
content: For example, suppose the graph of a linear function passes through the point (x1, y1) and the slope of the line is m. Since any other point (x, f(x)) on the graph of f must satisfy the equation f(x) - y1 / (x - x1) = m.
summary: Establishing the equation that describes a linear function based on a point and its slope.

header: Introduction to Linear Functions and Equations
content: this linear function can be expressed by writing f(x)−y =m(x−x ). We call this equation the point-slope equation for that linear function.
summary: This section introduces the point-slope equation for linear functions.

content: Since every nonvertical line is the graph of a linear function, the points on a nonvertical line can be described using the slope-interceptor point-slope equations. However, a vertical line does not represent the graph of a function and cannot be expressed in either of these forms.
summary: Explains how nonvertical lines relate to linear functions and the implications for vertical lines.

header: Vertical Lines and Standard Form Notation
content: Instead, a vertical line is described by the equation x=k for some constant k. Since neither the slope-intercept form nor the point-slope form allows for vertical lines, we use the notation ax+by=c, where a, b are both not zero, to denote the standard form of a line.
summary: This section describes how vertical lines are represented in mathematics using standard form.

content: Definition Consider a line passing through the point (x , y ) with slope m. The equation y−y =m(x−x ) is the point-slope equation for that line.
summary: Provides the definition of the point-slope equation with a specific reference point and slope.

content: Consider a line with slope m and y-intercept (0, b). The equation y=mx+b is an equation for that line in slope-intercept form.
summary: Describes the slope-intercept form of a line and its components.

header: The Standard Form of a Line
content: The standard form of a line is given by the equation ax+by=c, where a and b are both not zero. This form is more general because it allows for a vertical line, x=k.
summary: Details the standard form of a line and its generality compared to other forms.

content:
Polynomials
A linear function is a special type of a more general class of functions: polynomials. A polynomial function is any function that can be written in the form
f(x)=anx n +a n−1 x n−1 +…+a 1 x+a 0
(1.7)
for some integer n≥0 and constants an, a n−1,…,a 0, where an≠0. In the case when n=0, we allow for a 0=0; if a =0, the function f(x)=0 is called the zero function. The value n is called the degree of the polynomial; the constant an is called the leading coefficient.
summary: This section explains what polynomials are and their general form.

header: Degrees and Types of Polynomial Functions
content: A linear function of the form f(x)=mx+b is a polynomial of degree 1 if m≠0 and degree 0 if m=0. A polynomial of degree 0 is also called a constant function. A polynomial function of degree 2 is called a quadratic function. In particular, a quadratic function has the form f(x)=ax 2 +bx+c, where a≠0. A polynomial function of degree 3 is called a cubic function.
summary: This section categorizes polynomial functions based on their degree.

content:
Power Functions
Some polynomial functions are power functions. A power function is any function of the form f(x)=ax b, where a and b are any real numbers. The exponent in a power function can be any real number, but here we consider the case when the exponent is a positive integer. (We consider other cases later.)
summary: This section defines power functions and discusses their characteristics.

content: If the exponent is a positive integer, then f(x)=ax n is a polynomial. If n is even, then f(x)=ax n is an even function because f(−x)=a(−x) n =ax n if n is even. If n is odd, then f(x)=ax n is an odd function because f(−x)=a(−x) n =−ax n if n is odd (Figure 1.18).
description: Figure 1.18 illustrates the characteristics of even and odd functions based on their polynomial exponent.
summary: This section describes how the parity of the exponent in power functions defines their behavior as even or odd functions.

header: Understanding Even and Odd Functions in Chapter 1
content: Figure 1.18 (a) For any even integer n, f(x)=ax n is an even function. (b) For any odd integer n, f(x)=ax n is an odd function.
description: Figure 1.18 illustrates even and odd functions based on integer n.
summary: An introduction to even and odd functions with examples.

content: Behavior at Infinity To determine the behavior of a function f as the inputs approach infinity, we look at the values f(x) as the inputs, x, become larger. For some functions, the values of f(x) approach a finite number. For example, for the function f(x)=2+1/x, the values 1/x become closer and closer to zero for all values of x as they get larger and larger. For this function, we say “f(x) approaches two as x goes to infinity,” and we write f(x)→2 as x→∞. The line y=2 is a horizontal asymptote for the function f(x)=2+1/x because the graph of the function gets closer to the line as x gets larger.
summary: Explanation of how functions behave as inputs go to infinity.

header: Exploring Increasing Outputs in Functions as Inputs Grow
content: For other functions, the values f(x) may not approach a finite number but instead may become larger for all values of x as they get larger. In that case, we say “f(x) approaches infinity as x approaches infinity,” and we write f(x)→∞ as x→∞. For example, for the function f(x)=3x^2, the outputs f(x) become larger as the inputs x get larger. We can conclude that the function f(x)=3x^2 approaches infinity as x approaches infinity, and we write 3x^2→∞ as x→∞.
summary: Understanding the approach to infinity for certain functions.

header: End Behavior of Functions as Inputs Approach Negative Infinity
content: The behavior as x→−∞ and the meaning of f(x)→−∞ as x→∞ or x→−∞ can be defined similarly. We can describe what happens to the values of f(x) as x→∞ and as x→−∞ as the end behavior of the function.
summary: Explains end behavior of functions at both positive and negative infinity.

content: To understand the end behavior for polynomial functions, we can focus on quadratic and cubic functions. The behavior for higher-degree polynomials can be analyzed similarly. Consider a quadratic function f(x)=ax^2+bx+c. If a>0, the values f(x)→∞ as x→±∞. If a<0, the values f(x)→−∞ as x→±∞. Since the graph of a quadratic function is a parabola, the parabola opens upward if a>0; the parabola opens downward if a<0. (See Figure 1.19(a).)
description: Figure 1.19(a) depicts the behavior of a quadratic function based on the sign of 'a'.
summary: Examines how the leading coefficient of quadratic functions influences their end behavior.

header: Cubic Functions and Their End Behavior Analysis
content: Now consider a cubic function f(x)=ax^3+bx^2+cx+d. If a>0, then f(x)→∞ as x→∞ and f(x)→−∞ as x→−∞. If a<0, then f(x)→−∞ as x→∞ and f(x)→∞ as x→−∞. As we can see from both of these graphs, the leading term of the polynomial determines the end behavior. (See Figure 1.19(b).)
description: Figure 1.19(b) shows the end behavior of a cubic function according to the leading term.
summary: Explains how cubic functions react to increasing and decreasing inputs.

header: Overview of Polynomial Functions and Their Graphs
content: Figure 1.19 (a) For a quadratic function, if the leading coefficient a>0, the parabola opens upward. If a<0, the parabola opens downward. (b) For a cubic function f, if the leading coefficient a>0, the values f(x)→∞ as x→∞ and the values f(x)→−∞ as x→−∞. If the leading coefficient a<0, the opposite is true.
description: Figure 1.19 illustrates the behaviors of quadratic and cubic functions based on the sign of their leading coefficients.
summary: This section discusses how the leading coefficient affects the direction of polynomials' graphs.

content: Another characteristic of the graph of a polynomial function is where it intersects the x-axis. To determine where a function f intersects the x-axis, we need to solve the equation f(x)=0 for x. In the case of the linear function f(x)=mx+b, the x-intercept is given by solving the equation mx+b=0. In this case, we see that the x-intercept is given by (−b/m, 0).
summary: This section describes how to find x-intercepts of polynomial functions.

content: In the case of a quadratic function, finding the x-intercept(s) requires finding the zeros of a quadratic equation: ax^2 + bx + c = 0. In some cases, it is easy to factor the polynomial ax^2 + bx + c to find the zeros. If not, we make use of the quadratic formula.
summary: This section explains how to find zeros of quadratic functions, often requiring the quadratic formula.

content: Rule: The Quadratic Formula Consider the quadratic equation ax^2 + bx + c = 0, where a≠0. The solutions of this equation are given by the quadratic formula x = (−b ± √(b² − 4ac)) / (2a). If the discriminant b²−4ac>0, this formula tells us there are two real numbers that satisfy the quadratic equation. If b²−4ac=0, this formula tells us there is only one solution, and it is a real number. If b²−4ac<0, no real numbers satisfy the quadratic equation.
summary: This section articulates the quadratic formula and its implications based on the discriminant's value.

content: In the case of higher-degree polynomials, it may be more complicated to determine where the graph intersects the x-axis. In some instances, it is possible to find the x-intercepts by factoring the polynomial to find its zeros. In other cases, it is impossible to calculate the exact values of the x-intercepts. However, as we see later in the text, in cases such as this, we can use analytical tools to approximate (to a very high degree) where the x-intercepts are located. Here we focus on the graphs of polynomials for which we can calculate their zeros explicitly.
summary: This section discusses the challenges of finding x-intercepts for higher-degree polynomial functions.

content: Alargevarietyofreal-worldsituationscanbedescribedusingmathematicalmodels.Amathematicalmodelisamethodofsimulatingreal-lifesituationswithmathematicalequations.Physicists,engineers,economists,andotherresearchersdevelop models by combining observation with quantitative data to develop equations, functions, graphs, and other mathematical tools to describe the behavior of various systems accurately.Modelsareusefulbecausetheyhelppredictfutureoutcomes.
summary: This section defines mathematical models and their applications in various fields.

content: Examples of mathematical models include the study of population dynamics, investigations of weather patterns, and predictions of product sales.
summary: Examples are provided to illustrate different types of mathematical models.

content: As an example, let’s consider a mathematical model that a company could use to describe its revenue for the sale of a particular item.The amount of revenue R a company receives for the sale of n items sold at a price of p dollars per item is described by the equation R= p·n.
summary: An example of a revenue model using a simple equation.

content: The company is interested in how the sales change as the price of the item changes.
summary: Discussion on the relationship between pricing and sales.

content: Suppose the data in Table 1.6 show the number of units a company sells as a function of the price per item.
description: Table 1.6 presents data correlating sales units with price per item.
summary: Introduction to a data table illustrating the relationship between sales and price.

content: Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs 45
summary: Introduction to Chapter 1 focusing on functions and graphs.

header: Data Representation: Units Sold as a Relation to Price
content: Table 1.6 Number of Units Sold n (in Thousands) as a Function of Price per Unit p (in Dollars)
description: Table showcasing the number of units sold (in thousands) corresponding to different price points (in dollars).
summary: Presents a table showing the relationship between units sold and price.

header: Graph Interpretation: Units Sold and Price Relation
content: In Figure 1.20, we see the graph the number of units sold (in thousands) as a function of price (in dollars). We note from the shape of the graph that the number of units sold is likely a linear function of price per item.
description: Graph illustrating the number of units sold as a function of price, suggesting a linear relationship.
summary: Describes a graph indicating that units sold depend linearly on price.

header: Linear Function Model for Units Sold
content: and the data can be closely approximated by the linear function n=−1.04p+26 for 0≤ p≤25, where n predicts the number of units sold in thousands.
summary: Defines a linear function model for estimating units sold based on price.

content: Using this linear function, the revenue (in thousands of dollars) can be estimated by the quadratic function R(p)= p·⎛−1.04p+26⎞=−1.04p^2 +26p for 0≤ p≤25.
summary: Discusses how to estimate revenue using a quadratic function derived from the linear model.

content: In Example 1.15, we use this quadratic function to predict the amount of revenue the company receives depending on the price the company charges per item. Not that we cannot conclude definitively the actual number of units sold for values of p, for which no data are collected.
summary: Highlights limitations in predicting actual sales data based on the derived function.

header: Conclusion: Approximating Units Sold from Price
content: However, given the other data values and the graph shown, it seems reasonable that the number of units sold (in thousands) if the price charged is p dollars may be close to the values predicted by the linear function n=−1.04p+26.
summary: Concludes that predicted sales values are likely accurate within the linear function.

content: A company is interested in predicting the amount of revenue it will receive depending on the price it charges for a particular item. Using the data from Table 1.6, the company arrives at the following quadratic function to model revenue R (in thousands of dollars) as a function of price per item p: R(p) = p·(−1.04p + 26) = −1.04p² + 26p for 0 ≤ p ≤ 25.
description: Table 1.6 provides data used to derive the revenue function, but the content does not reference any specific figure.
summary: Introduction to the revenue model using a quadratic function.

content: a. Predict the revenue if the company sells the item at a price of p = $5 and p = $17. Evaluating the revenue function at p = 5 and p = 17, we can conclude that R(5) = −1.04(5) + 26(5) = 104, so revenue = $104,000; R(17) = −1.04(17) + 26(17) = 141.44, so revenue = $141,440.
summary: Calculating revenue at specific price points.

header: Finding and Interpreting Zeros of the Revenue Function
content: b. The zeros of this function can be found by solving the equation −1.04p² + 26p = 0. When we factor the quadratic expression, we get p(−1.04p + 26) = 0. The solutions to this equation are given by p = 0, 25. For these values of p, the revenue is zero. When p = $0, the revenue is zero because the company is giving away its merchandise for free. When p = $25, the revenue is zero because the price is too high, and no one will buy any items.
summary: Finding and interpreting the zeros of the revenue function.

header: Graphical Representation of the Revenue Function
content: c. Knowing the fact that the function is quadratic, we also know the graph is a parabola. Since the leading coefficient is negative, the parabola opens downward. One property of parabolas is that they are symmetric about the axis, so since the zeros are at p = 0 and p = 25, the parabola must be symmetric about the line halfway between them, or p = 12.5.
description: The paragraph discusses the characteristics of the graph of the quadratic revenue function, indicating its symmetry and direction.
summary: Describing the graph of the revenue function and its symmetry.

content: The function is a parabola with zeros at p=0 and p=25, and it is symmetric about the line p=12.5, so the maximum revenue occurs at a price of p=$12.50 per item. At that price, the revenue is R(p)=−1.04(12.5)² +26(12.5)=$162,500.
summary: This section discusses the properties of a parabolic function and its maximum revenue point.

content: By allowing for quotients and fractional powers in polynomial functions, we create a larger class of functions. An algebraic function is one that involves addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, rational powers, and roots.
summary: This section defines algebraic functions and mentions the operations that can be performed.

content: Two types of algebraic functions are rational functions and root functions. Just as rational numbers are quotients of integers, rational functions are quotients of polynomials. In particular, a rational function is any function of the form f(x)= p(x)/q(x), where p(x) and q(x) are polynomials.
summary: This section categorizes algebraic functions into rational and root functions, explaining their definitions and forms.

content: For example, f(x)= (3x−1)/4 and g(x)= (5x+2)/(x²+1) are rational functions.
summary: This section provides specific examples of rational functions for clarity.

content: A root function is a power function of the form f(x)= x^(1/n), where n is a positive integer greater than one. For example, f(x)= x^(1/2) = √x is the square-root function and g(x)= x^(1/3) = ³√x is the cube-root function.
summary: This section defines root functions and provides examples of square-root and cube-root functions.

header: Combining Functions to Form Algebraic Functions
content: By allowing for compositions of root functions and rational functions, we can create other algebraic functions. For example, f(x)= 4−x² is an algebraic function.
summary: This section discusses how to combine different types of functions to create new algebraic functions.

content: Example 1.16 Finding Domain and Range for Algebraic Functions For each of the following functions, find the domain and range.
summary: Introduction to finding domain and range for algebraic functions.

content: a. f(x)= 3x−1 / 5x+2 To find the domain of f, we need x such that x≠−2/5. To find the range, we need to find the values y for which there exists a real number x such that (3x−1)/(5x+2)=y. When we multiply both sides of this equation by 5x+2, we see that x must satisfy the equation 5xy+2y=3x−1. From this equation, we can see that x must satisfy 2y+1= x(3−5y). If y=3/5, this equation has no solution. On the other hand, as long as y≠3/5, 2y+1 / (3−5y) satisfies this equation. We can conclude that the range of f is {y|y≠3/5}.
summary: Finding the domain and range for function a.

content: b. To find the domain of f, we need 4−x²≥0. When we factor, we write 4−x²=(2−x)(2+x)≥0. This inequality holds if and only if both terms are positive or both terms are negative. For both terms to be positive, we need to find x such that 2−x≥0 and 2+x≥0. The set two inequalities reduce to 2≥x and x≥−2. Therefore, the set {x|−2≤x≤2} must be part of the domain.
summary: Calculating the domain for function b using inequalities.

content: For both terms to be negative, we need 2−x≤0 and 2+x≥0. These inequalities also reduce to 2≤x and x≥−2. There are no values of x that satisfy both of these inequalities. Thus, we conclude the domain of this function is {x|−2≤x≤2}. If −2≤x≤2, then 0≤4−x²≤4. Therefore, 0≤4−x²≤2, and the range of f is {y|0≤y≤2}.
summary: Finalizing the domain and range for function b.

header: Understanding Domains and Ranges of Functions in Chapter 1
content: 1.11 Find the domain and range for the function f(x)=(5x+2)/(2x−1). The root functions f(x)= x 1/n have defining characteristics depending on whether n is odd or even. For all even integers n≥2, the domain of f(x)= x 1/n is the interval [0, ∞). For all odd integers n≥1, the domain of f(x)= x 1/n is the set of all real numbers. Since x 1/n = (−x) 1/n for odd integers n, f(x)= x 1/n is an odd function if n is odd.
description: Figure 1.21 illustrates the different domains for even and odd integers n. (a) If n is even, the domain of f(x)= n x is [0, ∞). (b) If n is odd, the domain of f(x)= n x is (−∞, ∞) and the function f(x)= n x is an odd function.
summary: This section discusses the domains and characteristics of root functions based on whether their exponent is odd or even.

header: Example Calculation of Function Domains in Chapter 1
content: Example 1.17 Finding Domains for Algebraic Functions For each of the following functions, determine the domain of the function. a. f(x)= 3 2 x −1 b. f(x)= 2x+5 2 3x +4 c. f(x)= 4−3x 3 d. f(x)= 2x−1 Solution a. You cannot divide by zero, so the domain is the set of values x such that x 2 −1≠0. Therefore, the domain is {x | x≠±1}. b. You need to determine the values of x for which the denominator is zero. Since 3x 2 +4≥4 for all real numbers x, the denominator is never zero. Therefore, the domain is (−∞, ∞). c. Since the square root of a negative number is not a real number, the domain is the set of values x for
summary: This example shows how to determine the domains for various algebraic functions.

content: which 4−3x≥0. Therefore, the domain is {x|x≤4/3}. d. The cube root is defined for all real numbers, so the domain is the interval (−∞, ∞). 1.12 Find the domain for each of the following functions: f(x)=(5−2x)/(x 2 +2) and g(x)= 5x−1.
summary: Discussion of domains for specific functions.

content: Transcendental Functions Thus far, we have discussed algebraic functions. Some functions, however, cannot be described by basic algebraic operations. These functions are known as transcendental functions because they are said to 'transcend,' or go beyond, algebra. The most common transcendental functions are trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions.
summary: Introduction to types of functions beyond basic algebraic operations.

content: A trigonometric function relates the ratios of two sides of a right triangle. They are sinx, cosx, tanx, cotx, secx, and cscx. (We discuss trigonometric functions later in the chapter.)
summary: Explanation of the basic trigonometric functions.

content: An exponential function is a function of the form f(x)=b^x, where the base b>0, b≠1. A logarithmic function is a function of the form f(x)=log_b(x) for some constant b>0, b≠1, where log_b(x)=y if and only if b^y = x. (We also discuss exponential and logarithmic functions later in the chapter.)
summary: Definition and structure of exponential and logarithmic functions.

content: Example 1.18 Classifying Algebraic and Transcendental Functions Classify each of the following functions, a. through c., as algebraic or transcendental. a. f(x)= x +1 b. f(x)=2^x c. f(x)=sin(2x)
summary: Introduction to classifying functions into algebraic and transcendental.

content: Solution a. Since this function involves basic algebraic operations only, it is an algebraic function. b. This function cannot be written as a formula that involves only basic algebraic operations, so it is transcendental. (Note that algebraic functions can only have powers that are rational numbers.) c. As in part b., this function cannot be written using a formula involving basic algebraic operations only; therefore, this function is transcendental.
summary: Explanation of whether specific functions are algebraic or transcendental.

content: 1.13 Is f(x)= x/2 an algebraic or a transcendental function?
summary: Question regarding the classification of a specific function.

content: Piecewise-Defined Functions Sometimes a function is defined by different formulas on different parts of its domain. A function with this property is known as a piecewise-defined function. The absolute value function is an example of a piecewise-defined function because
summary: Introduction to piecewise-defined functions.

header: Understanding Piecewise-Defined Functions and Their Graphs
content:
the formula changes with the sign of x:
⎧−x, x<0
f(x)=⎨ .
⎩ x, x≥0
Other piecewise-defined functions may be represented by completely different formulas, depending on the part of the domain in which a point falls. To graph a piecewise-defined function, we graph each part of the function in its respective domain, on the same coordinates system. If the formula for a function is different for x<a and x>a, we need to pay special attention to what happens at x=a when we graph the function. Sometimes the graph needs to include an open or closed circle to indicate the value of the function at x=a.
summary: Introduction to how piecewise-defined functions vary based on the domain of x.

content:
Example 1.19
Graphing a Piecewise-Defined Function
Sketch a graph of the following piecewise-defined function:
⎧x+3, x<1
f(x)=⎨ .
⎩(x−2)², x≥1
Solution
Graph the linear function y= x+3 on the interval (−∞, 1) and graph the quadratic function y=(x−2)² on the interval [1, ∞). Since the value of the function at x=1 is given by the formula f(x)=(x−2)², we see that f(1)=1. To indicate this on the graph, we draw a closed circle at the point (1, 1). The value of the function is given by f(x)= x+2 for all x<1, but not at x=1. To indicate this on the graph, we draw an open circle at (1, 4).
description: This example graphically represents how to depict a piecewise function with a linear and quadratic component, including the use of open and closed circles.
summary: A practical example demonstrating how to graph a piecewise-defined function.

content: Figure 1.22 This piecewise-defined function is linear for x<1 and quadratic for x≥1.
description: A visual representation showing the linear component of the function for values less than 1 and the quadratic component for values equal to or greater than 1.
summary: Visual aid illustrating the characteristics of the defined piecewise function.

The parking garage is open from 6 a.m. to 12 midnight.

content: 1.15 The cost of mailing a letter is a function of the weight of the letter. Suppose the cost of mailing a letter is 49¢ for the first ounce and 21¢ for each additional ounce. Write a piecewise-defined function describing the cost C as a function of the weight x for 0< x≤3, where C is measured in cents and x is measured in ounces.
summary: This section introduces a mailing cost function based on letter weight.

content: Transformations of Functions We have seen several cases in which we have added, subtracted, or multiplied constants to form variations of simple.
summary: This introduces the concept of transformations of functions.

content: In the previous example, for instance, we subtracted 2 from the argument of the function y= x 2 to get the function f(x)=(x−2)2. This subtraction represents a shift of the function y= x 2 two units to the right. A shift, horizontally or vertically, is a type of transformation of a function. Other transformations include horizontal and vertical scalings, and reflections about the axes.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of transforming functions through shifting.

content: A vertical shift of a function occurs if we add or subtract the same constant to each output y. For c>0, the graph of f(x)+c is a shift of the graph of f(x) up c units, whereas the graph of f(x)−c is a shift of the graph of f(x) down c units. For example, the graph of the function f(x)= x 3 +4 is the graph of y= x 3 shifted up 4 units; the graph of the function f(x)= x 3 −4 is the graph of y= x 3 shifted down 4 units.
description: This section describes the effect of vertical shifts on function graphs.
summary: Explains how adding or subtracting from function outputs results in vertical shifts.

description: Figure 1.23 (a) shows the graph of y= f(x)+c as a vertical shift up, while (b) illustrates the graph of y= f(x)−c as a vertical shift down.
summary: Figure 1.23 visually depicts the effects of vertical shifts on function graphs.

content: A horizontal shift of a function occurs if we add or subtract the same constant to each input x. For c>0, the graph of f(x+c) is a shift of the graph of f(x) to the left c units; the graph of f(x−c) is a shift of the graph of f(x) to the right c units. Why does the graph shift left when adding a constant and shift right when subtracting a constant? To answer this question, let’s look at an example.
summary: Discusses how horizontal shifts are conducted through alterations to the input of functions.

content: Consider the function f(x)=|x+3| and evaluate this function at x−3. Since f(x−3)=|x| and x−3< x, the graph of f(x)=|x+3| is the graph of y=|x| shifted left 3 units. Similarly, the graph of f(x)=|x−3| is the graph of y=|x| shifted right 3 units.
description: This example illustrates specific horizontal shifts using absolute functions.
summary: Uses the function f(x)=|x+3| to demonstrate shifts in the graph due to input changes.

description: Figure 1.24 illustrates the graphs of f(x)=|x+3| and f(x)=|x−3| along with their respective horizontal shifts.
summary: Figure 1.24 provides a visual representation of horizontal shifts in specific function examples.

content: Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs
summary: An introduction to the chapter focusing on functions and their graphical representations.

content: Figure 1.24 (a) For c>0, the graph of y= f(x+c) is a horizontal shift left c units of the graph of y= f(x). (b) For c>0, the graph of y= f(x−c) is a horizontal shift right c units of the graph of y= f(x).
description: Figure 1.24 illustrates how horizontal shifts occur in the graphs of functions when adjusted by a constant c.
summary: Explanation of horizontal shifts in function graphs.

content: A vertical scaling of a graph occurs if we multiply all outputs y of a function by the same positive constant. For c>0, the graph of the function cf(x) is the graph of f(x) scaled vertically by a factor of c. If c>1, the values of the outputs for the function cf(x) are larger than the values of the outputs for the function f(x); therefore, the graph has been stretched vertically. If 0<c<1, then the outputs of the function cf(x) are smaller, so the graph has been compressed. For example, the graph of the function f(x)=3x² is the graph of y=x² stretched vertically by a factor of 3, whereas the graph of f(x)=x²/3 is the graph of y=x² compressed vertically by a factor of 3 (Figure 1.25).
description: This section discusses vertical scaling of function graphs, illustrating how scaling factors affect their appearance.
summary: Description of vertical scaling effects on function graphs.

content: Figure 1.25 (a) If c>1, the graph of y=cf(x) is a vertical stretch of the graph of y= f(x). (b) If 0<c<1, the graph of y=cf(x) is a vertical compression of the graph of y= f(x).
description: Figure 1.25 shows how vertical stretches and compressions affect function graphs based on the constant factor c.
summary: Visual representation of vertical adjustments in function graphs.

content: The horizontal scaling of a function occurs if we multiply the inputs x by the same positive constant. For c>0, the graph of the function f(cx) is the graph of f(x) scaled horizontally by a factor of c. If c>1, the graph of f(cx) is the graph of f(x) compressed horizontally. If 0<c<1, the graph of f(cx) is the graph of f(x) stretched horizontally.
summary: Introduction to horizontal scaling and its effects on function graphs.

header: Transformations of Function Graphs: Horizontal Stretch and Compression
content: For example, consider the function f(x) = 2x and evaluate f at x/2. Since f(x/2) = x, the graph of f(x) = 2x is the graph of y = x compressed horizontally. The graph of y = x/2 is a horizontal stretch of the graph of y = x.
summary: Discusses the influence of multiplying the input or output of a function on its graph shape.

content: Figure 1.26 (a) If c > 1, the graph of y = f(cx) is a horizontal compression of the graph of y = f(x). (b) If 0 < c < 1, the graph of y = f(cx) is a horizontal stretch of the graph of y = f(x).
description: Figure 1.26 shows the transformations of function graphs under different conditions of the constant c, indicating how they affect horizontal compression and stretching.
summary: Provides a visual comparison of the effects of horizontal transformations on function graphs.

header: Effect of Negative Constants on Function Graphs
content: We have explored what happens to the graph of a function f when we multiply f by a constant c > 0 to get a new function cf(x). We have also discussed what happens to the graph of a function f when we multiply the independent variable x by c > 0 to get a new function f(cx). However, we have not addressed what happens to the graph of the function if the constant c is negative.
summary: Introduction to the concept of how negative constants affect function graphs.

header: Reflections in Function Graphs Due to Negative Multiplication
content: If we have a constant c < 0, we can write c as a positive number multiplied by -1; but, what kind of transformation do we get when we multiply the function or its argument by -1? When we multiply all the outputs by -1, we get a reflection about the x-axis. When we multiply all inputs by -1, we get a reflection about the y-axis.
summary: Describes the impact of multiplying by negative constants on the orientation of function graphs.

content: For example, the graph of f(x) = −(x³ + 1) is the graph of y = (x³ + 1) reflected about the x-axis. The graph of f(x) = (−x)³ + 1 is the graph of y = x³ + 1 reflected about the y-axis.
description: This content includes specific examples illustrating how negative multiplication results in reflections across the x-axis and y-axis.
summary: Provides specific function examples to illustrate reflections resulting from negative multiplications.

content: Figure 1.27 shows the graphs of f(x) = −(x³ + 1) and f(x) = (−x)³ + 1, highlighting their reflections about the x-axis and y-axis respectively.
description: Figure 1.27 visually represents the transformations and reflections that occur in the function graphs discussed prior.
summary: Visual representation of the reflected graphs based on the transformations explained.

content: Figure 1.27 (a) The graph of y=−f(x) is the graph of y= f(x) reflected about the x-axis. (b) The graph of y= f(−x) is the graph of y= f(x) reflected about the y-axis.
description: Figure 1.27 illustrates the reflection of graphs across the x-axis and y-axis.
summary: This section introduces the concept of graph reflections for functions.

content: If the graph of a function consists of more than one transformation of another graph, it is important to transform the graph in the correct order. Given a function f(x), the graph of the related function y=cf⎛a(x+b)⎞+d can be obtained from the graph of y= f(x) by performing the transformations in the following order.
summary: This portion explains the importance of the order in which transformations are applied to function graphs.

content: 1. Horizontal shift of the graph of y= f(x). If b>0, shift left. If b<0, shift right.
summary: This chunk describes the first transformation, which involves horizontal shifts.

content: 2. Horizontal scaling of the graph of y= f(x+b) by a factor of |a|. If a<0, reflect the graph about the y-axis.
summary: This section covers horizontal scaling and its effects on the graph.

content: 3. Vertical scaling of the graph of y= f(a(x+b)) by a factor of |c|. If c<0, reflect the graph about the x-axis.
summary: Here, vertical scaling is discussed, noting how it alters the graph.

content: 4. Vertical shift of the graph of y=cf(a(x+b)). If d >0, shift up. If d <0, shift down.
summary: This chunk describes the final transformation involving vertical shifts.

content: We can summarize the different transformations and their related effects on the graph of a function in the following table.
description: This section introduces a table that summarizes various transformations and their effects on function graphs.
summary: A table will help consolidate the understanding of transformations and their effects.

content:
Transformation of f(c>0) Effect on the graph of f
f(x)+c Vertical shift up c units
f(x)−c Vertical shift down c units
f(x+c) Shift left by c units
f(x−c) Shift right by c units
Vertical stretch if c>1;
cf(x)
vertical compression if 0<c<1
f(cx) Horizontal stretch if 0<c<1; horizontal compression if c>1
−f(x) Reflection about the x-axis
f(−x) Reflection about the y-axis
description: Table summarizing transformations of functions.
summary: This section outlines the various transformations of functions including vertical and horizontal shifts, stretches, and reflections.

header: Understanding Depreciation of Equipment - Functions and Graphs
content: A company purchases some computer equipment for $20,500. At the end of a 3-year period, the value of the equipment has decreased linearly to $12,300. a. What is the value of the equipment at the end of 5 years? d. When will the value of the equipment be $3000?

Interpret the slope of the graph of S. c. Use part a to predict the year when online shopping during Christmas will reach $60 billion.

content: Trigonometric functions are used to model many phenomena, including sound waves, vibrations of strings, alternating electrical current, and the motion of pendulums. In fact, almost any repetitive, or cyclical, motion can be modeled by some combination of trigonometric functions. In this section, we define the six basic trigonometric functions and look at some of the main identities involving these functions.
summary: An introduction to trigonometric functions and their applications.

content: To use trigonometric functions, we first must understand how to measure angles. Although we can use both radians and degrees, radians are a more natural measurement because they are related directly to the unit circle, a circle with radius 1.
summary: Explaining the necessity of radians in trigonometry.

content: The radian measure of an angle is defined as follows. Given an angle θ, let s be the length of the corresponding arc on the unit circle (Figure 1.30). We say the angle corresponding to the arc of length 1 has radian measure 1.
description: Figure 1.30 illustrates that the radian measure of an angle θ is the arc length s of the associated arc on the unit circle.
summary: Defining how radian measure correlates with arc length on the unit circle.

content: Since an angle of 360° corresponds to the circumference of a circle, or an arc of length 2π, we conclude that an angle with a degree measure of 360° has a radian measure of 2π. Similarly, we see that 180° is equivalent to π radians.
description: Table 1.8 illustrates the relationship between common degree and radian values.
summary: Explaining the conversion between degrees and radians.

header: Introduction to the Six Basic Trigonometric Functions
content:
The Six Basic Trigonometric Functions
Trigonometric functions allow us to use angle measures, in radians or degrees, to find the coordinates of a point on any circle—not only on a unit circle—or to find an angle given a point on a circle. They also define the relationship among the sides and angles of a triangle.
summary: This section introduces trigonometric functions and their applications in geometry.

header: Defining Trigonometric Functions Using the Unit Circle
content: To define the trigonometric functions, first consider the unit circle centered at the origin and a point P=(x, y) on the unit circle. Let θ be an angle with an initial side that lies along the positive x-axis and with a terminal side that is the line segment OP. An angle in this position is said to be in standard position (Figure 1.31). We can then define the values of the six trigonometric functions for θ in terms of the coordinates x and y.
description: Illustration of the unit circle and standard position of angle θ.
summary: This section explains how trigonometric functions are defined in relation to the unit circle.

header: Introduction to Trigonometric Functions and their Definitions
content:
Let P=(x, y) be a point on the unit circle centered at the origin O. Let θ be an angle with an initial side along the positive x-axis and a terminal side given by the line segment OP. The trigonometric functions are then defined as:

1. sinθ = y,  cscθ = 1/y
2. cosθ = x,  secθ = 1/x
3. tanθ = y/x,  cotθ = x/y

If x=0, secθ and tanθ are undefined. If y=0, then cotθ and cscθ are undefined.
summary: This chunk introduces the definitions of trigonometric functions based on the coordinates of a point on the unit circle.

content:
We can see that for a point P=(x, y) on a circle of radius r with a corresponding angle θ, the coordinates x and y satisfy:

- cosθ = x/r
- x = r cosθ
- sinθ = y/r
- y = r sinθ.
description: Figure 1.32: Visual representation illustrating the relationships between the angle θ, the coordinates x and y, and the radius r, helping to establish the definitions of the trigonometric functions.
summary: This chunk describes the geometric relationships between the coordinates of points on a circle and trigonometric functions.

content: For a point P=(x, y) on a circle of radius r, the coordinates x and y satisfy x=rcosθ and y=rsinθ.
summary: This section explains the relationship between the coordinates of a point on a circle and trigonometric functions.

content: Table 1.9 shows the values of sine and cosine at the major angles in the first quadrant. From this table, we can determine the values of sine and cosine at the corresponding angles in the other quadrants. The values of the other trigonometric functions are calculated easily from the values of sinθ and cosθ.

header: Trigonometric Functions and Right Triangle Relationships
content: Evaluate cos(3π/4) and sin(−π/6). As mentioned earlier, the ratios of the side lengths of a right triangle can be expressed in terms of the trigonometric functions evaluated at either of the acute angles of the triangle. Let θ be one of the acute angles. Let A be the length of the adjacent leg, O be the length of the opposite leg, and H be the length of the hypotenuse. By inscribing the triangle into a circle of radius H, as shown in Figure 1.33, we see that A, H, and O satisfy the following relationships with θ: O/H, sinθ = cscθ = H/O, A/H, cosθ = secθ = H/A, O/A, tanθ = cotθ = A/O.
description: Figure 1.33 illustrates the relationships between the side lengths of a right triangle inscribed in a circle.
summary: This section discusses the relationships between side lengths of a right triangle and trigonometric functions.

content: Example 1.24 Constructing a Wooden Ramp A wooden ramp is to be built with one end on the ground and the other end at the top of a short staircase. If the top of the staircase is 4 ft from the ground and the angle between the ground and the ramp is to be 10°.
summary: This example applies trigonometric concepts to a practical scenario involving the construction of a ramp.

content: long does the ramp need to be? Solution Let x denote the length of the ramp. In the following image, we see that x needs to satisfy the equation sin(10°)=4/x. Solving this equation for x, we see that x=4/sin(10°)≈23.035 ft.
description: An equation illustrating how to determine the length of the ramp based on a right triangle formed by the ramp, the height, and the angle.
summary: This section details the ramp length calculation using trigonometric functions.

content: 1.19 A housepainter wants to lean a 20-ft ladder against a house. If the angle between the base of the ladder and the ground is to be 60°, how far from the house should she place the base of the ladder?
summary: This section presents a problem involving the placement of a ladder, requiring trigonometric methods for solving the distance from the house.

content: Trigonometric Identities A trigonometric identity is an equation involving trigonometric functions that is true for all angles θ for which the functions are defined. We can use the identities to help us solve or simplify equations. The main trigonometric identities are listed next.
summary: This section defines trigonometric identities and their importance in solving equations.

content: Reciprocal identities sinθ cosθ tanθ= cotθ= cosθ sinθ 1 1 cscθ= secθ= sinθ cosθ
summary: This section enumerates the reciprocal identities used in trigonometry.

content: Pythagorean identities 2 2 2 2 2 2 sin θ+cos θ=1 1+tan θ=sec θ 1+cot θ=csc θ
summary: This section presents the essential Pythagorean identities in trigonometric functions.

content: Addition and subtraction formulas sin(α±β)=sinαcosβ±cosαsinβ cos(α±β)=cosαcosβ∓sinαsinβ
summary: This section details the addition and subtraction formulas for sine and cosine functions.

content: Double-angle formulas sin(2θ)=2sinθcosθ cos(2θ)=2cos²θ−1=1−2sin²θ=cos²θ−sin²θ
summary: This section introduces the double-angle formulas for sine and cosine functions.

content: Example 1.25 Solving Trigonometric Equations For each of the following equations, use a trigonometric identity to find all solutions. a. 1+cos(2θ)=cosθ b. sin(2θ)=tanθ
summary: This section provides examples of solving trigonometric equations using identities.

content: a. Using the double-angle formula for cos(2θ), we see that θ is a solution of 1+cos(2θ)=cosθ if and only if 2(1+2cos θ−1)=cosθ, which is true if and only if 2(2cos θ−cosθ)=0. To solve this equation, it is important to note that we need to factor the left-hand side and not divide both sides of the equation by cosθ. The problem with dividing by cosθ is that it is possible that cosθ is zero. In fact, if we did divide both sides of the equation by cosθ, we would miss some of the solutions of the original equation. Factoring the left-hand side of the equation, we see that θ is a solution of this equation if and only if cosθ(2cosθ−1)=0.
summary: This chunk explains the application of double-angle formula for cosine and emphasizes the importance of factoring.

header: Finding Solutions for the Cosine Equation
content: Since cosθ=0 when θ=π/2, ±π/2, ±2π,…, and cosθ=1/2 when θ=π/3, ±2π,… or θ=−π/3, −π/3 ± 2nπ,…, we conclude that the set of solutions to this equation is θ=π/2 + nπ, θ=π/3 + 2nπ, and θ=−π/3 + 2nπ, n=0, ±1, ±2,….
summary: This chunk details the specific angle solutions derived from the cosine equation.

header: Transforming the Equation Using Sine and Tangent
content: b. Using the double-angle formula for sin(2θ) and the reciprocal identity for tan(θ), the equation can be written as sinθ/(cosθ) = 2sinθcosθ. To solve this equation, we multiply both sides by cosθ to eliminate the denominator, and say that if θ satisfies this equation, then θ satisfies the equation 2sinθcos θ−sinθ=0.
summary: This chunk focuses on transforming the original equation using sine and tangent identities.

content: However, we need to be a little careful here. Even if θ satisfies this new equation, it may not satisfy the original equation because, to satisfy the original equation, we would need to be able to divide both sides of the equation by cosθ. However, if cosθ=0, we cannot divide both sides of the equation by cosθ. Therefore, it is possible that we may arrive at extraneous solutions. So, at the end, it is important to check for extraneous solutions.
summary: This chunk advises careful consideration of potential extraneous solutions when manipulating trigonometric equations.

header: Factoring to Solve the Sine Equation
content: Returning to the equation, it is important that we factor sinθ out of both terms on the left-hand side instead of dividing both sides of the equation by sinθ. Factoring the left-hand side of the equation, we can rewrite this equation as 2sinθ(2cos θ−1)=0.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the significance of factoring in solving the sine equation.

content: Therefore, the solutions are given by the angles θ such that sinθ=0 or cos 2 θ=1/2. The solutions of the first equation are θ=0, ±π, ±2π,…. The solutions of the second equation are θ=π/4, (π/4)±(π/2), (π/4)±π,…. After checking for extraneous solutions, the set of solutions to the equation is θ=nπ and θ=π/4+nπ/2, n=0, ±1, ±2,….
summary: This section discusses solutions for specific trigonometric equations.

content: 1.20 Find all solutions to the equation cos(2θ)=sinθ.
summary: This section introduces the task of finding solutions for a trigonometric identity.

content: Prove the trigonometric identity 1+tan 2 θ=sec 2 θ. Solution We start with the identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1. Dividing both sides of this equation by cos²θ, we obtain sin²θ/cos²θ + 1 = 1/cos²θ. Since sinθ/cosθ=tanθ and 1/cosθ=secθ, we conclude that tan²θ + 1 = sec²θ.
summary: This section contains an example of proving a trigonometric identity.

content: 1.21 Prove the trigonometric identity 1+cot 2 θ=csc 2 θ.
summary: This section presents another task of proving a different trigonometric identity.

content: Graphs and Periods of the Trigonometric Functions We have seen that as we travel around the unit circle, the values of the trigonometric functions repeat. We can see this pattern in the graphs of the functions. Let P=(x, y) be a point on the unit circle and let θ be the corresponding angle. Since the angle θ and θ+2π correspond to the same point P, the values of the trigonometric functions at θ and at θ+2π are the same.
summary: This section discusses the periodic nature of trigonometric functions as derived from their circular motion.

content: Consequently, the trigonometric functions are periodic functions. The period of a function f is defined to be the smallest positive value p such that f(x+p)= f(x) for all values x in the domain of f. The sine, cosine, secant, and cosecant functions have a period of 2π. Since the tangent and cotangent functions repeat on an interval of length π, their period is π (Figure 1.34).
description: Figure 1.34 illustrates the periodicity of the tangent and cotangent functions.
summary: This section defines and explains the concept of periodicity in trigonometric functions.

header: Understanding the Transformation of Trigonometric Functions
content: Just as with algebraic functions, we can apply transformations to trigonometric functions. In particular, consider the following function: f(x)= Asin⎛B(x−α)⎞+C. (1.10)
summary: This chunk discusses the transformations applicable to trigonometric functions.

header: Key Characteristics of the Transformed Sine Function
content: In Figure 1.35, the constant α causes a horizontal or phase shift. The factor B changes the period. This transformed sine function will have a period 2π/|B|. The factor A results in a vertical stretch by a factor of |A|. We say |A| is the 'amplitude of f.' The constant C causes a vertical shift.
description: Figure 1.35 illustrates a graph of a general sine function demonstrating the various transformations.
summary: Explanation of how different constants affect the sine function transformations.

header: Relationship Between Sine and Cosine Graphs
content: Notice in Figure 1.34 that the graph of y=cosx is the graph of y=sinx shifted to the left π/2 units.
description: Figure 1.34 shows the periodic nature of the six trigonometric functions.
summary: This section highlights the relationship between sine and cosine graphs through horizontal shifts.

header: Understanding Sine and Cosine Relationships in Functions and Graphs
content: can write cos x = sin(x + π/2). Similarly, we can view the graph of y = sin x as the graph of y = cos x shifted right π/2 units, and state that sin x = cos(x - π/2).
summary: Explains the relationship between sine and cosine functions.

header: Modeling Daylight Hours with a Shifted Sine Curve
content: A shifted sine curve arises naturally when graphing the number of hours of daylight in a given location as a function of the day of the year. For example, suppose a city reports that June 21 is the longest day of the year with 15.7 hours and December 21 is the shortest day of the year with 8.3 hours. It can be shown that the function h(t) = 3.7sin(2π/365(t - 80.5)) + 12 is a model for the number of hours of daylight h as a function of day of the year t.
description: Figure 1.36 shows the relationship between hours of daylight and the day of the year modeled by a shifted sine curve.
summary: Demonstrates how a sine curve represents the daylight hours throughout the year.

header: Example of Graphing a Transformed Sine Curve
content: Example 1.27 Sketching the Graph of a Transformed Sine Curve Sketch a graph of f(x) = 3sin(2(x - π/4)) + 1.
summary: Introduces an example involving the transformation of a sine curve.

header: Solution to the Sine Curve Graphing Example
content: This graph is a phase shift of y = sin(x) to the right by π/4 units, followed by a horizontal compression by a factor of 2, a vertical stretch by a factor of 3, and then a vertical shift by 1 unit. The period of f is π.
