content:
1.4 | Inverse Functions
Learning Objectives
1.4.1 Determine the conditions for when a function has an inverse.
1.4.2 Use the horizontal line test to recognize when a function is one-to-one.
1.4.3 Find the inverse of a given function.
1.4.4 Draw the graph of an inverse function.
1.4.5 Evaluate inverse trigonometric functions.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to inverse functions.

content: An inverse function reverses the operation done by a particular function. In other words, whatever a function does, the inverse function undoes it. In this section, we define an inverse function formally and state the necessary conditions for an inverse function to exist. We examine how to find an inverse function and study the relationship between the graph of a function and the graph of its inverse. Then we apply these ideas to define and discuss properties of the inverse trigonometric functions.
summary: This chunk explains the concept and definition of inverse functions and their properties.

content:
Existence of an Inverse Function
We begin with an example. Given a function f and an output y= f(x), we are often interested in finding what value or values x were mapped to y by f. For example, consider the function f(x)= x^3 +4. Since any output y= x^3 +4, we can solve this equation for x to find that the input is x= 3√(y−4). This equation defines x as a function of y. Denoting this function as f −1, and writing x= f −1(y)= 3√(y−4), we see that for any x in the domain of f, f −1(f(x))= f −1(x^3 +4) = x. Thus, this new function, f −1, 'undid' what the original function f did. A function with this property is called the inverse function of the original function.
summary: This chunk provides an example illustrating how to find an inverse function.

content:
Definition
Given a function f with domain D and range R, its inverse function (if it exists) is the function f −1 with domain R and range D such that f −1(y)= x if f(x)=y. In other words, for a function f and its inverse f −1, f −1(f(x))= x for all x in D, and f(f −1(y))= y for all y in R.
summary: This section provides a formal definition of inverse functions involving their domains and ranges.

content: Note that f −1 is read as 'f inverse.' Here, the −1 is not used as an exponent and f −1(x)≠1/f(x).
summary: This chunk clarifies the notation used for inverse functions.

content: Figure 1.37 shows the relationship between the domain and range of f and the domain and range of f −1.
description: Figure 1.37 illustrates the correspondence between the output of function f and the input of its inverse f −1, emphasizing how the range of f becomes the domain of f −1.
summary: This figure visualizes the relationship between a function and its inverse.

content: Recall that a function has exactly one output for each input. Therefore, to define an inverse function, we need to map each output back to one and only one input.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the unique mapping required for a function to have an inverse.

header: Understanding Inverse Functions and One-to-One Functions
content: inputtoexactlyoneoutput.Forexample,let’strytofindtheinversefunctionfor f(x)= x 2 . Solvingtheequation y= x 2 for x, wearriveattheequation x= ± y. Thisequationdoesnotdescribe x asafunctionof y becausetherearetwo solutions to this equation for every y>0. The problem with trying to find an inverse function for f(x)= x 2 is that two inputs are sent to the same output for each output y>0. The function f(x)= x 3 +4 discussed earlier did not have this problem. For that function, each input was sent to a different output.
summary: This section explains the concept of inverse functions and introduces one-to-one functions, highlighting the uniqueness of outputs.

content: A function thtat sends each input to a different output is called a one-to-one function. We say f is a one-to-one function if f(x1) ≠ f(x2) when x1 ≠ x2.
summary: This definition establishes the criteria for identifying one-to-one functions based on unique output values.

header: Using Graphs to Determine One-to-One Functions
content: One way to determine whether a function is one-to-one is by looking at its graph. If a function is one-to-one, then no two inputs can be sent to the same output. Therefore, if we draw a horizontal line anywhere in the xy-plane, according to the horizontal line test, it cannot intersect the graph more than once. We note that the horizontal line test is different from the vertical line test. The vertical line test determines whether a graph is the graph of a function. The horizontal line test determines whether a function is one-to-one.
summary: This section describes how to use graphs, specifically the horizontal line test, to verify if a function is one-to-one.

header: Rule for the Horizontal Line Test
content: Rule: Horizontal Line Test A function f is one-to-one if and only if every horizontal line intersects the graph of f no more than once.
summary: This rule succinctly outlines the criteria for determining one-to-one functions via the horizontal line test.

content: (Figure 1.38) (a) The function f(x)= x 2 is not one-to-one because it fails the horizontal line test. (b) The function f(x)= x 3 is one-to-one because it passes the horizontal line test.
description: Figure 1.38 illustrates examples of functions failing and passing the horizontal line test to demonstrate one-to-one characteristics.
summary: This figure visually distinguishes between one-to-one and non one-to-one functions through examples.

content: Example 1.28 Determining Whether a Function Is One-to-One For each of the following functions, use the horizontal line test to determine whether it is one-to-one.
summary: This example prompts the user to apply the horizontal line test to various functions to assess their one-to-one status.

header: Understanding Inverse Functions | Functions and Graphs
content: We can now consider one-to-one functions and show how to find their inverses. Recall that a function maps elements in the domain of f to elements in the range of f. The inverse function maps each element from the range of f back to its corresponding element from the domain of f. Therefore, to find the inverse function of a one-to-one function f, given any y in the range of f, we need to determine which x in the domain of f satisfies f(x)=y. Since f is one-to-one, there is exactly one such value x. We can find that value x by solving the equation f(x)=y for x. Doing so, we are able to write x as a function of y where the domain of this function is the range of f and the range of this new function is the domain of f. Consequently, this function is the inverse of f, and we write x= f −1 (y). Since we typically use the variable x to denote the independent variable and y to denote the dependent variable, we often interchange the roles of x and y, and write y= f −1 (x). Representing the inverse function in this way is also helpful later when we graph a function f and its inverse f −1 on the same axes.
summary: Introduction to one-to-one functions and their inverses.

header: Problem-Solving Strategy for Inverses | Functions and Graphs
content: Problem-Solving Strategy: Finding an Inverse Function 1. Solve the equation y= f(x) for x. 2. Interchange the variables x and y and write y= f −1 (x).
summary: Steps to find the inverse function.

header: Example of Finding an Inverse Function | Functions and Graphs
content: Example 1.29 Finding an Inverse Function Find the inverse for the function f(x)=3x−4. State the domain and range of the inverse function. Verify that f −1 (f(x))= x. Solution Follow the steps outlined in the strategy. Step 1. If y=3x−4, then 3x=y+4 and x= (y+4)/3. Step 2. Rewrite as y= (1/3)x+ (4/3) and let y= f −1(x). Therefore, f −1(x)= (1/3)x+ (4/3). Since the domain of f is (−∞, ∞), the range of f −1 is (−∞, ∞). Since the range of f is (−∞, ∞), the domain of f −1 is (−∞, ∞). You can verify that f −1 (f(x))= x by writing f −1 (f(x))= f (3x−4)= (3x−4)+(4/3) = x. Notethatfor f −1 (x) to be the inverse of f(x), both f −1 (f(x))= x and f(f −1 (x))= x for all x in the domain of the inside function.
summary: A detailed example illustrating how to find an inverse function.

content: 1.24 Find the inverse of the function f(x)=3x/(x−2). State the domain and range of the inverse function.
summary: This section introduces the concept of finding the inverse of a specific function.

header: Graphing Relationships Between Functions and Their Inverses
content:
Graphing Inverse Functions
Let’s consider the relationship between the graph of a function f and the graph of its inverse. Consider the graph of f shown in Figure 1.39 and a point (a, b) on the graph. Since b= f(a), then f −1(b)=a. Therefore, when we graph f −1, the point (b, a) is on the graph. As a result, the graph of f −1 is a reflection of the graph of f about the line y= x.
description: Figure 1.39 illustrates the relationship between a function f and its inverse f −1, showcasing how the points reflect across the line y = x.
summary: This section explains how the graphs of a function and its inverse are related, specifically through reflection.

content:
Example 1.30
Sketching Graphs of Inverse Functions

content: Reflect the graph about the line y = x. The domain of f −1 is [0, ∞). The range of f −1 is [−2, ∞). By using the preceding strategy for finding inverse functions, we can verify that the inverse function is f −1(x) = x^2 − 2, as shown in the graph.
summary: Introduction to the concept of reflecting graphs and finding inverse functions.

content: 1.25 Sketch the graph of f(x) = 2x + 3 and the graph of its inverse using the symmetry property of inverse functions.

content: As we have seen, f(x) = x^2 does not have an inverse function because it is not one-to-one. However, we can choose a subset of the domain of f such that the function is one-to-one. This subset is called a restricted domain.
summary: Explains the importance of restricting domains to make functions one-to-one for inverses.

header: Defining New Functions on Restricted Domains
content: By restricting the domain of f, we can define a new function g such that the domain of g is the restricted domain of f and g(x) = f(x) for all x in the domain of g. Then we can define an inverse function for g on that domain.
summary: Details on creating new functions based on restricted domains.

content: For example, since f(x) = x^2 is one-to-one on the interval [0, ∞), we can define a new function g such that the domain of g is [0, ∞) and g(x) = x^2 for all x in its domain. Since g is a one-to-one function, it has an inverse function, given by the formula g −1(x) = x.
summary: An example illustrating the use of restricted domains to define a new function and its inverse.

content: On the other hand, the function f(x) = x^2 is also one-to-one on the domain (−∞, 0]. Therefore, we could also define a new function h such that the domain of h is (−∞, 0] and h(x) = x^2 for all x in the domain of h. Then h is a one-to-one function and must also have an inverse. Its inverse is given by the formula h −1(x) = −x (Figure 1.40).
description: Figure 1.40 illustrates the function h(x) = x^2 for x in the domain (−∞, 0] and its inverse.
summary: Another example showing how to define a new one-to-one function with a negative domain and its inverse.

header: Functions and Graphs: Understanding Inverse Functions
content: The domain and range of f −1 are given by the range and domain of f, respectively. Therefore, the domain of f −1 is [0, ∞) and the range of f −1 is [−1, ∞). To find a formula for f −1 , solve the equation y=(x+1)2 for x. If y=(x+1)2 , then x=−1± y. Since we are restricting the domain to the interval where x≥−1, we need ± y≥0. Therefore, x=−1+ y. Interchanging x and y, we write y=−1+ x and conclude that f −1(x)=−1+ x.
summary: This section covers the basics of determining the domain and range of inverse functions.

header: Inverse Trigonometric Functions: Definitions and Domains
content: The six basic trigonometric functions are periodic, and therefore they are not one-to-one. However, if we restrict the domain of a trigonometric function to an interval where it is one-to-one, we can define its inverse. Consider the sine function (Figure 1.34). The sine function is one-to-one on an infinite number of intervals, but the standard convention is to restrict the domain to the interval ⎡ − π , π⎤. By doing so, we define the inverse sine function on the domain [−1, 1] such that for any x in the interval [−1, 1], the inverse sine function tells us which angle θ in the interval ⎡ − π , π⎤ satisfies sinθ= x. Similarly, we can restrict the domains of the other trigonometric functions to define inverse trigonometric functions, which are functions that tell us which angle in a certain interval has a specified trigonometric value.
description: Figure 1.34 illustrates the sine function and its restricted domain to define the inverse function.
summary: This section explains the need to restrict trigonometric functions to define their inverses clearly.

header: Formal Definitions of Inverse Trigonometric Functions
content: Definition The inverse sine function, denoted sin −1 or arcsin, and the inverse cosine function, denoted cos −1 or arccos, are defined on the domain D={x|−1≤ x≤1} as follows:
summary: This definition formalizes the inverse functions for sine and cosine.

content: sin −1(x)=y if and only if sin(y)= x and − π ≤ y ≤ π ; (1.12)
summary: This section introduces the definition of the inverse sine function.

content: cos −1(x)=y if and only if cos(y)= x and 0 ≤ y ≤ π.
summary: The definition of the inverse cosine function is presented following the sine function.

content: The inverse tangent function, denoted tan −1 or arctan, and inverse cotangent function, denoted cot −1 or arccot, are defined on the domain D={x|−∞< x<∞} as follows: tan −1(x)=y if and only if tan(y)= x and − π <y< π ; (1.13) cot −1(x)=y if and only if cot(y)= x and 0<y<π.
summary: This segment outlines the definitions and domains of the inverse tangent and cotangent functions.

header: Defining Inverse Cosecant and Secant Functions
content: The inverse cosecant function, denoted csc −1 or arccsc, and inverse secant function, denoted sec −1 or arcsec, are defined on the domain D={x||x|≥1} as follows: csc −1(x)=y if and only if csc(y)= x and − π ≤ y ≤ π , y≠0; (1.14) sec −1(x)=y if and only if sec(y)= x and 0≤y≤π, y≠π/2.
summary: This section defines the inverse cosecant and secant functions along with their respective domains.

content: To graph the inverse trigonometric functions, we use the graphs of the trigonometric functions restricted to the domains defined earlier and reflect the graphs about the line y= x.

header: Composition of Trigonometric Functions and Inverses Example
content: We now consider a composition of a trigonometric function and its inverse. For example, consider the two expressions sin⎛sin −1⎛ 2⎞⎞and sin −1 (sin(π)). For the first one, we simplify as follows:⎝ ⎝ 2⎠⎠⎛ −1 ⎛ 2⎞⎞ ⎛π⎞ 2sin sin =sin = .⎝ ⎝2 ⎠⎠ ⎝4⎠ 2. For the second one, we have sin−1⎛sin(π)⎞=sin −1(0)=0.⎝ ⎠

header: Understanding the Inverse Property of Sine Function
content: Theinversefunctionissupposedto“undo”theoriginalfunction,sowhyisn’t sin −1⎛sin(π)⎞=π? Recallingourdefinitionofinversefunctions,afunction f anditsinverse f −1 satisfytheconditions f ⎛ f −1(y) ⎞ =y forall y inthedomainoff −1 and f −1⎛f(x)⎞= x forall x inthedomainoff, sowhathappenedhere? The issueisthattheinversesinefunction,sin −1 , is the inverse of the restricted sine function defined on the domain ⎡ − π , π⎤ . Therefore, for x in the interval⎣ 2 2⎦⎡ − π , π⎤ , itistruethat sin −1(sinx)= x. content: In many areas of science, engineering, and mathematics, it is useful to know the maximum value a function can obtain, even if we don’t know its exact value at a given instant. For instance, if we have a function describing the strength of a roof beam, we would want to know the maximum weight the beam can support without breaking. If we have a function that describes the speed of a train, we would want to know its maximum speed before it jumps off the rails. Safe design often depends on knowing maximum values.
summary: This section highlights the importance of understanding maximum values in various fields.

header: Learning Objectives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
content:
1.5.1 Identify the form of an exponential function.

1.5.3 Recognize the significance of the number e.

summary: This section outlines the key learning objectives related to exponential and logarithmic functions.

header: Overview of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
content: In this section we examine exponential and logarithmic functions. We use the properties of these functions to solve equations involving exponential or logarithmic terms, and we study the meaning and importance of the number e. We also define hyperbolic and inverse hyperbolic functions, which involve combinations of exponential and logarithmic functions. (Note that we present alternative definitions of exponential and logarithmic functions in the chapter Applications of Integrations, and prove that the functions have the same properties with either definition.)
summary: This section provides an overview of exponential and logarithmic functions, highlighting their applications and definitions.

header: Population Growth as an Example of Exponential Functions
content: Exponential functions arise in many applications. One common example is population growth. For example, if a population starts with P individuals and then grows at an annual rate of 2%, its population after 1 year is P(1)=P +0.02P =P (1+0.02)=P (1.02).
summary: Population growth illustrates the application of exponential functions.

content: Its population after 2 years is P(2)=P(1)+0.02P(1)=P(1)(1.02)=P(1.02). In general, its population after t years is P(t)=P(1.02)^t, which is an exponential function.
summary: Continued growth of population over multiple years exemplifies a general exponential function.

content: More generally, any function of the form f(x)=b^x, where b>0, b≠1, is an exponential function with base b and exponent x. Exponential functions have constant bases and variable exponents. Note that a function of the form f(x)=x^b for some constant b is not an exponential function but a power function.
summary: This section defines the general form of exponential functions and distinguishes them from power functions.

header: Comparison of Exponential and Power Functions
content: To see the difference between an exponential function and a power function, we compare the functions y=x^2 and y=2^x. In Table 1.10, we see that both 2^x and x^2 approach infinity as x→∞. Eventually, however, 2^x becomes larger than x^2 and grows more rapidly as x→∞. In the opposite direction, as x→−∞, x^2→∞, whereas 2^x→0. The line y=0 is a horizontal asymptote for y=2^x.
description: Table 1.10 displays the values of x^2 and 2^x for different values of x to illustrate their growth comparison.
summary: This section compares the growth rates of exponential and power functions using specific examples.

header: Growth Values of Exponential and Power Functions
description: This table presents the values of x^2 and 2^x for various values of x, illustrating their growth behavior.
summary: This table provides specific values for x^2 and 2^x to support the comparison of their growth.

header: Analysis of Exponential Growth and Quadratic Growth in Graphs
content: In Figure 1.43, we graph both y= x 2 and y=2 x to show how the graphs differ. Figure 1.43 Both 2 x and x 2 approach infinity as x→∞, but 2 x grows more rapidly than x 2 . As x→−∞, x 2 →∞, whereas 2 x →0.
description: Figure 1.43 illustrates the graphs of the functions y = x^2 and y = 2^x, highlighting their growth rates as x approaches both positive and negative infinity.
summary: This chunk discusses the differences in growth rates between quadratic and exponential functions.

content: Evaluating Exponential Functions Recall the properties of exponents: If x is a positive integer, then we define b x =b·b⋯b (with x factors of b). If x is a negative integer, then x=−y for some positive integer y, and we define b x =b −y =1/b y . Also, b 0 is defined to be 1. If x is a rational number, then x= p/q, where p and q are integers and b x =b p/q = q b p .
summary: This chunk outlines the rules and definitions associated with exponents and their evaluation for various types of numbers.

header: Defining Exponential Values for Irrational Numbers
content: For example, 9 3/2 = 9 3 =27. However, how is b x defined if x is an irrational number? For example, what do we mean by 2 2 ? This is too complex a question for us to answer fully right now; however, we can make an approximation.
summary: This chunk raises the question of defining exponentials for irrational numbers and discusses the complexity involved.

header: Approaching Exponential Values Through Rational Numbers
content: In Table 1.11, we list some rational numbers approaching 2, and the values of 2 x for each rational number x are presented as well. We claim that if we choose rational numbers x getting closer and closer to 2, the values of 2 x get closer and closer to some number L. We define that number L to be 2 2.

header: Understanding Bacterial Growth Through Exponential Functions
content: Example 1.33 Bacterial Growth Suppose a particular population of bacteria is known to double in size every 4 hours. If a culture starts with 1000 bacteria, then the number of bacteria after 4 hours is n(4)=1000·2. The number of bacteria after 8 hours is n(8)=n(4)·2=1000·2 2 . In general, the number of bacteria after 4m hours is n(4m)=1000·2 m.
summary: This chunk provides an example of how bacterial populations can be modeled using exponential growth functions.

header: Introduction to Bacterial Growth Functions in Chapter 1
content: t=4m, we see that the number of bacteria after t hours is n(t)=1000·2 t/4 . Find the number of bacteria after 6 hours, 10 hours, and 24 hours.
summary: This section introduces a function to calculate bacterial growth over time.

content: Solution The number of bacteria after 6 hours is given by n(6)=1000·2 6/4 ≈2828 bacteria. The number of bacteria after 10 hours is given by n(10)=1000·2 10/4 ≈5657 bacteria. The number of bacteria after 24 hours is given by n(24)=1000·2 6 =64,000 bacteria.
summary: This section provides the calculated bacterial counts after specific hours.

summary: This section outlines the definition and characteristics of exponential functions.

header: Behavior of Increasing and Decreasing Exponential Functions
content: To graph b x , we note that for b>1, b x is increasing on (−∞, ∞) and b x →∞ as x→∞, whereas b x →0 as x→−∞. On the other hand, if 0<b<1, f(x)=b x is decreasing on (−∞, ∞) and b x →0 as x→∞ whereas b x →∞ as x→−∞ (Figure 1.44).
description: Figure 1.44 illustrates the behavior of exponential functions based on the value of b.
summary: This section describes how the behavior of the graph of an exponential function depends on the base.

header: Introduction to the Number e and Real-World Applications
content:
The Number e
Aspecialtypeofexponentialfunctionappearsfrequentlyinreal-worldapplications.Todescribeit,considerthefollowing
exampleofexponentialgrowth,whicharisesfromcompoundinginterestinasavingsaccount.Supposeapersoninvests P
dollars in a savings account with an annual interest rate r, compounded annually. The amount of money after 1 year is
A(1)=P+rP=P(1+r).

The amount of money after 2 years is
2
A(2)= A(1)+rA(1)=P(1+r)+rP(1+r)=P(1+r) .

More generally, the amount after t years is
t
A(t)=P(1+r) .
summary: This segment introduces the number e and its application in exponential growth scenarios, particularly with compounding interest.

header: Understanding Compound Interest Calculations - Functions and Graphs
content: If the money is compounded 2 times per year, the amount of money after half a year is A = P + P = P(1 + r/2). The amount of money after 1 year is A(1) = A + A = P(1 + r/2) + P(1 + r/2) = P(1 + r). After t years, the amount of money in the account is A(t) = P(1 + r/2)^{2t}.
summary: This section introduces the formulae for calculating compound interest over different periods.

header: General Formula for Compound Interest - Functions and Graphs
content: More generally, if the money is compounded n times per year, the amount of money in the account after t years is given by the function A(t) = P(1 + r/n)^{nt}.
summary: This section presents the general formula for compound interest based on different compounding frequencies.

header: Limit of Compound Interest as Frequency Increases - Functions and Graphs
content: What happens as n → ∞? To answer this question, we let m = n/r and write (1 + r/n)^{nt} = (1 + 1/m)^{mr} and examine the behavior of (1 + 1/m)^{m} as m → ∞, using a table of values (Table 1.12).
description: Table 1.12 shows the values of (1 + 1/m)^{m} as m approaches infinity, illustrating its convergence.
summary: This section explores the limit of compound interest as compounding frequency increases indefinitely.

header: Table of Values Approaching 'e' - Functions and Graphs
content: Looking at this table, it appears that (1 + 1/m)^{m} is approaching a number between 2.7 and 2.8 as m → ∞. In fact, (1 + 1/m)^{m} does approach some number as m → ∞. We call this number e. To six decimal places of accuracy, e ≈ 2.718282.
summary: This section concludes that as m approaches infinity, the expression converges to the constant e.

header: Historical Context of 'e' - Functions and Graphs
content: The letter e was first used to represent this number by the Swiss mathematician Leonhard Euler during the 1720s. Although Euler did not discover the number, he showed many important connections between e and logarithmic functions. We still use the notation e today to honor Euler’s work because it appears in many areas of mathematics and because we can use it in many practical applications.
summary: This chunk provides historical insight into the notation and significance of the constant e in mathematics.

header: Continuous Compounding Formula - Functions and Graphs
content: Returning to our savings account example, we can conclude that if a person puts P dollars in an account at an annual interest rate r, compounded continuously, then A(t) = P e^{rt}. This function may be familiar.
summary: This section introduces the formula for continuous compounding using the constant e.

header: Natural Exponential Function and Its Properties - Functions and Graphs
content: Since e > 1, we know e^{x} is increasing on (−∞, ∞). In Figure 1.45, we show a graph of f(x) = e^{x} along with a tangent line to the graph at x = 0. We give a precise definition of tangent line in the next chapter; but, informally, we say a tangent line to a graph of f at x = a is a line that passes through the point (a, f(a)) and has the same “slope” as f at that point.
description: Figure 1.45 illustrates the graph of the natural exponential function f(x) = e^{x} with its tangent line at the origin.
summary: This chunk discusses the properties of the natural exponential function and introduces the concept of tangent lines.

header: Uniqueness of the Natural Exponential Function - Functions and Graphs
content: The function f(x) = e^{x} is the only exponential function b^{x} with a tangent line at x = 0 that has a slope of 1. As we see later in the text, having this property makes the natural exponential function the most simple exponential function to use in many instances.
summary: This section highlights the unique property of the natural exponential function related to its slope at the origin.

content: Chapter 1 | Functions and Graphs 101
summary: This chapter introduces the fundamental concepts of functions and graphs.

content: Figure 1.45 The graph of f(x)=e^x has a tangent line with slope 1 at x=0.
description: A graph illustrating the function f(x) = e^x, highlighting a tangent line at the point where x=0.
summary: This figure showcases the behavior of the exponential function at a specific point.

content: Example 1.35 Compounding Interest Suppose $500 is invested in an account at an annual interest rate of r = 5.5%, compounded continuously.
summary: An example demonstrates how to calculate compounded interest.

content: a. Let t denote the number of years after the initial investment and A(t) denote the amount of money in the account at time t. Find a formula for A(t). If P dollars are invested in an account at an annual interest rate r, compounded continuously, then A(t) = P e^(rt). Here P = $500 and r = 0.055. Therefore, A(t) = 500 e^(0.055t).
summary: This section derives the formula for the amount in a continuously compounded interest account.

content: b. Find the amount of money in the account after 10 years and after 20 years. After 10 years, the amount of money in the account is A(10) = 500 e^(0.055·10) = 500 e^0.55 ≈ $866.63. After 20 years, the amount of money in the account is A(20) = 500 e^(0.055·20) = 500 e^1.1 ≈ $1,502.08.
summary: This section provides calculations for account balances after specific time periods.

content: 1.29 If $750 is invested in an account at an annual interest rate of 4%, compounded continuously, find a formula for the amount of money in the account after t years. Find the amount of money after 30 years.
summary: This example explores a different investment scenario with a 4% interest rate.

content: Logarithmic Functions Using our understanding of exponential functions, we can discuss their inverses, which are the logarithmic functions. These come in handy when we need to consider any phenomenon that varies over a wider range of values, such as pH in chemistry or decibels in sound levels.
summary: This section introduces logarithmic functions and their applications.

header: Properties of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
content: The exponential function f(x) = b^x is one-to-one, with domain (−∞, ∞) and range (0, ∞). Therefore, it has an inverse function, called the logarithmic function with base b. For any b > 0, b ≠ 1, the logarithmic function with base b, denoted log_b, has domain (0, ∞) and range (−∞, ∞), and satisfies log_b(x) = y if and only if b^y = x.
summary: This section outlines the properties of exponential and logarithmic functions.

content: The most commonly used logarithmic function is the function loge. Since this function uses natural e as its base, it is called thenatural logarithm. Here we use the notation ln(x) or lnx to mean loge(x). For example, ln(e)=loge(e)=1, ln(e)=3, ln(1)=loge(1)=0.

content:
Rule: Properties of Logarithms
If a, b, c>0, b≠1, and r is any real number, then
1. log (ac)=log (a)+log (c) (Product property)
2. log(c)=log(a)−log(c) (Quotient property)
3. log (a^r)=rlog (a) (Power property)
summary: This section outlines the essential rules governing logarithmic functions.

header: Quadratic Equations and Exponential Functions - Functions and Graphs
content: Rewriting this equation as 2x x e −5e +6=0, we can then rewrite it as a quadratic equation in e^x: (e^x)² −5(e^x)+6=0. Now we can solve the quadratic equation. Factoring this equation, we obtain (e^x −3)(e^x −2)=0. Therefore, the solutions satisfy e^x =3 and e^x =2. Taking the natural logarithm of both sides gives us the solutions x=ln3, ln2.
summary: This section explains how to solve a quadratic equation rewritten from an exponential form.

content: Example 1.37 Solving Equations Involving Logarithmic Functions Solve each of the following equations for x. a. ln (1/x) = 4 b. log(x) + log(x) = 2 c. ln(2x) − 3ln(x^2) = 0
summary: This section presents various equations involving logarithmic functions to solve for x.

header: Solution of Logarithmic Equations - Functions and Graphs
content: Solution a. By the definition of the natural logarithm function, ln (1/x) = 4 if and only if e^4 = 1/x. Therefore, the solution is x = 1/e^4. b. Using the product and power properties of logarithmic functions, rewrite the left-hand side of the equation as log(x) + log(x) = log(x^2). Therefore, the equation can be rewritten as log(x^2) = log(100), which gives x^2 = 100. Hence, x = 10 or x = -10. c. For ln(2x) − 3ln(x^2) = 0, applying properties results in ln(2x) = 6ln(x), leading to ln(2) + ln(x) = 6ln(x). Thus, ln(2) = 5ln(x) gives x = e^(ln(2)/5).
summary: This section provides step-by-step solutions for the previously mentioned logarithmic equations.

content: 1.31 Solve ln ⎛ x 3⎞ −4ln(x)=1. ⎝ ⎠ When evaluating a logarithmic function with a calculator, you may have noticed that the only options are log or log, 10 called the common logarithm, or ln, which is the natural logarithm. content: If you need to use a calculator to evaluate an expression with a different base, you can apply the change-of-base formulas first. Using this change of base, we typically write a given exponential or logarithmic function in terms of the natural exponential and natural logarithmic functions.

content: Rule: Change-of-Base Formulas Let a>0, b>0, and a≠1, b≠1. 1. a x =b x log_b a for any real number x. If b=e, this equation reduces to a x =e x log_e a =e x ln a. 2. log_a x = b for any real number x>0. If b=e, this equation reduces to log_a x = ln x / ln a.
summary: This chunk lists the change-of-base formulas with conditions applied to variables.

content: Proof For the first change-of-base formula, we begin by making use of the power property of logarithmic functions. We know that for any base b>0, b≠1, log_b(a^x)=x log_b a. Therefore, b^x log_b(a) = b.
summary: This chunk presents the proof for the first change-of-base formula for logarithmic functions.

content: In addition, we know that b^x and log_b(x) are inverse functions. Therefore, b^x log_b(a) = a. Combining these last two equalities, we conclude that a^x = b^x log_b a.
summary: This chunk discusses the relationship between exponential and logarithmic functions as inverses.

header: Functions and Graphs: Earthquake Measurement Using the Richter Scale
content:
Chapter Opener: The Richter Scale for Earthquakes
Figure 1.48 (credit: modification of work by Robb Hannawacker, NPS) In 1935, Charles Richter developed a scale (now known as the Richter scale) to measure the magnitude of an earthquake. The scale is a base-10 logarithmic scale, and it can be described as follows: Consider one earthquake with magnitude R on the Richter scale and a second earthquake with magnitude R on the Richter scale. Suppose R >R , which means the earthquake of magnitude R is stronger, but how much stronger is it than the other earthquake? A way of measuring the intensity of an earthquake is by using a seismograph to measure the amplitude of the earthquake waves. If A is the amplitude measured for the first earthquake and A is the amplitude measured for the second earthquake, then the amplitudes and magnitudes of the two earthquakes satisfy the following equation: R −R =log 1. Consider an earthquake that measures 8 on the Richter scale and an earthquake that measures 7 on the Richter scale.
description: Figure 1.48 represents the Richter scale and its application in measuring earthquake magnitudes.
summary: This section details the Richter scale's development and its method for measuring earthquake magnitude.

content: ⎛A ⎞ 8−7=log 1 . 10⎝A ⎠ 2 Therefore, ⎛A ⎞ log 1 =1, 10⎝A ⎠ 2 which implies A /A =10 or A =10A . Since A is 10 times the size of A , we say that the first earthquake is10timesasintenseasthesecondearthquake.Ontheotherhand,ifoneearthquakemeasures8on the Richter scale and another measures 6, then the relative intensity of the two earthquakes satisfies the equation ⎛A ⎞ log 1 =8−6=2. 10⎝A ⎠ 2 Therefore, A =100A . That is, the first earthquake is 100 times more intense than the second earthquake.
summary: This section explains how logarithmic functions can be used to compare the intensity of earthquakes.

content: Howcanweuselogarithmicfunctionstocomparetherelativeseverityofthemagnitude9earthquakeinJapanin 2011 with the magnitude 7.3 earthquake in Haiti in 2010? summary: This section demonstrates the application of logarithmic functions to assess the relative intensity of earthquakes in Japan and Haiti.

summary: This section introduces hyperbolic functions and their significance in engineering and physics.

header: Hyperbolic Identity and Its Geometric Representation
content: This identity is the analog of the trigonometric identity cos 2 t + sin 2 t = 1. Here, given a value t, the point (x, y) = (cosh t, sinh t) lies on the unit hyperbola x² − y² = 1.
description: Figure 1.50 shows the unit hyperbola defined by the hyperbolic identity cosh² t − sinh² t = 1.
summary: The relationship between hyperbolic functions and their geometric representation on the unit hyperbola.

content: To graph coshx and sinhx, we make use of the fact that both functions approach (1/2)e^x as x → ∞, since e^−x → 0 as x → ∞. As x → −∞, coshx approaches 1/2e^−x, whereas sinhx approaches −1/2e^−x. Therefore, using the graphs of 1/2e^x, 1/2e^−x, and −1/2e^−x as guides, we graph coshx and sinhx. To graph tanhx, we use the fact that tanh(0) = 0, −1 < tanh(x) < 1 for all x, tanhx → 1 as x → ∞, and tanhx → −1 as x → −∞. The graphs of the other three hyperbolic functions can be sketched using the graphs of coshx, sinhx, and tanhx.
description: Figure 1.51 illustrates the graphs of the hyperbolic functions cosh, sinh, and tanh based on their respective asymptotic behaviors.
summary: An explanation of the methods used to graph hyperbolic functions along with their asymptotic behaviors.

header: Introduction to Hyperbolic Functions and Their Identities

content: The first four properties follow easily from the definitions of hyperbolic sine and hyperbolic cosine. Except for some differences in signs, most of these properties are analogous to identities for trigonometric functions.

Inverse Hyperbolic Functions
From the graphs of the hyperbolic functions, we see that all of them are one-to-one except coshx and sechx. If we restrict the domains of these two functions to the interval [0, ∞), then all the hyperbolic functions are one-to-one, and we can define the inverse hyperbolic functions. Since the hyperbolic functions themselves involve exponential functions, the inverse hyperbolic functions involve logarithmic functions.

header: Understanding the Derivation of Inverse Hyperbolic Functions
content: Let’s look at how to derive the first equation. The others follow similarly. Suppose y = sinh −1 x. Then, x = sinh y and, y - y by the definition of the hyperbolic sine function, x = e^y - e^-y. Therefore, e^y - e^-2x - e = 0. Multiplying this equation by e^y, we obtain e^2y - 2x e^y - 1 = 0. This can be solved like a quadratic equation, with the solution e^(2y) = x ± sqrt(x^2 + 1). Since e^y > 0, the only solution is the one with the positive sign. Applying the natural logarithm to both sides of the equation, we conclude that y = ln(x + sqrt(x^2 + 1)).
summary: This section explains the derivation of the inverse hyperbolic sine function.

header: Example of Evaluating Inverse Hyperbolic Functions
content: Example 1.41 Evaluating Inverse Hyperbolic Functions Evaluate each of the following expressions. sinh −1(2) tanh −1(1/4) Solution sinh −1(2)=ln(2 + sqrt(2^2 + 1)) = ln(2 + sqrt(5)) ≈ 1.4436 tanh −1(1/4)= ln(1 + 1/4) / (1 - 1/4) = ln(5/4) ≈ 0.2554.
summary: This section provides an example of how to evaluate specific inverse hyperbolic functions.

content: The number of bacteria N in culture after t days can be modeled by the function N(t)=1300·(2)^(t/4). Find the number of bacteria present after 15 days.
summary: Introduction to modeling bacterial growth.

content: According to the World Bank, at the end of 2013 (t=0) the U.S. population was 316 million and was increasing according to the following model: P(t)=316e^(0.0074t), where P is measured in millions of people and t is measured in years after 2013.
summary: Modeling population growth in the U.S. post-2013.

content: a. Based on this model, what will be the population of the United States in 2020? b. Determine when the U.S. population will be twice what it is in 2013.
summary: Calculating future U.S. population based on model.

content: The amount A of a $100,000 investment paying continuously and compounded for t years is given by the function A(t)=100,000·e^(0.055t). Find the amount A accumulated in 5 years.
summary: Introduction to the financial growth of an investment.

content: a. Find the amount accumulated after 5 years and 10 years. b. Determine how long it takes for the original investment to triple.
summary: Calculating different timeframes of investment growth.

content: An investment is compounded monthly, quarterly, or yearly and is given by the function A=P(1+j/n)^(nt), where A is the value of the investment at time t, P is the initial principal that was invested, j is the annual interest rate, and n is the number of times the interest is compounded per year.
summary: Explaining how compounding interest works.

content: Given a yearly interest rate of 3.5% and an initial principle of $100,000, find the amount A accumulated in 5 years for interest that is compounded: a. daily, b. monthly, c. quarterly, and d. yearly.
summary: Calculating investment returns based on different compounding frequencies.

content: A bacterial colony grown in a lab is known to double in number in 12 hours. Suppose initially, there are 1000 bacteria present.
summary: Modeling the growth rate of a bacterial colony.

content: a. Use the exponential function Q=Q₀e^(kt) to determine the value k, which is the growth rate of the bacteria. Round to four decimal places. b. Determine approximately how long it takes for 200,000 bacteria to grow.
summary: Calculating the growth rate and the time for bacterial population increase.

content: The rabbit population on a game reserve doubles every 6 months. Suppose there were 120 rabbits initially.
summary: Exploring how rabbit populations grow in a controlled environment.

content: a. Use the exponential function P=P₀e^(at) to determine the growth rate constant a. Round to four decimal places. b. Use the function in part a to determine approximately how long it takes for the rabbit population to reach 3500.
summary: Determining growth rates and population targets for rabbits.

content: The concentration of hydrogen ions in a substance is denoted by [H⁺], measured in moles per liter. The pH of a substance is defined by the logarithmic function pH=-log[H⁺]. This function is used to measure the acidity of a substance. The pH of water is 7. A substance with a pH less than 7 is an acid, whereas one that has a pH of more than 7 is a base.
summary: Understanding pH and its implications for acidity.

content: a. Find the pH of the following substances. Round answers to one digit. b. Determine whether the substance is an acid or a base. i. Eggs: [H⁺]=1.6×10⁻⁸ mol/L ii. Beer: [H⁺]=3.16×10⁻³ mol/L iii. Tomato Juice: [H⁺]=7.94×10⁻⁵ mol/L
summary: Calculating the pH values and classifying substances.

content: The 1906 earthquake in San Francisco had a magnitude of 8.3 on the Richter scale. At the same time, in Japan, an earthquake with magnitude 4.9 caused only minor damage. Approximately how much more energy was released by the San Francisco earthquake than by the Japanese earthquake?
summary: Comparing seismic activity and its energy release.

content: Iodine-131 is a radioactive substance that decays according to the function Q(t)=Q₀·e^(-0.08664t), where Q₀ is the initial quantity of a sample of the substance and t is in days. Determine how long it takes (to the nearest day) for 95% of a quantity to decay.
summary: Modeling the decay of iodine-131 and calculating decay times.

content: This chapter introduces the fundamental concepts of functions and their graphical representations. Functions are essential building blocks in mathematics, and understanding their properties is crucial for further studies in calculus and applied mathematics.
summary: Overview of the importance of functions and graphs in mathematics.

content: A function is a relationship between a set of inputs and a set of possible outputs, where each input is related to exactly one output. The set of inputs is called the domain, and the set of outputs is called the range.
summary: Introduction to the definition and components of a function.

content: Functions can be classified into several types, including linear, quadratic, polynomial, rational, and exponential functions. Each type has distinct characteristics and typical graph shapes.
summary: Classification of different types of functions.

content: Linear functions can be expressed in the form f(x) = mx + b, where m is the slope and b is the y-intercept. The graph of a linear function is a straight line.
summary: Explanation of linear functions and their graphical representation.

content: Quadratic functions are represented by the equation f(x) = ax^2 + bx + c, where a, b, and c are constants. The graph of a quadratic function is a parabola, which can open upwards or downwards depending on the sign of a.
summary: Introduction to quadratic functions and their characteristic graph shapes.

content: The key features of the graph of a quadratic function include the vertex, axis of symmetry, and intercepts. The vertex represents the minimum or maximum point of the parabola.
summary: Discussion of important features of quadratic function graphs.

content: Rational functions are the quotient of two polynomial functions. They can exhibit asymptotic behavior, where the graph approaches a line but never touches it. Understanding vertical and horizontal asymptotes is critical for graphing these functions.
summary: Explanation of rational functions and their graphing characteristics.

content: Exponential functions are characterized by their rapid increase or decrease proportional to the value of the function. They are written in the form f(x) = a * b^x, where b is a positive constant.
summary: Overview of exponential functions and their unique properties.

header: Summary of Functions and Their Graphs
content: In conclusion, understanding different types of functions and their graphs is crucial for solving problems in mathematics and applying these concepts in various fields such as physics and engineering.
summary: Wrap-up of the chapter's key points on functions and graphs.

summary: Describes the concepts of symmetry in function graphs concerning the origin and y-axis.

header: Overview of Line Equations and Functions
content: • Point-slope equation of a line y−y1 = m(x−x1) • Slope-intercept form of a line y = mx + b • Standard form of a line ax + by = c • Polynomial function f(x) = anxn + an−1xn−1 + ⋯ + a1x + a0 • Generalized sine function f(x) = Asin(B(x−α)) + C • Inverse functions f−1(f(x)) = x for all x in D, and f(f−1(y)) = y for all y in R.
summary: This section introduces various forms of line equations and important mathematical functions.

content: KEY CONCEPTS 1.1 Review of Functions • A function is a mapping from a set of inputs to a set of outputs with exactly one output for each input. • If no domain is stated for a function y = f(x), the domain is considered to be the set of all real numbers x for which the function is defined. • When sketching the graph of a function f, each vertical line may intersect the graph, at most, once. • A function may have any number of zeros, but it has, at most, one y-intercept. • To define the composition g∘f, the range of f must be contained in the domain of g. • Even functions are symmetric about the y-axis whereas odd functions are symmetric about the origin.
summary: This section discusses the fundamental definitions and properties of functions.

content: 1.2 Basic Classes of Functions • The power function f(x) = xn is an even function if n is even and n ≠ 0, and it is an odd function if n is odd. • The root function f(x) = x1/n has the domain [0, ∞) if n is even and the domain (−∞, ∞) if n is odd. If n is odd, then f(x) = x1/n is an odd function. • The domain of the rational function f(x) = p(x)/q(x), where p(x) and q(x) are polynomial functions, is the set of x such that q(x) ≠ 0. • Functions that involve the basic operations of addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and powers are algebraic functions. All other functions are transcendental. Trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions are examples of transcendental functions. • A polynomial function f with degree n ≥ 1 satisfies f(x) → ±∞ as x → ±∞. The sign of the output as x → ∞ depends on the sign of the leading coefficient only and on whether n is even or odd. • Vertical and horizontal shifts, vertical and horizontal scalings, and reflections about the x- and y-axes are examples of transformations of functions.
summary: This section outlines different classes of functions, including algebraic and transcendental functions.

content: 1.3 Trigonometric Functions • Radian measure is defined such that the angle associated with the arc of length 1 on the unit circle has radian measure 1. An angle with a degree measure of 180° has a radian measure of π rad.
summary: This section explains the concept of radian measure in relation to trigonometric functions.

header: Introduction to Trigonometric Functions and Their Definitions
content: For acute angles θ, the values of the trigonometric functions are defined as ratios of two sides of a right triangle in which one of the acute angles is θ.
summary: This section introduces the basic definition of trigonometric functions using right triangles.

content: For a general angle θ, let (x, y) be a point on a circle of radius r corresponding to this angle θ. The trigonometric functions can be written as ratios involving x, y, and r.
summary: This part extends the definition of trigonometric functions to general angles using coordinates on a circle.

content: The trigonometric functions are periodic. The sine, cosine, secant, and cosecant functions have period 2π. The tangent and cotangent functions have period π.
summary: This section discusses the periodic properties of various trigonometric functions.

content: For a function to have an inverse, the function must be one-to-one. Given the graph of a function, we can determine whether the function is one-to-one by using the horizontal line test.
summary: This section outlines the conditions under which a function possesses an inverse.

content: If a function is not one-to-one, we can restrict the domain to a smaller domain where the function is one-to-one and then define the inverse of the function on the smaller domain.
summary: This part explains how to define an inverse for functions that are not one-to-one by restricting their domain.

content: For a function f and its inverse f −1, f(f −1(x)) = x for all x in the domain of f −1 and f −1(f(x)) = x for all x in the domain of f.
summary: This section clarifies the relationship between a function and its inverse.

content: Since the trigonometric functions are periodic, we need to restrict their domains to define the inverse trigonometric functions. The graph of a function f and its inverse f −1 are symmetric about the line y = x.
summary: This part explains the symmetry of graphs of functions and their inverses.

content: The exponential function y = b^x is increasing if b > 1 and decreasing if 0 < b < 1. Its domain is (−∞, ∞) and its range is (0, ∞).
summary: This section describes the properties of exponential functions.

content: The logarithmic function y = log_b(x) is the inverse of y = b^x. Its domain is (0, ∞) and its range is (−∞, ∞).
summary: This part introduces the logarithmic function as an inverse of the exponential function.

content: The natural exponential function is y = e^x and the natural logarithmic function is y = ln(x) = log_e(x).
summary: This section highlights the special characteristics of natural exponential and logarithmic functions.

header: Changing the Base of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
content: Given an exponential function or logarithmic function in base a, we can make a change of base to convert this function to any base b > 0, b ≠ 1. We typically convert to base e.
summary: This section discusses how to change the base of exponential and logarithmic functions.

content: The hyperbolic functions involve combinations of the exponential functions e^x and e^−x. As a result, the inverse hyperbolic functions involve the natural logarithm.
summary: This part introduces hyperbolic functions and their relation to exponential functions.

header: Population Modeling in Ocean City, NJ
content: For the following problems, consider the population of Ocean City, New Jersey, which is cyclical by season. 333. The population can be modeled by P(t)=82.5−67.5cos[(π/6)t], where t is time in months (t=0 represents January 1) and P is population (in thousands). During a year, in what intervals is the population less than 20,000? During what intervals is the population more than 140,000?
summary: This section introduces a seasonal model of population dynamics for Ocean City, NJ.

content: 334. In reality, the overall population is most likely increasing or decreasing throughout each year. Let’s reformulate the model as P(t)=82.5−67.5cos[(π/6)t]+t, where t is time in months (t=0 represents January 1) and P is population (in thousands). When is the first time the population reaches 200,000?
summary: Refinements to the population model account for yearly trends while providing a new equation.

header: The Intersection of Science Fiction and Mathematical Limits
content:
Introduction
Science fiction writers often imagine spaceships that can travel to far-off planets in distant galaxies. However, back in 1905, Albert Einstein showed that a limit exists to how fast any object can travel.
summary: The introduction connects science fiction ideas to the mathematical limits established by Einstein.

header: Mass and Energy: The Formula Behind Travel Limits
content: The problem is that the faster an object moves, the more mass it attains (in the form of energy), according to the equation m = m0 / sqrt(1 − v^2/c^2).
summary: This chunk explains how speed affects mass and energy, introducing a key equation.

content:
2.1 | A Preview of Calculus
Learning Objectives
2.1.1 Describe the tangent problem and how it led to the idea of a derivative.
2.1.2 Explain how the idea of a limit is involved in solving the tangent problem.
2.1.3 Recognize a tangent to a curve at a point as the limit of secant lines.
2.1.4 Identify instantaneous velocity as the limit of average velocity over a small time interval.
2.1.5 Describe the area problem and how it was solved by the integral.
2.1.6 Explain how the idea of a limit is involved in solving the area problem.
2.1.7 Recognizehowtheideasoflimit,derivative,andintegralledtothestudiesofinfiniteseriesandmultivariablecalculus.
summary: Outlines the learning objectives focusing on key calculus problems and concepts.

content: As we embark on our study of calculus, we shall see how its development arose from common solutions to practical problems in areas such as engineering physics—like the space travel problem posed in the chapter opener. Two key problems led to the initial formulation of calculus: (1) the tangent problem, or how to determine the slope of a line tangent to a curve at a point; and (2) the area problem, or how to determine the area under a curve.
summary: Discusses the origins of calculus as responses to practical problems.

content:

header: Understanding the Graph of k(x) - Limits
content: Compare the graphs of these three functions with the graph of k(x)= x². The graph of k(x)= x² starts from the left by decreasing rapidly, then begins to decrease more slowly and level off, and then finally begins to increase—slowly at first, followed by an increasing rate of increase as it moves toward the right. Unlike a linear function, no single number represents the rate of change for this function. We quite naturally ask: How do we measure the rate of change of a nonlinear function?
summary: This section explains the behavior of the graph of k(x) and raises questions about measuring nonlinear rates of change.

content: Figure 2.3 The function k(x)= x² does not have a constant rate of change.
description: Figure 2.3 illustrates that the function k(x)= x² exhibits varying rates of change, emphasizing its nonlinear characteristics.
summary: Figure 2.3 highlights the non-constant nature of change in the function k(x).

content: We can approximate the rate of change of a function f(x) at a point (a, f(a)) on its graph by taking another point (x, f(x)) on the graph of f(x), drawing a line through the two points, and calculating the slope of the resulting line. Such a line is called a secant line.
summary: This section discusses the method of estimating the rate of change using a secant line.

content: Figure 2.4 shows a secant line to a function f(x) at a point (a, f(a)).
description: Figure 2.4 demonstrates how the slope of a secant line through a point estimates the rate of change of the function at that point.
summary: Figure 2.4 illustrates the concept of a secant line and its relevance to estimating rates of change.

header: Formal Definition of Secant Line - Limits
content: We formally define a secant line as follows:
summary: This section introduces a formal definition for a secant line.

content: The secant to the function f(x) through the points (a, f(a)) and (x, f(x)) is the line passing through these points. Its slope is given by f(x)− f(a)/(x−a). The accuracy of approximating the rate of change of the function with a secant line depends on how close x is to a.
summary: This section introduces the concept of secant lines and how they are used to approximate the rate of change of a function.

content: As we see in Figure 2.5, if x is closer to a, the slope of the secant line is a better measure of the rate of change of f(x) at a.
description: Figure 2.5 illustrates how the slope of the secant line becomes a better approximation to the rate of change of the function f(x) as x approaches a.
summary: This section discusses how the proximity of x to a affects the accuracy of the slope of the secant line.

content: The secant lines themselves approach a line that is called the tangent to the function f(x) at a. The slope of the tangent line to the graph at a measures the rate of change of the function at a. This value also represents the derivative of the function f(x) at a, or the rate of change of the function at a. This derivative is denoted by f′(a).
summary: This section explains how secant lines lead to the concept of tangent lines and their relationship to derivatives.

content: Differential calculus is the field of calculus concerned with the study of derivatives and their applications.
summary: This section provides a brief overview of differential calculus and its focus on derivatives.

description: Figure 2.6 illustrates the tangent problem, where secant lines converge towards the tangent line as x nears a.
summary: This section visualizes the concept of secant lines approaching tangent lines.

header: Understanding Secant Lines and Their Slopes in Calculus

header: Example Calculation of Secant Line Slopes
content:
Example 2.1
Finding Slopes of Secant Lines

header: Introduction to Area Problems in Calculus - The Area Problem and Integral Calculus
content: We now turn our attention to a classic question from calculus. Many quantities in physics—for example, quantities of work—may be interpreted as the area under a curve. This leads us to ask the question: How can we find the area between the graph of a function and the x-axis over an interval (Figure 2.8)?
description: Figure 2.8 illustrates the area problem, posing the question of how to find the area of the shaded region under the curve.
summary: This section introduces the concept of finding areas under curves in calculus.

header: Approximating Area Under the Curve - The Area Problem and Integral Calculus
content: As in the answer to our previous questions on velocity, we first try to approximate the solution. We approximate the area by dividing up the interval [a, b] into smaller intervals in the shape of rectangles. The approximation of the area comes from adding up the areas of these rectangles (Figure 2.9).
description: Figure 2.9 demonstrates how the area of the region under the curve is approximated by summing the areas of thin rectangles.
summary: This section explains how to approximate the area under a curve using rectangles.

header: Limits and Definite Integrals - Integral Calculus
content: As the widths of the rectangles become smaller (approach zero), the sums of the areas of the rectangles approach the area between the graph of f(x) and the x-axis over the interval [a, b]. Once again, we find ourselves taking a limit. Limits of this type serve as a basis for the definition of the definite integral. Integral calculus is the study of integrals and their applications.
summary: This section discusses how limits are foundational to defining the definite integral in calculus.

header: Understanding Area Estimation Using Rectangles in Limits

content: Other Aspects of Calculus So far, we have studied functions of one variable only. Such functions can be represented visually using graphs in two dimensions; however, there is no good reason to restrict our investigation to two dimensions.
summary: This introduction highlights the limitations of studying only one-variable functions.

header: Exploration Beyond One Dimension in Calculus
content: Suppose, for example, that instead of determining the velocity of an object moving along a coordinate axis, we want to determine the velocity of a rock fired from a catapult at a given time, or of an airplane moving in three dimensions.
summary: This section explores the applications of calculus in understanding motion in three dimensions.

content: We might want to graph real-value functions of two variables or determine volumes of solids of the types shown in Figure 2.11. These are only a few of the types of questions that can be asked and answered using multivariable calculus.
description: Figure 2.11 We can use multivariable calculus to find the volume between a surface defined by a function of two variables and a plane.
summary: This section discusses potential applications of multivariable calculus, including graphing functions of two variables.

content: Informally, multivariable calculus can be characterized as the study of the calculus of functions of two or more variables. However, before exploring these and other ideas, we must first lay a foundation for the study of calculus in one variable by exploring the concept of a limit.
summary: This section defines multivariable calculus and emphasizes the need to understand limits before further exploration.

content:
2.2 | The Limit of a Function
Learning Objectives
2.2.1 Using correct notation, describe the limit of a function.
2.2.2 Use a table of values to estimate the limit of a function or to identify when the limit does not exist.
2.2.3 Use a graph to estimate the limit of a function or to identify when the limit does not exist.
2.2.4 Define one-sided limits and provide examples.
2.2.5 Explain the relationship between one-sided and two-sided limits.
2.2.6 Using correct notation, describe an infinite limit.
2.2.7 Define a vertical asymptote.
summary: Outlines the objectives related to understanding the limit of a function.

content: The concept of a limit or limiting process, essential to the understanding of calculus, has been around for thousands of years. In fact, early mathematicians used a limiting process to obtain better and better approximations of areas of circles. Yet, the formal definition of a limit—as we know and understand it today—did not appear until the late 19th century. We therefore begin our quest to understand limits, as our mathematical ancestors did, by using an intuitive approach. At the end of this chapter, armed with a conceptual understanding of limits, we examine the formal definition of a limit.
summary: Discusses the historical evolution and importance of the concept of limits in mathematics.

content: We begin our exploration of limits by taking a look at the graphs of the functions f(x)=(x^2 −4)/(x−2), g(x)=(x−2), and h(x)=(x−2)^2, which are shown in Figure 2.12. In particular, let’s focus our attention on the behavior of each graph at and around x=2.
description: Figure 2.12 shows the graphs of three different functions: f(x), g(x), and h(x) around x=2.
summary: Introduces the graphs of three functions to understand their behavior around the limit.

content: Each of the three functions is undefined at x=2, but if we make this statement and no other, we give a very incomplete picture of how each function behaves in the vicinity of x=2. To express the behavior of each graph in the vicinity of 2 more completely, we need to introduce the concept of a limit.
summary: Explains the necessity of understanding limits to describe function behavior near points of discontinuity.

content: Let’s first take a closer look at how the function f(x)=(x^2 −4)/(x−2) behaves around x=2 in Figure 2.12. As the values of x approach 2 from either side of 2, the values of y=f(x) approach 4. Mathematically, we say that the limit of f(x) as x approaches 2 is 4. Symbolically, we express this limit as...
summary: Introduces the intuitive understanding of limits using a specific function around a point.

content:
lim f(x)=4.
x→2
From this very brief informal look at one limit, let’s start to develop an intuitive definition of the limit. We can think of the limit of a function at a number a as being the one real number L that the functional values approach as the x-values approach a, provided such a real number L exists. Stated more carefully, we have the following definition:
summary: An introduction to the intuitive definition of limits in calculus.

content:
Definition
Let f(x) be a function defined at all values in an open interval containing a, with the possible exception of a itself, and let L be a real number. If all values of the function f(x) approach the real number L as the values of x(≠a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit of f(x) as x approaches a is L. (More succinctly, as x gets closer to a, f(x) gets closer and stays close to L.) Symbolically, we express this idea as
lim f(x)=L. (2.3)
x→a
summary: A formal definition of limits in calculus, describing how limits are expressed mathematically.

content: We can estimate limits by constructing tables of functional values and by looking at their graphs. This process is described in the following Problem-Solving Strategy.
summary: An overview of using tables to estimate limits in calculus.

content:
Problem-Solving Strategy: Evaluating a Limit Using a Table of Functional Values
1. To evaluate lim f(x), we begin by completing a table of functional values. We should choose two sets of x-values—one set of values approaching a and less than a, and another set of values approaching a and greater than a. Table 2.1 demonstrates what your tables might look like.
description: Table 2.1: A table illustrating values of x and their corresponding f(x) values approaching a from both sides.
summary: A strategy outlining the steps to evaluate a limit using functional value tables.

content:
x f(x) x f(x)
a−0.1 f(a−0.1) a+0.1 f(a+0.1)
a−0.01 f(a−0.01) a+0.01 f(a+0.01)
a−0.001 f(a−0.001) a+0.001 f(a+0.001)
a−0.0001 f(a−0.0001) a+0.0001 f(a+0.0001)
Use additional values as necessary. Use additional values as necessary.
Table 2.1 Table of Functional Values for lim f(x)
x→a
description: Table 2.1 shows a structured approach to listing x-values and their corresponding f(x) values to evaluate limits.
summary: A sample table for evaluating limits with functional values approaching a.

header: Analyzing Functional Values in Limit Tables
content: 2. Next, let’s look at the values in each of the f(x) columns and determine whether the values seem to be approaching a single value as we move down each column. In our columns, we look at the sequence f(a−0.1), f(a−0.01), f(a−0.001), f(a−0.0001), and so on, and f(a+0.1), f(a+0.01), f(a+0.001), f(a+0.0001), and so on. (Note: Although we have chosen the x-values a±0.1, a±0.01, a±0.001, a±0.0001, and so forth, these values will probably work nearly every time, on very rare occasions we may need to modify our choices.)
summary: A guideline for analyzing whether the functional values in the limit evaluation are approaching a single value.

content: 3. If both columns approach a common value L, we state lim f(x)=L. We can use the following strategy to confirm the result obtained from the table or as an alternative method for estimating a limit.
summary: The final step in limit evaluation where a common value is used to state the limit.

header: Graphical Analysis of Limits - Using Technology
content: Using a graphing calculator or computer software that allows us graph functions, we can plot the function f(x), making sure the functional values of f(x) for x-values near a are in our window. We can use the trace feature to move along the graph of the function and watch the y-value readout as the x-values approach a. If the y-values approach L as our x-values approach a from both directions, then lim f(x) = L. We may need to zoom in on our graph and repeat this process several times.
summary: Technological tools can assist in visually interpreting limits of functions.

content: We apply this Problem-Solving Strategy to compute a limit in Example 2.4.
summary: An example is provided to illustrate the application of limit computation strategies.

header: Evaluating the Limit of g(x) at x = -1
content: Despite the fact that g(−1)=4, as the x-values approach −1 from either side, the g(x) values approach 3. Therefore, lim g(x)=3.
summary: The limit of g(x) is evaluated, showing that it approaches 3 as x approaches -1.

content: Note that we can determine this limit without even knowing the algebraic expression of the function. Based on Example 2.6, we make the following observation: It is possible for the limit of a function to exist at a point, and for the function to be defined at this point, but the limit of the function and the value of the function at the point may be different.
summary: This section discusses the distinction between the limit of a function and its actual value at a defined point.

content: 2.5 Use the graph of h(x) in Figure 2.16 to evaluate lim h(x), if possible. Looking at a table of functional values or looking at the graph of a function provides us with useful insight into the value of the limit of a function at a given point. However, these techniques rely too much on guesswork. We eventually need to develop alternative methods of evaluating limits. These new methods are more algebraic in nature and we explore them in the next section; however, at this point we introduce two special limits that are foundational to the techniques to come.
description: This section references a graph that is essential for visualizing the limit of the function h(x) as x approaches 2.
summary: This section discusses the evaluation of limits using graphical insights and introduces foundational limits.

content: Theorem 2.1: Two Important Limits Let a be a real number and c be a constant. i. lim x=a (2.4) x→a ii. lim c=c (2.5) We can make the following observations about these two limits.

content: One-Sided Limits Sometimes indicating that the limit of a function fails to exist at a point does not provide us with enough information about the behavior of the function at that particular point. To see this, we now revisit the function g(x)=|x−2|/(x−2) introduced at the beginning of this section (see Figure 2.12(b)). As we pick values of x close to 2, g(x) does not approach a single value, so the limit as x approaches 2 does not exist—that is, lim g(x) DNE. However, this statement alone does not give us a complete picture of the behavior of the function around the x-value 2.
description: Figure 2.12(b) depicts the behavior of the function g(x)=|x−2|/(x−2) as x approaches 2.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of one-sided limits and revisits a specific function to illustrate limit non-existence.

content: Toprovideamoreaccuratedescription, weintroducetheideaofaone-sidedlimit. Forallvaluestotheleftof2(orthenegativesideof2), g(x) = −1. Thus, as x approaches 2 from the left, g(x) approaches −1. Mathematically, we say that the limit as x approaches 2 from the left is −1. Symbolically, we express this idea as lim g(x) = −1.
summary: This section mathematically defines the behavior of a function as x approaches a point from the left.

header: Analyzing One-Sided Limits from the Right
content: Similarly, as x approaches 2 from the right (or from the positive side), g(x) approaches 1. Symbolically, we express this idea as lim g(x) = 1.
summary: This section defines the behavior of a function as x approaches a point from the right.

content: We can now present an informal definition of one-sided limits.
summary: This chunk includes an introduction to the formal definition of one-sided limits.

content: Limit from the left: Let f(x) be a function defined at all values in an open interval of the form (c, a), and let L be a real number. If the values of the function f(x) approach the real number L as the values of x (where x < a) approach the number a, then we say that L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches a from the left. Symbolically, we express this idea as lim f(x) = L. (2.6)
summary: This section defines the concept of left-hand limits in calculus.

content: Limit from the right: Let f(x) be a function defined at all values in an open interval of the form (a, c), and let L be a real number. If the values of the function f(x) approach the real number L as the values of x (where x > a) approach the number a, then we say that L is the limit of f(x) as x approaches a from the right. Symbolically, we express this idea as lim f(x) = L. (2.7)
summary: This section defines the concept of right-hand limits in calculus.

header: Evaluating One-Sided Limits: A Practical Example
content: Example 2.8 Evaluating One-Sided Limits. For the function f(x) = { x + 1 if x < 2; x^2 - 4 if x ≥ 2 }, evaluate each of the following limits. a. lim f(x) as x approaches 2 from the left. b. lim f(x) as x approaches 2 from the right.
summary: This section presents an example to evaluate one-sided limits for a piecewise function.

content: Solution. We can use tables of functional values again. Observe that for values of x less than 2, we use f(x) = x + 1 and for values of x greater than 2, we use f(x) = x^2 - 4.

header: Relationship Between One-Sided and Two-Sided Limits (Parent topic: Chapter 2 | Limits)
content: Let us now consider the relationship between the limit of a function at a point and the limits from the right and left at that point. It seems clear that if the limit from the right and the limit from the left have a common value, then that common value is the limit of the function at that point. Similarly, if the limit from the left and the limit from the right take on different values, the limit of the function does not exist.
description: Empty string
summary: This section describes how one-sided limits relate to the existence of two-sided limits.

header: Theorem: Relating One-Sided and Two-Sided Limits (Parent topic: Chapter 2 | Limits)
content:
Theorem 2.2: Relating One-Sided and Two-Sided Limits
Let f(x) be a function defined at all values in an open interval containing a, with the possible exception of a itself, and let L be a real number. Then, lim f(x)=L if and only if lim f(x)=L and lim f(x)=L.
description: Empty string
summary: This theorem formalizes the conditions under which one-sided and two-sided limits are equal.

content: Evaluating the limit of a function at a point or evaluating the limit of a function from the right and left at a point helps us to characterize the behavior of a function around a given value. As we shall see, we can also describe the behavior of functions that do not have finite limits.
summary: This section introduces the concept of infinite limits and their importance in understanding function behavior.

header: Example of an Infinite Limit: h(x) = 1/(x−2)²
content: We now turn our attention to h(x)=1/(x−2)², the third and final function introduced at the beginning of this section. From its graph we see that as the values of x approach 2, the values of h(x)=1/(x−2)² become larger and larger and, in fact, become infinite. Mathematically, we say that the limit of h(x) as x approaches 2 is positive infinity. Symbolically, we express this idea as lim h(x)=+∞. x→2
description: Figure 2.12(c) shows the graph of h(x)=1/(x−2)², illustrating how it approaches infinity as x approaches 2.
summary: This example uses the function h(x)=1/(x−2)² to illustrate the concept of positive infinity as x approaches 2.

content: More generally, we define infinite limits as follows: We define three types of infinite limits.
summary: This section sets the framework for defining various types of infinite limits.

header: Types of Infinite Limits from the Left
content: Infinite limits from the left: Let f(x) be a function defined at all values in an open interval of the form (b, a). i. If the values of f(x) increase without bound as the values of x (where x<a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit as x approaches a from the left is positive infinity and we write lim f(x)=+∞. (2.8) x→a ii. If the values of f(x) decrease without bound as the values of x (where x<a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit as x approaches a from the left is negative infinity and we write lim f(x)=−∞. (2.9)
summary: This section defines infinite limits from the left side of a point.

header: Types of Infinite Limits from the Right
content: Infinite limits from the right: Let f(x) be a function defined at all values in an open interval of the form (a, c). i. If the values of f(x) increase without bound as the values of x (where x>a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit as x approaches a from the right is positive infinity and we write lim f(x)=+∞. (2.10) ii. If the values of f(x) decrease without bound as the values of x (where x>a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit as x approaches a from the right is negative infinity and we write lim f(x)=−∞. (2.11)
summary: This section defines infinite limits from the right side of a point.

content: Two-sided infinite limit: Let f(x) be defined for all x≠a in an open interval containing a. i. If the values of f(x) increase without bound as the values of x (where x≠a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit as x approaches a is positive infinity and we write lim f(x)=+∞. (2.12) ii. If the values of f(x) decrease without bound as the values of x (where x≠a) approach the number a, then we say that the limit as x approaches a is negative infinity and we write lim f(x)=−∞. (2.13)
summary: This section defines the two-sided infinite limits and their characteristics.

content: It is important to understand that when we write statements such as lim f(x)=+∞ or lim f(x)=−∞ we are describing the behavior of the function, as we have just defined it. We are not asserting that a limit exists. For the limit of a function f(x) to exist at a, it must approach a real number L as x approaches a. That said, if, for example, lim f(x)=+∞, we always write lim f(x)=+∞ rather than lim f(x) DNE.
summary: This section clarifies the notation used for infinite limits and how it differs from asserting that a limit exists.
