header: Understanding Infinite Limits with Positive Integers
content: It is useful to point out that functions of the form f(x)=1/(x−a)^n, where n is a positive integer, have infinite limits as x approaches a from either the left or right.
description: Figure 2.20 would illustrate the behavior of functions f(x)=1/(x−a)^n near the point a.

header: Understanding Infinite Limits and Vertical Asymptotes in Functions
content: Figure 2.20 The function f(x)=1/(x−a)n has infinite limits at a.
description: Figure showing the function f(x) and its infinite limits at a.
summary: This figure illustrates how the function behaves around the point where it has infinite limits.

header: Theorem on Infinite Limits from Positive Integers
content: Theorem 2.3: Infinite Limits from Positive Integers If n is a positive even integer, then lim (1/(x−a)^n) as x approaches a equals +∞. If n is a positive odd integer, then lim (1/(x−a)^n) as x approaches a equals +∞ and lim (1/(−(x−a)^n) as x approaches a equals -∞.
summary: This theorem describes the behavior of limits involving positive even and odd integers.

content: We should also point out that in the graphs of f(x)=1/(x−a)^n, points on the graph having x-coordinates very near to a are very close to the vertical line x=a. That is, as x approaches a, the points on the graph of f(x) are closer to the line x=a.
summary: This section discusses how the graph behaves near the vertical asymptote.

content: The line x=a is called a vertical asymptote of the graph. We formally define a vertical asymptote as follows: Let f(x) be a function. If any of the following conditions hold, then the line x=a is a vertical asymptote of f(x). 1) lim f(x) as x approaches a = +∞ or −∞, 2) lim f(x) as x approaches a from the right = +∞ or −∞, 3) lim f(x) as x approaches a = +∞ or −∞.
summary: This segment provides a formal definition for vertical asymptotes based on limit conditions.

header: Limits of Speed According to Einstein
content: In the chapter opener, we mentioned briefly how Albert Einstein showed that a limit exists to how fast any object can travel. Given Einstein’s equation for the mass of a moving object, what is the value of this bound?
summary: This section introduces the concept of the speed limit according to Einstein's theory.

content: Our starting point is Einstein’s equation for the mass of a moving object, m = m0 / sqrt(1 - v^2/c^2), where m is the object’s mass at rest, v is its speed, and c is the speed of light.
summary: This section defines the equation for the mass of a moving object as per Einstein.

content: To see how the mass changes at high speeds, we can graph the ratio of masses m/m0 as a function of the ratio of speeds, v/c.
description: Figure 2.23 shows the ratio of masses as a function of the ratio of speeds in Einstein’s equation for the mass of a moving object.
summary: This section discusses the graphical representation of mass changes with speed ratios.

header: Behavior of Mass as Speed Approaches Light Speed
content: We can see that as the ratio of speeds approaches 1—that is, as the speed of the object approaches the speed of light—the ratio of masses increases without bound. In other words, the function has a vertical asymptote at v/c = 1.
summary: This section explains the implications of mass changes as speed approaches the speed of light.

header: Testing the Mass and Speed Relationship
content: We can try a few values of this ratio to test this idea.
summary: This section suggests testing specific values to understand the relationship between mass and speed.

header: Limits of Mass and Speed in Relativity
summary: Introduction to the limits of mass and speed as outlined in the context of relativity.

header: Understanding the Impact of Speed on Mass
content: Thus, according to Table 2.8, if an object with mass 100kg is traveling at 0.9999c, its mass becomes 7071kg.
description: This chunk discusses the implications of the data presented in Table 2.8 regarding the increase in mass at relativistic speeds.
summary: Explanation of how relativistic speeds affect mass.

header: Conclusion on Speed Limits in Physics
content: Since no object can have an infinite mass, we conclude that no object can travel at or more than the speed of light.
summary: Final thoughts on the impossibility of exceeding the speed of light in relation to mass.

content:
Learning Objectives
2.3.1 Recognize the basic limit laws.
2.3.2 Use the limit laws to evaluate the limit of a function.
2.3.3 Evaluate the limit of a function by factoring.
2.3.4 Use the limit laws to evaluate the limit of a polynomial or rational function.
2.3.5 Evaluate the limit of a function by factoring or by using conjugates.
2.3.6 Evaluate the limit of a function by using the squeeze theorem.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to the limit laws.

header: Applying Limit Laws to Evaluate Limits
content: In the previous section, we evaluated limits by looking at graphs or by constructing a table of values. In this section, we establish laws for calculating limits and learn how to apply these laws. In the Student Project at the end of this section, you have the opportunity to apply these limit laws to derive the formula for the area of a circle by adapting a method devised by the Greek mathematician Archimedes.
summary: This part introduces the application of limit laws through historical context.

content: We begin by restating two useful limit results from the previous section. These two results, together with the limit laws, serve as a foundation for calculating many limits.
summary: Foundational results are presented to support the understanding of limit calculations.

content: The first two limit laws were stated in Two Important Limits and we repeat them here. These basic results, together with the other limit laws, allow us to evaluate limits of many algebraic functions.
summary: Repeated definition of basic limit laws vital for evaluating algebraic functions.

content:
For any real number a and any constant c,
i. lim x=a (2.14)
x→a
ii. lim c=c (2.15)
x→a
summary: Theorem 2.4 presents fundamental results about limits.

content:
Example 2.13

x→2

content: We now take a look at the limit laws, the individual properties of limits. The proofs that these laws hold are omitted here.
summary: An exploration of limit laws is introduced, with a note on omitted proofs.

content:
Theorem 2.5: Limit Laws
Let f(x) and g(x) be defined for all x≠a over some open interval containing a. Assume that L and M are real numbers such that lim f(x)=L and lim g(x)=M. Let c be a constant. Then, each of the following statements holds:
summary: Introduces the theorem related to limit laws.

header: Sum and Difference Laws - Limit Laws
content:
Sum law for limits: lim ⎛f(x)+g(x)⎞= lim f(x)+ lim g(x)=L+M
Difference law for limits: lim ⎛f(x)−g(x)⎞= lim f(x)− lim g(x)=L−M
summary: Details the sum and difference laws for limits.

header: Constant Multiple and Product Laws - Limit Laws
content:
Constant multiple law for limits: lim cf(x)=c· lim f(x)=cL
Product law for limits: lim ⎛f(x)·g(x)⎞= lim f(x)· lim g(x)=L·M
summary: Describes the constant multiple and product laws for limits.

header: Quotient Law and Power Law - Limit Laws
content:
Quotient law for limits: lim f(x) = x li → m a f(x) = L for M ≠0
Power law for limits: lim ⎛f(x)⎞n = ⎛ lim f(x) ⎞ =L n for every positive integer n.
summary: Outlines the quotient and power laws for limits.

content: Root law for limits: lim n f(x)= n lim f(x)= n L for all L if n is odd and for L≥0 if n is even and f⎛x⎞≥0.
summary: Defines the root law for limits.

content:
We now practice applying these limit laws to evaluate a limit.

Solution
Let’s apply the limit laws one step at a time to be sure we understand how they work. We need to keep in mind the requirement that, at each application of a limit law, the new limits must exist for the limit law to be applied.

Continues the example to calculate the limit step-by-step.

summary: Introduction to the concept of applying limit laws for limit evaluation.

header: Theorem on Limits of Polynomial Functions
content: By now you have probably noticed that, in each of the previous examples, it has been the case that lim f(x)= f(a). This is not always true, but it does hold for all polynomials for any choice of a and for all rational functions at all values of a for which the rational function is defined.
summary: Establishing a theorem about limits and polynomials.

content: Theorem 2.6: Limits of Polynomial and Rational Functions. Let p(x) and q(x) be polynomial functions. Let a be a real number. Then, lim p(x) = p(a) when q(a)≠0.
summary: Formal statement of the theorem regarding limits of polynomial functions.

header: Proof of the Polynomial Limits Theorem
content: To see that this theorem holds, consider the polynomial p(x)=cnx^n + cn−1x^(n−1) + … + c1x + c0. By applying the sum, constant multiple, and power laws, we end up with lim p(x) as x approaches a equalling p(a).
summary: A proof demonstrating the theorem on limits of polynomial functions.

content: It now follows from the quotient law that if p(x) and q(x) are polynomials for which q(a)≠0, then lim (p(x)/q(x)) = (p(a)/q(a)).
summary: Application of the quotient law in evaluating limits of polynomial functions.

content: The limit has the form lim , where lim f(x)=0 and lim g(x)=0. (In this case, we say that f(x)/g(x) has the indeterminate form 0/0.) The following Problem-Solving Strategy provides a general outline for evaluating limits of this type.
summary: This section introduces a problem-solving strategy for handling limits that lead to indeterminate forms.

content:
1. First, we need to make sure that our function has the appropriate form and cannot be evaluated immediately using the limit laws.
2. We then need to find a function that is equal to h(x)= f(x)/g(x) for all x≠a over some interval containing a. To do this, we may need to try one or more of the following steps:
   a. If f(x) and g(x) are polynomials, we should factor each function and cancel out any common factors.
   b. If the numerator or denominator contains a difference involving a square root, we should try multiplying the numerator and denominator by the conjugate of the expression involving the square root.
   c. If f(x)/g(x) is a complex fraction, we begin by simplifying it.
3. Last, we apply the limit laws.
summary: This section outlines the steps to evaluate limits that lead to an indeterminate form.

content: The next examples demonstrate the use of this Problem-Solving Strategy. Example 2.17 illustrates the factor-and-cancel technique; Example 2.18 shows multiplying by a conjugate. In Example 2.19, we look at simplifying a complex fraction.
summary: This section previews examples that apply the previously discussed strategies for calculating limits.

header: The Squeeze Theorem: Introduction and Definition
content: Theorem 2.7: The Squeeze Theorem. Let f(x), g(x), and h(x) be defined for all x≠a over an open interval containing a. If f(x)≤g(x)≤h(x) for all x≠a in an open interval containing a and lim f(x)=L= lim h(x) where L is a real number, then lim g(x)=L.
summary: Introduction to the Squeeze Theorem and its mathematical definition.

header: Example 2.24: Evaluating Limits with the Squeeze Theorem
content: Example 2.24. Applying the Squeeze Theorem. Apply the squeeze theorem to evaluate lim xcosx as x approaches 0. Solution: Because −1≤cosx≤1 for all x, we have −|x|≤ xcosx≤|x|. Since lim (−|x|)=0= lim |x|, from the squeeze theorem, we obtain lim xcosx=0.
summary: An example showing the application of the Squeeze Theorem to evaluate a limit.

content: The graphs of f(x)= −|x|, g(x)= xcosx, and h(x)=|x| are shown in Figure 2.28.

header: Further Applications of the Squeeze Theorem
content: 2.19 Use the squeeze theorem to evaluate lim x^2 sin(1/x) as x approaches 0. We now use the squeeze theorem to tackle several very important limits. Although this discussion is somewhat lengthy, these limits prove invaluable for the development of the material in both the next section and the next chapter.
summary: Discussion on the importance of further applications of the Squeeze Theorem in limit evaluation.

header: Limit of sin(θ) as θ Approaches Zero

header: Geometric Foundations of Circle Area Calculation
content: Some of the geometric formulas we take for granted today were first derived by methods that anticipate some of the methods of calculus. The Greek mathematician Archimedes (ca. 287−212 BCE) was particularly inventive, using polygons inscribed within circles to approximate the area of the circle as the number of sides of the polygon increased. He never came up with the idea of a limit, but we can use this idea to see what his geometric constructions could have predicted about the limit.
summary: This chunk discusses Archimedes' innovative approach to approximating the area of a circle using inscribed polygons.

header: Estimating the Area of a Circle Using Triangles
content: We can estimate the area of a circle by computing the area of an inscribed regular polygon. Think of the regular polygon as being made up of n triangles. By taking the limit as the vertex angle of these triangles goes to zero, you can obtain the area of the circle. To see this, carry out the following steps:
summary: This section introduces the process of estimating a circle's area through inscribed polygons and triangles.

content: 1. Express the height h and the base b of the isosceles triangle in Figure 2.31 in terms of θ and r.
description: Figure 2.31 illustrates an isosceles triangle inscribed in a circle, showing the height and base related to the angles and radius.
summary: This step involves defining the dimensions of the triangle used in the area estimation.

header: Step 2: Area of the Isosceles Triangle
content: 2. Using the expressions that you obtained in step 1, express the area of the isosceles triangle in terms of θ and r. (Substitute (1/2)sinθ for sin(θ/2)cos(θ/2) in your expression.)
summary: This step describes how to formulate the area of the isosceles triangle based on previously defined dimensions.

header: Step 3: Relationship Between Angles and Sides
content: 3. If an n-sided regular polygon is inscribed in a circle of radius r, find a relationship between θ and n. Solve this for n. Keep in mind there are 2π radians in a circle. (Use radians, not degrees.)
summary: This step establishes a mathematical relationship between the number of polygon sides and the angle in radians.

header: Step 4: Area Expression for the Polygon
content: 4. Find an expression for the area of the n-sided polygon in terms of r and θ.
summary: This step focuses on deriving the area expression for the polygon inscribed in the circle.

header: Step 5: Limit to Find Area of a Circle
content: 5. To find a formula for the area of the circle, find the limit of the expression in step 4 as θ goes to zero. (Hint: (sinθ) lim = 1).
summary: This final step involves applying limits to derive the area formula for a circle.

content:
Learning Objectives
2.4.1 Explain the three conditions for continuity at a point.
2.4.2 Describe three kinds of discontinuities.
2.4.3 Define continuity on an interval.
2.4.4 State the theorem for limits of composite functions.
2.4.5 Provide an example of the intermediate value theorem.
summary: Overview of learning objectives related to limits and continuity.

content: Many functions have the property that their graphs can be traced with a pencil without lifting the pencil from the page. Such functions are called continuous. Other functions have points at which a break in the graph occurs, but satisfy this property over intervals contained in their domains. They are continuous on these intervals and are said to have a discontinuity at a point where a break occurs.
summary: Explanation of continuous and discontinuous functions.

content: We begin our investigation of continuity by exploring what it means for a function to have continuity at a point. Intuitively, a function is continuous at a particular point if there is no break in its graph at that point.
summary: Introduction to the concept of continuity at a point.

content: Before we look at a formal definition of what it means for a function to be continuous at a point, let’s consider various functions that fail to meet our intuitive notion of what it means to be continuous at a point. We then create a list of conditions that prevent such failures.
summary: Exploring conditions necessary for continuity and identifying failures.

header: Example of Discontinuity: Hole in the Graph
content:
Our first function of interest is shown in Figure 2.32. We see that the graph of f(x) has a hole at a. In fact, f(a) is undefined. At the very least, for f(x) to be continuous at a, we need the following condition:
i. f(a) is defined.
description: Figure 2.32 illustrates that the function f(x) is not continuous at 'a' because f(a) is undefined.
summary: First condition for continuity is that the function value must be defined at the point.

content:
However, as we see in Figure 2.33, this condition alone is insufficient to guarantee continuity at the point a. Although f(a) is defined, the function has a gap at a. In this example, the gap exists because lim f(x) does not exist.
We must add another condition for continuity at a—namely,
ii. lim f(x) exists.
description: Figure 2.33 shows that even if f(a) is defined, there may still be a gap in the graph, indicating discontinuity.
summary: A second condition for continuity at a point is that the limit must exist.

content: A function f(x) is continuous at a point a if and only if the following three conditions are satisfied: i. f(a) is defined ii. lim f(x) exists iii. lim f(x) = f(a)
summary: This chunk outlines the definition of continuity for a function at a point.

content: However, as we see in Figure 2.34, these two conditions by themselves do not guarantee continuity at a point. The function in this figure satisfies both of our first two conditions, but is still not continuous at a. We must add a third condition to our list: iii. lim f(x) = f(a).
description: Figure 2.34 illustrates a function that satisfies the first two conditions for continuity but fails the third condition, demonstrating it is not continuous at point a.
summary: This chunk delves into the limitations of the first two continuity conditions, emphasizing the need for a third condition.

content: A function is discontinuous at a point a if it fails to be continuous at a.
summary: This chunk defines what it means for a function to be discontinuous at a point.

content: The following procedure can be used to analyze the continuity of a function at a point using this definition.
summary: This chunk introduces a procedure for analyzing the continuity of a function.

content: This OpenStax book is available for free at http://cnx.org/content/col11964/1.12
summary: This chunk provides a reference for accessing the OpenStax textbook for further information.

content:
Problem-Solving Strategy: Determining Continuity at a Point
1. Check to see if f(a) is defined. If f(a) is undefined, we need go no further. The function is not continuous at a. If f(a) is defined, continue to step 2.
2. Compute lim f(x). In some cases, we may need to do this by first computing lim f(x) and lim f(x).
   x→a   x→a−   x→a+
If lim f(x) does not exist (that is, it is not a real number), then the function is not continuous at a and the problem is solved. If lim f(x) exists, then continue to step 3.
   x→a
3. Compare f(a) and lim f(x). If lim f(x) ≠ f(a), then the function is not continuous at a. If lim f(x) = f(a), then the function is continuous at a.
summary: This section outlines a step-by-step strategy to determine the continuity of a function at a point.

header: Applying the Continuity Definition in Practice
content: The next three examples demonstrate how to apply this definition to determine whether a function is continuous at a given point. These examples illustrate situations in which each of the conditions for continuity in the definition succeed or fail.
summary: This section introduces examples that will illustrate the continuity conditions in practice.

content:
Example 2.26
Determining Continuity at a Point, Condition 1
Using the definition, determine whether the function f(x)=(x²−4)/(x−2) is continuous at x=2. Justify the conclusion.
Solution
Let’s begin by trying to calculate f(2). We can see that f(2)=0/0, which is undefined. Therefore, f(x)= (x²−4)/(x−2) is discontinuous at 2 because f(2) is undefined.
summary: This example shows how to analyze the continuity of a function and finds that it is discontinuous at a specific point.

header: Calculating Function Value at a Point
content: Let’s begin by trying to calculate f(3). f(3) = - (3^2) + 4 = -5. Thus, f(3) is defined.
summary: This chunk shows the calculation of the function value at x = 3.

content: Next, we calculate lim f(x). To do this, we must compute lim f(x) as x approaches 3 from the left and the right. lim f(x) from the left: lim f(x) as x approaches 3- = - (3^2) + 4 = -5 and lim f(x) from the right: lim f(x) as x approaches 3+ = 4(3) - 8 = 4.
summary: This section describes the limit calculations necessary to analyze continuity.

content: Therefore, lim f(x) does not exist. Thus, f(x) is not continuous at 3.
description: This section concludes that the function is not continuous due to the limits not existing.
summary: This chunk states the outcome of whether the function is continuous at a specific point.

header: Determining Continuity at a Point, Example 2.28
content: Using the definition, determine whether the function f(x)=⎨ sin x if x≠0 ⎩ 1 if x=0 is continuous at x=0. Solution First, observe that f(0)=1. Next, lim f(x)= lim (sin x/x)=1. Last, compare f(0) and lim f(x). We see that f(0)=1= lim f(x). Since all three of the conditions in the definition of continuity are satisfied, f(x) is continuous at x=0.
summary: This example illustrates the process of determining continuity for a specific function at a point.

content: Using the definition, determine whether the function f(x)=⎨ 2x+1 if x<1 ⎨ 2 if x=1 ⎩ −x+4 if x>1 is continuous at x=1. If the function is not continuous at 1, indicate the condition for continuity at a point that fails to hold.
summary: This example assesses the continuity of a piecewise function at a specified point.

content: By applying the definition of continuity and previously established theorems concerning the evaluation of limits, we can state the following theorem. Theorem 2.8: Continuity of Polynomials and Rational Functions Polynomials and rational functions are continuous at every point in their domains.
summary: This theorem asserts the continuity of polynomial and rational functions across their domains.

content: Proof Previously, we showed that if p(x) and q(x) are polynomials, lim p(x)= p(a) for every polynomial p(x) and x→a p(x) p(a) lim = as long as q(a)≠0. Therefore, polynomials and rational functions are continuous on their domains.
summary: The proof demonstrates the conditions under which polynomials and rational functions maintain continuity.

header: Applying Theorem to Rational Functions, Example 2.29
content: We now apply Continuity of Polynomials and Rational Functions to determine the points at which a given rational function is continuous.
summary: This example shows how to utilize a theorem regarding continuity to analyze rational functions.

content: For what values of x is f(x)= x+1 continuous? The rational function f(x)= x+1 is continuous for every value of x except x=5.
summary: This chunk discusses the continuity of a specific rational function.

header: Exploring Continuity of a Polynomial Function
content: For what values of x is f(x)= 3x^4 − 4x^2 continuous?
summary: This chunk poses a question about the continuity of a polynomial function.

header: Types of Discontinuities and Their Characteristics
content: As we have seen in Example 2.26 and Example 2.27, discontinuities take on several different appearances. We classify the types of discontinuities we have seen thus far as removable discontinuities, infinite discontinuities, or jump discontinuities.
summary: This chunk introduces the classification of different types of discontinuities.

content: Intuitively, a removable discontinuity is a discontinuity for which there is a hole in the graph, a jump discontinuity is a noninfinite discontinuity for which the sections of the function do not meet up, and an infinite discontinuity is a discontinuity located at a vertical asymptote.
description: Figure 2.37 illustrates the differences in these types of discontinuities.
summary: This chunk describes different types of discontinuities along with a reference to a visual figure.

content: These three discontinuities are formally defined as follows: If f(x) is discontinuous at a, then 1. f has a removable discontinuity at a if lim f(x) exists. (Note: When we state that lim f(x) exists, we mean that lim f(x)=L, where L is a real number.) 2. f has a jump discontinuity at a if lim f(x) and lim f(x) both exist, but lim f(x)≠ lim f(x). (Note: When we state that lim f(x) and lim f(x) both exist, we mean that both are real-valued and that neither take on the values ±∞.) 3. f has an infinite discontinuity at a if lim f(x)=±∞ or lim f(x)=±∞.
summary: This chunk provides formal definitions of the three types of discontinuities.

content: In Example 2.26, we showed that f(x)= x −4 is discontinuous at x=2. Classify this discontinuity as removable, jump, or infinite.
summary: This chunk introduces the context of identifying and classifying discontinuities in mathematical functions.

Solution
Earlier, we showed that f is discontinuous at 3 because lim f(x) does not exist. However, since lim f(x)=−5 and lim f(x)=4 both exist, we conclude that the function has a jump discontinuity at 3.

header: Understanding Limits and Discontinuities in Functions
content: discontinuity, we must determine the limit at −1. We see that lim x→−1− (−x+1) = −∞ and lim x→−1+ (−x+1) = +∞. Therefore, the function has an infinite discontinuity at −1.
summary: This section explains how to identify infinite discontinuities in functions.

header: Continuity at a Point: An Example
content: For f(x)={2, x if x≠1; 3 if x=1}, decide whether f is continuous at 1. If f is not continuous at 1, classify the discontinuity as removable, jump, or infinite.
summary: This section presents a function and asks if it is continuous at a certain point.

content: Now that we have explored the concept of continuity at a point, we extend that idea to continuity over an interval. As we develop this idea for different types of intervals, it may be useful to keep in mind the intuitive idea that a function is continuous over an interval if we can use a pencil to trace the function between any two points in the interval without lifting the pencil from the paper.
summary: This section introduces the concept of continuity over an interval and uses a pencil analogy.

header: Continuity from the Right and Left
content: Continuity from the Right and from the Left: A function f(x) is said to be continuous from the right at a if lim x→a+ f(x) = f(a). A function f(x) is said to be continuous from the left at a if lim x→a− f(x) = f(a). A function is continuous over an open interval if it is continuous at every point in the interval.
summary: This explains the criteria for a function to be continuous from both the right and left at a point.

header: Continuity Over Different Types of Intervals
content: A function f(x) is continuous over a closed interval of the form [a, b] if it is continuous at every point in (a, b) and is continuous from the right at a and is continuous from the left at b. Analogously, a function f(x) is continuous over an interval of the form (a, b] if it is continuous over (a, b) and is continuous from the left at b. Continuity over other types of intervals are defined in a similar fashion.
summary: This chunk expands on continuity definitions across closed and open intervals.

content: Requiring that lim x→a+ f(x) = f(a) and lim x→b− f(x) = f(b) ensures that we can trace the graph of the function from the point (a, f(a)) to the point (b, f(b)) without lifting the pencil. If, for example, lim x→a+ f(x) ≠ f(a), we would need to lift our pencil to jump from f(a) to the graph of the rest of the function over (a, b].
summary: This section describes how continuity can be visually represented on a graph.

header: Example on Continuity of a Function
content: Example 2.33: State the interval(s) over which the function f(x)= x²/(x² + 2x) is continuous. Solution: Since f(x)= x²/(x² + 2x) is a rational function, it is continuous at every point in its domain. The domain of f(x) is the set (−∞, −2)∪(−2, 0)∪(0, +∞). Thus, f(x) is continuous over each of the intervals.
summary: This example illustrates how to identify intervals of continuity for a given rational function.
This section introduces the concept of limits and describes intervals relevant to continuous functions.

content:

x→a

x→−2 + x→2 −

content: The Intermediate Value Theorem Functions that are continuous over intervals of the form ⎡a, b⎤, where a and b are real numbers, exhibit many useful properties. Throughout our study of calculus, we will encounter many powerful theorems concerning such functions. The first of these theorems is the Intermediate Value Theorem.
summary: This section introduces the Intermediate Value Theorem, highlighting its relevance to continuous functions.

header: Statement of the Intermediate Value Theorem
content: Theorem 2.11: The Intermediate Value Theorem Let f be continuous over a closed, bounded interval ⎡a, b⎤. If z is any real number between f(a) and f(b), then there is a number c in ⎡a, b⎤ satisfying f(c) = z.
description: Figure 2.38 illustrates the Intermediate Value Theorem’s concept, showing how c exists in the interval.
summary: This section formally presents Theorem 2.11, which identifies conditions under which the Intermediate Value Theorem holds true.

header: Understanding the Intermediate Value Theorem in Chapter 2 | Limits
content: Figure 2.38 There is a number c∈⎡a, b⎤ that satisfies f(c)=z.
description: Figure illustrates the existence of a number c within the interval [a, b] that satisfies the equation f(c)=z.
summary: This figure visually represents the Intermediate Value Theorem.

header: Example of Applying the Intermediate Value Theorem
content: Example 2.36 Application of the Intermediate Value Theorem Show that f(x)= x−cosx has at least one zero. Solution Since f(x)= x−cosx is continuous over (−∞, +∞), it is continuous over any closed interval of the form ⎡a, b⎤. If you can find an interval ⎡a, b⎤ such that f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, you can use the Intermediate Value Theorem to conclude there must be a real number c in (a, b) that satisfies f(c)=0. Note that f(0)=0−cos(0)=−1<0 and f(π/2)=−cos(π/2)=0>0. Using the Intermediate Value Theorem, we can see that there must be a real number c in [0, π/2] that satisfies f(c)=0. Therefore, f(x)= x−cosx has at least one zero.
summary: This example demonstrates the application of the Intermediate Value Theorem to find zeros of a function.

header: Clarifying Conditions for the Intermediate Value Theorem
content: Example 2.37 When Can You Apply the Intermediate Value Theorem? If f(x) is continuous over [0, 2], f(0)>0 and f(2)>0, can we use the Intermediate Value Theorem to conclude that f(x) has no zeros in the interval ⎡0, 2]? Explain.
summary: This example questions the conditions under which the Intermediate Value Theorem can be applied.

header: Understanding the Intermediate Value Theorem in Limits
content: No. The Intermediate Value Theorem only allows us to conclude that we can find a value between f(0) and f(2); it doesn’t allow us to conclude that we can’t find other values. To see this more clearly, consider the function f(x)=(x−1)2. It satisfies f(0)=1>0, f(2)=1>0, and f(1)=0.
summary: The section discusses the limitations of the Intermediate Value Theorem.

header: Application of the Intermediate Value Theorem with Function Example

content:
Chapter 2 | Limits
2.5 | The Precise Definition of a Limit
Learning Objectives
2.5.1 Describe the epsilon-delta definition of a limit.
2.5.2 Apply the epsilon-delta definition to find the limit of a function.
2.5.3 Describe the epsilon-delta definitions of one-sided limits and infinite limits.
2.5.4 Use the epsilon-delta definition to prove the limit laws.
summary: Introduction to the section's learning objectives related to limits.

content: Bynowyouhaveprogressedfromtheveryinformaldefinitionofalimitintheintroductionofthischaptertotheintuitive understanding of a limit. At this point, you should have a very strong intuitive sense of what the limit of a function means and how you can find it. In this section, we convert this intuitive idea of a limit into a formal definition using precise mathematical language.
summary: Transition from intuitive understanding of limits to formal mathematical definitions.

content: The formal definition of a limit is quite possibly one of the most challenging definitions you will encounter early in your study of calculus; however, it is well worth any effort you make to reconcile it with your intuitive notion of a limit. Understanding this definition is the key that opens the door to a better understanding of calculus.
summary: Emphasizing the significance of mastering the formal definition of limits.

content:
Quantifying Closeness
Before stating the formal definition of a limit, we must introduce a few preliminary ideas. Recall that the distance between two points a and b on a number line is given by |a−b|. • The statement |f(x)−L|<ε may be interpreted as: The distance between f(x) and L is less than ε. • The statement 0<|x−a|<δ may be interpreted as: x≠a and the distance between x and a is less than δ.
summary: Introduction to the concept of quantifying closeness in the context of limits.

content: It is also important to look at the following equivalences for absolute value: • The statement |f(x)−L|<ε is equivalent to the statement L−ε< f(x)<L+ε. • The statement 0<|x−a|<δ is equivalent to the statement a−δ< x<a+δ and x≠a.
summary: Defining important equivalences related to absolute values within the limits context.

content: With these clarifications, we can state the formal epsilon-delta definition of the limit. Definition Let f(x) be defined for all x≠a over an open interval containing a. Let L be a real number. Then lim f(x)=L if, for every ε>0, there exists a δ>0, such that if 0<|x−a|<δ, then |f(x)−L|<ε.
summary: Stating the official formal epsilon-delta definition of a limit.

content: This definition may seem rather complex from a mathematical point of view, but it becomes easier to understand if we break it down phrase by phrase. The statement itself involves something called a universal quantifier (for every ε>0), an existential quantifier (there exists a δ>0), and, last, a conditional statement (if 0<|x−a|<δ, then |f(x)−L|<ε).
summary: Explanation of the complexity and structure of the formal limit definition.

content: Let’s take a look at Table 2.9, which breaks down the definition and translates each part.
description: Table 2.9 provides a breakdown of the formal epsilon-delta definition of a limit, translating each component for clarity.
summary: Introducing a table that aids in understanding the components of the limit definition.

content:
Definition Translation
1. For every ε>0, 
2. there exists a δ>0,
3. such that
4. if 0<|x−a|<δ, then
|f(x)−L|<ε.
summary: This chunk defines the mathematical translation of the epsilon-delta concept in limits.

Example 2.39

content: Solution PAGE: 204
summary: This section refers to the solution page for the example limit proof introduced earlier.

header: Definition of Limits and ε-δ Explanation
content:
Let ε>0.
The first part of the definition begins “For every ε>0.” This means we must prove that whatever follows is true no matter what positive value of ε is chosen. By stating “Let ε>0,” we signal our intent to do so.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of ε in the context of limits.

content: Choose δ= ε/2. The definition continues with “there exists a δ>0.” The phrase “there exists” in a mathematical statement is always a signal for a scavenger hunt. In other words, we must go and find δ. So, where exactly did δ=ε/2 come from? There are two basic approaches to tracking down δ. One method is purely algebraic and the other is geometric.
summary: This chunk discusses the introduction of δ and the methods to calculate it.

content: We begin by tackling the problem from an algebraic point of view. Since ultimately we want |(2x+1)−3|<ε, we begin by manipulating this expression: |(2x+1)−3|<ε is equivalent to |2x−2|<ε, which in turn is equivalent to |2||x−1|<ε. Last, this is equivalent to |x−1|<ε/2. Thus, it would seem that δ=ε/2 is appropriate.
summary: This chunk explains how to derive δ through algebraic manipulation.

content: We may also find δ through geometric methods. Figure 2.40 demonstrates how this is done.
description: Figure 2.40 shows a graph illustrating the geometric approach to finding δ.
summary: This chunk introduces the geometric method to find δ.

content: Assume 0<|x−1|<δ. When δ has been chosen, our goal is to show that if 0<|x−1|<δ, then |(2x+1)−3|<ε. To prove any statement of the form 'If this, then that,' we begin by assuming 'this' and trying to get 'that.'
summary: This chunk discusses the approach to proving the limit statement using assumptions.

content: Thus, |(2x+1)−3| =|2x−2| property of absolute value =|2(x−1)| =|2||x−1| |2|=2 =2|x−1| <2·δ here’s where we use the assumption that 0<|x−1|<δ ε =2· =ε here’s where we use our choice of δ=ε/2 2
summary: This chunk provides a detailed step-by-step proof using absolute values to demonstrate the limit.

content:
In this part of the proof, we started with |(2x+1)−3| and used our assumption 0<|x−1|<δ in a key part of the chain of inequalities to get |(2x+1)−3| to be less than ε. We could just as easily have manipulated the assumed inequality 0<|x−1|<δ to arrive at |(2x+1)−3|<ε as follows:
0<|x−1|<δ ⇒|x−1|<δ
⇒ −δ< x−1<δ
⇒ −ε<2x−2<ε
⇒|2x−2|<ε
⇒|(2x+1)−3|<ε. Therefore, lim (2x+1)=3. (Having completed the proof, we state what we have accomplished.)
summary: This chunk explains the initial part of a proof regarding limits.

content:
After removing all the remarks, here is a final version of the proof:
Let ε>0.
Choose δ=ε/2.
Assume 0<|x−1|<δ.
Thus,
|(2x+1)−3| =|2x−2|
=|2(x−1)|
=|2||x−1|
=2|x−1|
<2·δ
=2·(ε/2)
=ε.
Therefore, lim (2x+1)=3.
summary: This chunk presents the final version of the proof demonstrating the limit.

header: Problem-Solving Strategy for Proving Limits - Chapter 2
content:
The following Problem-Solving Strategy summarizes the type of proof we worked out in Example 2.39.
Problem-Solving Strategy: Proving That lim f(x)=L for a Specific Function f(x)
1. Let’s begin the proof with the following statement: Let ε>0.
2. Next, we need to obtain a value for δ. After we have obtained this value, we make the following statement, filling in the blank with our choice of δ: Choose δ=_______.
3. The next statement in the proof should be (at this point, we fill in our given value for a): Assume 0<|x−a|<δ.
4. Next, based on this assumption, we need to show that |f(x)−L|<ε, where f(x) and L are our function f(x) and our limit L. At some point, we need to use 0<|x−a|<δ.
5. We conclude our proof with the statement: Therefore, lim f(x)=L.
summary: This chunk outlines a problem-solving strategy for proving limits.

header: Geometric Approach to Limits - Proving a Statement
content:
Prove that lim x 2 =4.
x→2
Solution
1. Let ε>0. The first part of the definition begins “For every ε>0,” so we must prove that whatever follows is true no matter what positive value of ε is chosen. By stating “Let ε>0,” we signal our intent to do so.
2. Without loss of generality, assume ε≤4. Two questions present themselves: Why do we want ε≤4 and why is it okay to make this assumption? In answer to the first question: Later on, in the process of solving for δ, we will discover that δ involves the quantity 4−ε. Consequently, we need ε≤4. In answer to the second question: If we can find δ>0 that “works” for ε≤4, then it will “work” for any ε>4 as well. Keep in mind that, although it is always okay to put an upper bound on ε, it is never okay to put a lower bound (other than zero) on ε.
summary: This section introduces the proof of a limit using the epsilon-delta definition.

header: Choosing Delta in Limit Proof - Key Step
content:

header: Assumption in Limit Proof - Establishing Conditions
content: 4. We must show: If 0<|x−2|<δ, then |x 2 −4|<ε, so we must begin by assuming 0<|x−2|<δ. We don’t really need 0<|x−2| (in other words, x≠2) for this proof. Since 0<|x−2|<δ⇒|x−2|<δ, it is okay to drop 0<|x−2|. |x−2|<δ. Hence, −δ< x−2<δ. Recall that δ=min⎧ ⎨2− 4−ε, 4+ε−2⎫ ⎬. Thus, δ≤2− 4−ε and consequently ⎩ ⎭ −⎛2− 4−ε⎞≤ −δ. We also use δ≤ 4+ε−2 here.

header: Limit Proofs - Geometric vs Algebraic Approaches
content: The geometric approach to proving that the limit of a function takes on a specific value works quite well for some functions. Also, the insight into the formal definition of the limit that this method provides is invaluable. However, we may also approach limit proofs from a purely algebraic point of view. In many cases, an algebraic approach may not only provide us additional insight into the definition, it may prove to be simpler as well. Furthermore, an algebraic approach is the primary tool used in proofs of statements about limits.
summary: Comparison between geometric and algebraic methods for proving limits.

header: Example of Algebraic Limit Proof - Specific Function Limits
content:
For Example 2.42, we take on a purely algebraic approach.
Example 2.42
Proving a Statement about the Limit of a Specific Function (Algebraic Approach)
Prove that lim ⎛ x 2 −2x+3 ⎞ =6.
⎝ ⎠
x→−1
summary: Introduction to an example that uses an algebraic approach to prove a limit.

header: Solution Outline for Limit Proof - Step by Step Process
content:
Solution
Let’s use our outline from the Problem-Solving Strategy:
1. Let ε>0.
2. Choose δ=min{1, ε/5}. This choice of δ may appear odd at first glance, but it was obtained by
summary: Step-by-step outline for solving the limit proof using ε and δ.

content: Taking a look at our ultimate desired inequality: |⎛ x 2 −2x+3 ⎞ −6|<ε. This inequality is equivalent to |x+1|·|x−3|<ε. At this point, the temptation simply to choose δ= ε is very strong.
summary: Introduction to the desired inequality in limit analysis.

content: Unfortunately, our choice of δ must depend on ε only and no other variable. If we can replace |x−3| by a numerical value, our problem can be resolved. This is the place where assuming δ≤1 comes into play.
summary: Highlighting the need for delta to depend solely on epsilon.

content: The choice of δ≤1 here is arbitrary. We could have just as easily used any other positive number. In some proofs, greater care in this choice may be necessary.
summary: Explaining the arbitrary nature of choosing delta.

content: Now, since δ≤1 and |x+1|<δ≤1, we are able to show that |x−3|<5. Consequently, |x+1|·|x−3|<|x+1|·5. At this point we realize that we also need δ≤ε/5. Thus, we choose δ=min{1, ε/5}.
summary: How to derive inequalities using delta and epsilon.

content: Assume 0<|x+1|<δ. Thus, |x+1|<1 and |x+1|<ε/5. Since |x+1|<1, we may conclude that −1< x+1<1. Thus, by subtracting 4 from all parts of the inequality, we obtain −5< x−3<−1. Consequently, |x−3|<5. This gives us |⎛ x 2 −2x+3 ⎞ −6|=|x+1|·|x−3|<5 ε ·5=ε.
summary: Assuming values to establish a conclusion in the limit.

content: Therefore, lim ⎛ 2 ⎞ x 2 −2x+3 =6.
summary: Concluding the limit value from previous derivations.

content: Complete the proof that lim x 2 =1. Let ε>0; choose δ=min{1, ε/3}; assume 0<|x−1|<δ. Since |x−1|<1, we may conclude that −1< x−1<1. Thus, 1< x+1<3. Hence, |x+1|<3.
summary: Instructions for completing a limit proof.

content: You will find that, in general, the more complex a function, the more likely it is that the algebraic approach is the easiest to apply. The algebraic approach is also more useful in proving statements about limits.
summary: Discussing the ease of algebraic methods with complex functions.

content: We now demonstrate how to use the epsilon-delta definition of a limit to construct a rigorous proof of one of the limit laws. The triangle inequality is used at a key point of the proof, so we first review this key property of absolute value.
summary: Introduction to limit laws and their proofs.

header: Proving Limit Laws - Triangle Inequality Definition
content: The triangle inequality states that if a and b are any real numbers, then |a+b|≤|a|+|b|.
summary: Definition and importance of the triangle inequality.

header: Proving Limit Laws - Limit Law Proof
content: We prove the following limit law: If lim f(x)=L and lim g(x)=M, then lim ⎛f(x)+g(x)⎞=L+M. Let ε>0. Choose δ >0 so that if 0<|x−a|<δ, then |f(x)−L|<ε/2. Choose δ >0 so that if 0<|x−a|<δ, then |g(x)−M|<ε/2.
summary: Proving a limit law using the epsilon-delta method.

header: Understanding Limits through Definitions and Opposites
content: Choose δ=min{δ1, δ2}. Assume 0<|x−a|<δ. Thus, 0<|x−a|<δ and 0<|x−a|<δ1 2. Hence, |(f(x)+g(x))−(L+M)| =|(f(x)−L)+(g(x)−M)| ≤|f(x)−L|+|g(x)−M| < ε/2 + ε/2 =ε.
summary: This chunk introduces the epsilon-delta definition of limits.

content: We now explore what it means for a limit not to exist. The limit lim f(x) does not exist if there is no real number L for which lim f(x)=L. Thus, for all real numbers L, lim f(x)≠L. To understand what this means, we look at each part of the definition of lim f(x)=L together with its opposite.
summary: This section discusses the conditions under which a limit does not exist.

header: Definitions and Their Opposites in Limit Theory
content: A translation of the definition is given in Table 2.10. Definition: 1. For every ε>0, 1. There exists ε>0 so that 2. there exists a δ>0, so that 2. for every δ>0, 3. if 0<|x−a|<δ, then 3. There is an x satisfying 0<|x−a|<δ so that |f(x)−L|<ε. |f(x)−L|≥ε.
description: Table 2.10 presents the translation of the definition of limits and their opposite, clarifying the conceptual differences.
summary: This chunk outlines a table that translates the definition of limits and its opposite.

header: Summary of the Non-existence of Limits
content: Finally, we may state what it means for a limit not to exist. The limit lim f(x) does not exist if for every real number L, there exists a real number ε>0 so that for all δ>0, there is an x satisfying 0<|x−a|<δ, so that |f(x)−L|≥ε.
summary: This concludes the discussion on the non-existence of limits.

content: Let's apply this in Example 2.43 to show that a limit does not exist. Example 2.43: Show that lim |x|/x does not exist. The graph of f(x)=|x|/x is shown here:
description: The graph illustrates the function f(x)=|x|/x, demonstrating the point at which the limit does not exist.
summary: This chunk introduces an example to illustrate the non-existence of a limit.

content: Suppose that L is a candidate for a limit. Choose ε=1/2. Let δ>0. Either L≥0 or L<0. If L≥0, then let x= −δ/2. Thus, |x−0|=|−δ/2−0|=δ/2 and |−δ/2−L| =|−1−L|=L+1≥1>1=ε.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of limits, specifying how to choose ε and δ related to a candidate limit L.

content: On the other hand, if L<0, then let x=δ/2. Thus, |x−0|=|δ/2−0|=δ/2 and |δ/2−L| =|1−L|=|L|+1≥1>1=ε.
summary: This section continues the analysis of limits by considering cases where L is less than zero.

content: Thus, for any value of L, lim |x| ≠ L.
summary: This chunk summarizes that for any chosen L, the limit of |x| does not equate to L.

content: One-Sided and Infinite Limits Just as we first gained an intuitive understanding of limits and then moved on to a more rigorous definition of a limit, we now revisit one-sided limits. To do this, we modify the epsilon-delta definition of a limit to give formal epsilon-delta definitions for limits from the right and left at a point.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of one-sided limits and their significance in the context of the epsilon-delta definition.

header: Modifying Definitions for Right and Left Limits
content: These definitions only require slight modifications from the definition of the limit. In the definition of the limit from the right, the inequality 0 < x−a < δ replaces 0 < |x−a| < δ, which ensures that we only consider values of x that are greater than (to the right of) a. Similarly, in the definition of the limit from the left, the inequality −δ < x−a < 0 replaces 0 < |x−a| < δ, which ensures that we only consider values of x that are less than (to the left of) a.
summary: This section elaborates on the modifications needed for establishing the definitions of limits from the right and left.

header: Formal Definition of Limit from the Right
content: Definition Limit from the Right: Let f(x) be defined over an open interval of the form (a, b) where a < b.
summary: This chunk presents a formal definition of the limit from the right in the context of functions defined over open intervals.

header: Understanding Infinite Limits and Their Formal Definition
content: pursuing a formal definition of infinite limits. To have lim f(x)=+∞, we want the values of the function f(x) to get larger and larger as x approaches a. Instead of the requirement that |f(x)−L|<ε for arbitrarily small ε when 0<|x−a|<δ for small enough δ, we want f(x)>M for arbitrarily large positive M when 0<|x−a|<δ for small enough δ.
summary: This section introduces the concept of infinite limits and the criteria for defining them.

header: Illustration of Infinite Limits with Figure 2.43
content: Figure 2.43 illustrates this idea by showing the value of δ for successively larger values of M.
description: Figure 2.43 shows the graphs plotting values of δ for M to demonstrate the concept of lim f(x)=+∞.
summary: The figure visually represents the relationship between δ and M in the context of infinite limits.

content: Let f(x) be defined for all x≠a in an open interval containing a. Then, we have an infinite limit lim f(x)=+∞ if for every M >0, there exists δ>0 such that if 0<|x−a|<δ, then f(x)>M.
summary: This section formally defines what constitutes a positive infinite limit in mathematical terms.

content: Let f(x) be defined for all x≠a in an open interval containing a. Then, we have a negative infinite limit lim f(x)=−∞ if for every M >0, there exists δ>0 such that if 0<|x−a|<δ, then f(x)<−M.
summary: This section provides a formal definition for negative infinite limits.

content: Limits are fundamental to calculus and help us understand the behavior of functions as they approach a certain point. They allow us to deal with values that are not necessarily defined within a function by examining what happens as we get closer to that point.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of limits in calculus.

content: There are various types of limits, including one-sided limits and infinite limits. One-sided limits examine the behavior of a function as it approaches a point from one direction, either from the left or the right. Infinite limits assess the behavior of functions as they approach infinity.
summary: This section discusses different types of limits in calculus.

content: A one-sided limit is denoted as lim x→c⁻ f(x) for the left-hand limit and lim x→c⁺ f(x) for the right-hand limit. It allows us to evaluate how a function behaves as it approaches a specific value c from either side.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of one-sided limits.

content: Infinite limits occur when the outputs of a function increase or decrease without bound as the input approaches a certain value. This can indicate vertical asymptotes in the function's graph.
summary: This section clarifies what infinite limits entail in calculus.

content: To evaluate limits, several methods can be employed, such as direct substitution, factored form, or L'Hôpital's Rule. Each method offers unique advantages depending on the complexity of the function involved.
summary: This chunk introduces various methods to evaluate limits.

content: Limits can also be interpreted graphically. The limit of a function can be visualized as the value that the function approaches as the input approaches a specific point, which can be seen on the graph of the function.
description: Figure 2.1 illustrates the approach of a function toward a limit graphically.
summary: This section discusses the graphical interpretation of limits.

content: There are certain laws governing limits that simplify calculations. These laws include the sum law, product law, and quotient law, which allow for limits to be computed in a systematic way.
summary: This chunk outlines the laws that govern the calculation of limits.

content: A function is said to be continuous at a point if the limit of the function at that point equals the function's value at that point. This relationship is central to understanding both limits and continuity.
summary: This section describes the relationship between limits and continuity.

content: average velocity the change in an object’s position divided by the length of a time period; the average velocity of an object over a time interval [t, a] (if t < a or [a, t] if t > a), with a position given by s(t), that is vave = (s(t) - s(a)) / (t - a)
summary: Introduction to average velocity as a fundamental concept in calculus.

content:
constant multiple law for limits the limit law lim cf(x) = c· lim f(x) = cL; x→a x→a

difference law for limits the limit law lim (f(x) - g(x)) = lim f(x) - lim g(x) = L - M; x→a x→a x→a
summary: Discussion of key limit laws and their mathematical implications.

content: continuity at a point A function f(x) is continuous at a point a if and only if the following three conditions are satisfied: (1) f(a) is defined, (2) lim f(x) exists, and (3) lim f(x) = f(a); x→a x→a
summary: Description of the conditions for continuity of functions.

content: continuity from the left A function is continuous from the left at b if lim f(x) = f(b); continuity from the right A function is continuous from the right at a if lim f(x) = f(a); continuity over an interval a function that can be traced with a pencil without lifting the pencil; a function is continuous over an open interval if it is continuous at every point in the interval; a function f(x) is continuous over a closed interval of the form [a, b] if it is continuous at every point in (a, b), and it is continuous from the right at a and from the left at b.
summary: Insights into different aspects of continuity in functions.

content: discontinuity at a point A function is discontinuous at a point or has a discontinuity at a point if it is not continuous at the point.
summary: Definition and implications of discontinuities in functions.

content: epsilon-delta definition of the limit lim f(x) = L if for every ε > 0, there exists a δ > 0 such that if 0 < |x - a| < δ, then |f(x) - L| < ε; x→a
summary: Introduction to the epsilon-delta definition of limits.

content:
infinite discontinuity An infinite discontinuity occurs at a point a if lim f(x) = ±∞ or lim f(x) = ±∞; x→a− x→a+
infinite limit A function has an infinite limit at a point a if it either increases or decreases without bound as it approaches a.
summary: Explanation of infinite discontinuities and infinite limits.

content: instantaneous velocity The instantaneous velocity of an object with a position function that is given by s(t) is the value that the average velocities on intervals of the form [t, a] and [a, t] approach as the values of t move closer to a, provided such a value exists.
summary: Comparison between instantaneous and average velocity in calculus.

content: integral calculus the study of integrals and their applications.
summary: Overview of integral calculus and its significance.

content: Intermediate Value Theorem Let f be continuous over a closed bounded interval [a, b]; if z is any real number between f(a) and f(b), then there is a number c in [a, b] satisfying f(c) = z.
summary: Description of the Intermediate Value Theorem and its implications.

content: intuitive definition of the limit If all values of the function f(x) approach the real number L as the values of x (≠ a) approach a, f(x) approaches L.
summary: An intuitive perspective on limits in calculus.

content: jump discontinuity A jump discontinuity occurs at a point a if lim f(x) and lim f(x) both exist, but lim f(x) ≠ lim f(x); x→a− x→a+
summary: Explanation of jump discontinuities and their characteristics.

content: limit the process of letting x or t approach a in an expression; the limit of a function f(x) as x approaches a is the value.
summary: Summary of the concept of limits in calculus.

content: that f(x) approaches as x approaches a limit laws the individual properties of limits; for each of the individual laws, let f(x) and g(x) be defined for all x≠a over some open interval containing a; assume that L and M are real numbers so that lim f(x)=L and lim g(x)=M; let c be a constant
summary: This section introduces limit laws and their properties in the context of calculus.

header: Introduction to One-Sided Limits and Multivariable Calculus
content: multivariable calculus the study of the calculus of functions of two or more variables one-sided limit A one-sided limit of a function is a limit taken from either the left or the right
summary: This section discusses the basics of one-sided limits and outlines multivariable calculus.

header: Limit Laws for Evaluation of Limits
content: power law for limits n the limit law lim ⎛f(x)⎞n = ⎛ lim f(x) ⎞ =L n for every positive integer n x→a⎝ ⎠ ⎝x→a ⎠
summary: This chunk specifies the power law of limits for evaluating functions.

header: Product and Quotient Laws in Limit Analysis
content: product law for limits the limit law lim ⎛f(x)·g(x)⎞= lim f(x)· lim g(x)=L·M x→a⎝ ⎠ x→a x→a
summary: Here, the product law for limits is introduced along with the subsequent law.

content: removable discontinuity A removable discontinuity occurs at a point a if f(x) is discontinuous at a, but lim f(x) exists
summary: This section defines removable discontinuities in the context of function limits.

content: root law for limits the limit law lim n f(x)= n lim f(x)= n L for all L if n is odd and for L≥0 if n is even x→a x→a
summary: This chunk discusses the root law applicable when evaluating limits.

content: secant A secant line to a function f(x) at a is a line through the point ⎛a, f(a)⎞ and another point on the function; the slope of the secant line is given by msec= f(x)− f(a)/x−a
summary: The concept of secant lines and their significance in slope calculation is explained.

content: squeeze theorem states that if f(x)≤g(x)≤h(x) for all x≠a over an open interval containing a and lim f(x)=L= lim h(x) where L is a real number, then lim g(x)=L
summary: This chunk details the Squeeze Theorem and its relevance in evaluating limits.

content: sum law for limits The limit law lim ⎛f(x)+g(x)⎞= lim f(x)+ lim g(x)=L+M x→a⎝ ⎠ x→a x→a
summary: The sum law for limits is presented with an equation format for further comprehension.

content: tangent A tangent line to the graph of a function at a point ⎛a, f(a)⎞ is the line that secant lines through ⎛a, f(a)⎞ approach as they are taken through points on the function with x-values that approach a; the slope of the tangent line to a graph at a measures the rate of change of the function at a
summary: The relationship between tangents, secant lines, and the rate of change in calculus is explained.

content: triangle inequality If a and b are any real numbers, then |a+b|≤|a|+|b|
summary: The triangle inequality principle in the context of real numbers is introduced.

content: vertical asymptote A function has a vertical asymptote at x=a if the limit as x approaches a from the right or left is infinite
summary: This section provides clarity on the idea of vertical asymptotes in functions.

content: KEY EQUATIONS • Slope of a Secant Line f(x)− f(a)/(x−a) • Average Velocity over Interval [a, t] s(t)−s(a)/(t−a) • Intuitive Definition of the Limit lim f(x)=L • Two Important Limits lim x=a lim c=c • One-Sided Limits
summary: This section outlines key equations that assist in limit evaluation and understanding.

KEY CONCEPTS
2.1A Preview of Calculus
• Differential calculus arose from trying to solve the problem of determining the slope of a line tangent to a curve at a point. The slope of the tangent line indicates the rate of change of the function, also called the derivative. Calculating a derivative requires finding a limit.
• Integral calculus arose from trying to solve the problem of finding the area of a region between the graph of a function and the x-axis. We can approximate the area by dividing it into thin rectangles and summing the areas of these rectangles. This summation leads to the value of a function called the integral. The integral is also calculated by finding a limit and, in fact, is related to the derivative of a function.
• Multivariable calculus enables us to solve problems in three-dimensional space, including determining motion in space and finding volumes of solids.
summary: This segment introduces key calculus concepts, focusing on differential and integral calculus.

content:
2.2 The Limit of a Function
• A table of values or graph may be used to estimate a limit.
• If the limit of a function at a point does not exist, it is still possible that the limits from the left and right at that point may exist.
• If the limits of a function from the left and right exist and are equal, then the limit of the function is that common value.
• We may use limits to describe infinite behavior of a function at a point.
summary: This section explains how to determine the limits of a function using different methods.

content:
2.3 The Limit Laws
• The limit laws allow us to evaluate limits of functions without having to go through step-by-step processes each time.
• For polynomials and rational functions, lim f(x)= f(a).
x→a
summary: This section discusses the limit laws that simplify the evaluation of limits.

content: You can evaluate the limit of a function by factoring and canceling, by multiplying by a conjugate, or by simplifying a complex fraction.
summary: This chunk discusses methods for evaluating limits of functions.

content: The squeeze theorem allows you to find the limit of a function if the function is always greater than one function and less than another function with limits that are known.
summary: The squeeze theorem helps evaluate limits based on bounding functions.

content: For a function to be continuous at a point, it must be defined at that point, its limit must exist at the point, and the value of the function at that point must equal the value of the limit at that point.
summary: Continuity at a point requires specific conditions related to limits and definitions.

content: Discontinuities may be classified as removable, jump, or infinite.
summary: This section categorizes the different types of discontinuities in functions.

content: A function is continuous over an open interval if it is continuous at every point in the interval. It is continuous over a closed interval if it is continuous at every point in its interior and is continuous at its endpoints.
summary: Conditions of continuity are outlined for open and closed intervals.

content: The composite function theorem states: If f(x) is continuous at L and lim g(x)=L, then lim f(g(x))= f(L).
summary: This theorem describes continuity in the context of composite functions.

content: The Intermediate Value Theorem guarantees that if a function is continuous over a closed interval, then the function takes on every value between the values at its endpoints.
summary: This theorem confirms that continuous functions attain all intermediate values.

content: The intuitive notion of a limit may be converted into a rigorous mathematical definition known as the epsilon-delta definition of the limit.
summary: The epsilon-delta definition formalizes the concept of limits.

content: The epsilon-delta definition may be used to prove statements about limits.
summary: This section discusses using the epsilon-delta definition for limit proofs.

content: The epsilon-delta definition of a limit may be modified to define one-sided limits.
summary: One-sided limits can also be defined using the epsilon-delta approach.

header: Overview of Derivatives and their Significance
content: The Hennessey Venom GT is one of the fastest cars in the world. In 2014, it reached a record-setting speed of 270.49 mph. It can go from 0 to 200 mph in 14.51 seconds. The techniques in this chapter can be used to calculate the acceleration the Venom achieves in this feat (see Example 3.8.) Calculating velocity and changes in velocity are important uses of calculus, but it is far more widespread than that. Calculus is important in all branches of mathematics, science, and engineering, and it is critical to analysis in business and health as...
description: Introduction to the fastest car and the relevance of derivatives.
summary: The introduction highlights the significance of derivatives through the example of a high-speed car.

header: Introduction to Derivatives and Their Importance
content: In this chapter, we explore one of the main tools of calculus, the derivative, and show convenient ways to calculate derivatives. We apply these rules to a variety of functions in this chapter so that we can then explore applications of these techniques.
summary: This section introduces the concept and importance of derivatives in calculus.

content: Learning Objectives 3.1.1 Recognize the meaning of the tangent to a curve at a point. 3.1.2 Calculate the slope of a tangent line. 3.1.3 Identify the derivative as the limit of a difference quotient. 3.1.4 Calculate the derivative of a given function at a point. 3.1.5 Describe the velocity as a rate of change. 3.1.6 Explain the difference between average velocity and instantaneous velocity. 3.1.7 Estimate the derivative from a table of values.
summary: This section outlines the concrete learning objectives for the study of derivatives.

content: Now that we have both a conceptual understanding of a limit and the practical ability to compute limits, we have established the foundation for our study of calculus, the branch of mathematics in which we compute derivatives and integrals. Most mathematicians and historians agree that calculus was developed independently by the Englishman Isaac Newton (1643–1727) and the German Gottfried Leibniz (1646–1716), whose images appear in Figure 3.2.
summary: This section provides historical context regarding the development of calculus and its founders.

header: The Contribution of Newton and Leibniz
content: When we credit Newton and Leibniz with developing calculus, we are really referring to the fact that Newton and Leibniz were the first to understand the relationship between the derivative and the integral. Both mathematicians benefited from the work of predecessors, such as Barrow, Fermat, and Cavalieri.
summary: This section discusses the contributions of Newton and Leibniz toward the understanding of calculus.

content: The initial relationship between the two mathematicians appears to have been amicable; however, in later years a bitter controversy erupted over whose work took precedence. Although it seems likely that Newton did, indeed, arrive at the ideas behind calculus first, we are indebted to Leibniz for the notation that we commonly use today.
summary: This section narrates the controversy surrounding the priority of calculus developments between Newton and Leibniz.

description: Figure 3.2 shows the images of Isaac Newton and Gottfried Leibniz, credited with the independent development of calculus.
summary: This section references a figure illustrating the key figures in calculus.

header: Introduction to Tangent and Secant Lines in Calculus
content: We begin our study of calculus by revisiting the notion of secant lines and tangent lines. Recall that we used the slope of a secant line to a function at a point (a, f(a)) to estimate the rate of change, or the rate at which one variable changes in relation to another variable.
summary: Introduction to the concepts of tangent and secant lines in the context of calculus.

content: We can obtain the slope of the secant by choosing a value of x near a and drawing a line through the points (a, f(a)) and (x, f(x)), as shown in Figure 3.3. The slope of this line is given by an equation in the form of a difference quotient: f(x)− f(a) / x−a.
description: Figure 3.3 illustrates the slope of the secant line calculated using points (a, f(a)) and (x, f(x)).
summary: Explanation of how to calculate the slope of secant lines using a difference quotient.

header: Alternative Method for Secant Line Slope Calculation
content: We can also calculate the slope of a secant line to a function at a value a by using this equation and replacing x with a+h, where h is a value close to 0. We can then calculate the slope of the line through the points (a, f(a)) and (a+h, f(a+h)). In this case, we find the secant line has a slope given by the following difference quotient with increment h: f(a+h)− f(a) / h.
summary: A method for calculating the slope of a secant line using an increment h.

content: Definition: Let f be a function defined on an interval I containing a. If x≠a is in I, then Q = (f(x)− f(a)) / (x−a) is a difference quotient. Also, if h≠0 is chosen so that a+h is in I, then Q = (f(a+h)− f(a)) / h is a difference quotient with increment h.
summary: Definitions of the difference quotients used for calculating slopes.

content: These two expressions for calculating the slope of a secant line are illustrated in Figure 3.3. We will see that each of these two methods for finding the slope of a secant line is of value. Depending on the setting, we can choose one or the other. The primary consideration in our choice usually depends on ease of calculation.
description: Figure 3.3 illustrates both methods for calculating the slope of a secant line.
summary: Discussion on the methods for calculating the slope of a secant line and their relative ease.

header: Understanding Secant Lines and Tangent Slopes in Derivatives
content: Figure 3.3 We can calculate the slope of a secant line in either of two ways. In Figure 3.4(a) we see that, as the values of x approach a, the slopes of the secant lines provide better estimates of the rate of change of the function at a. Furthermore, the secant lines themselves approach the tangent line to the function at a, which represents the limit of the secant lines. Similarly, Figure 3.4(b) shows that as the values of h get closer to 0, the secant lines also approach the tangent line. The slope of the tangent line at a is the rate of change of the function at a, as shown in Figure 3.4(c).
description: Figure 3.4 illustrates the relationship between secant lines and the tangent line as parameters approach their limits.
summary: This chunk discusses how secant lines approximate tangents to calculate the rate of change.

header: Graphical Representation of Tangents and Local Linearity
content: You can use this site (http://www.openstax.org/l/20_diffmicros) to explore graphs to see if they have a tangent line at a point. In Figure 3.5 we show the graph of f(x) = x and its tangent line at (1, 1) in a series of tighter intervals about x = 1. As the intervals become narrower, the graph of the function and its tangent line appear to coincide, making the values on the tangent line a good approximation to the values of the function for choices of x close to 1. In fact, the graph of f(x) itself appears to be locally linear in the immediate vicinity of x = 1.
description: Figure 3.5 demonstrates the graph of a linear function and its tangent line to illustrate local linearity.
summary: This chunk highlights the application of tangent lines in approximating function values near specific points.

content: For values of x close to 1, the graph of f(x)= x and its tangent line appear to coincide.

header: Formal Definition of the Tangent Line
content: Formally we may define the tangent line to the graph of a function as follows. Definition Let f(x) be a function defined in an open interval containing a. The tangent line to f(x) at a is the line passing through the point ⎛a, f(a)⎞ having slope ⎝ ⎠ f(x)− f(a) (3.3) m = lim tan x→a x−a provided this limit exists.
summary: This chunk provides the formal definition of a tangent line at a point for a function.

header: Alternative Definition of the Tangent Line
content: Equivalently, we may define the tangent line to f(x) at a to be the line passing through the point ⎛a, f(a)⎞ having slope ⎝ ⎠ f(a+h)− f(a) (3.4) m = lim tan h→0 h provided this limit exists.
summary: This chunk presents an alternative way to define the tangent line using limits.

header: Using Different Expressions to Define the Slope of a Tangent Line
content: Just as we have used two different expressions to define the slope of a secant line, we use two different forms to define the slope of the tangent line. In this text we use both forms of the definition. As before, the choice of definition will depend on the setting.
summary: This chunk discusses the dual approaches to defining the slope of the tangent line.

header: Application of the Tangent Line Definition
content: Now that we have formally defined a tangent line to a function at a point, we can use this definition to find equations of tangent lines.
summary: This chunk indicates the applicability of the tangent line definition for practical use.

content: 3.3 For f(x)= x 2 +3x+2, find f′(1). Velocities and Rates of Change Now that we can evaluate a derivative, we can use it in velocity applications. Recall that if s(t) is the position of an object moving along a coordinate axis, the average velocity of the object over a time interval [a, t] if t>a or [t, a] if t<a is given by the difference quotient s(t)−s(a) (3.7) vave = t−a . As the values of t approach a, the values of vave approach the value we call the instantaneous velocity at a. That is, instantaneous velocity at a, denoted v(a), is given by s(t)−s(a) (3.8) v(a)=s′(a)= lim . t→a t−a
summary: This section explores how derivatives can be applied to find average and instantaneous velocity.

content: To better understand the relationship between average velocity and instantaneous velocity, see Figure 3.8. In this figure, the slope of the tangent line (shown in red) is the instantaneous velocity of the object at time t=a whose position at time t is given by the function s(t). The slope of the secant line (shown in green) is the average velocity of the object over the time interval [a, t].
description: Figure 3.8 illustrates the relationship between instantaneous and average velocity using tangent and secant lines to represent their respective slopes.
summary: This section discusses a figure that visually compares instantaneous and average velocities through slopes of lines.

header: Understanding Average and Instantaneous Velocity in Derivatives
content: The slope of the secant line is the average velocity over the interval [a, t]. The slope of the tangent line is the instantaneous velocity.
summary: This section explains the difference between average and instantaneous velocity.

content: We can use Equation 3.5 to calculate the instantaneous velocity, or we can estimate the velocity of a moving object by using a table of values. We can then confirm the estimate by using Equation 3.7.
summary: This section provides methods for calculating or estimating instantaneous velocity.

A lead weight on a spring is oscillating up and down. Its position at time t with respect to a fixed horizontal line is given by s(t)=sin(t) (Figure 3.9). Use a table of values to estimate v(0). Check the estimate by using Equation 3.5.

header: Application of Derivatives in Motion Problems
content: A rock is dropped from a height of 64 feet. Its height above ground at time t seconds later is given by s(t)=−16t² +64, 0≤t≤2. Find its instantaneous velocity 1 second after it is dropped, using Equation 3.5.
summary: This chunk introduces a practical application of derivatives in finding the rock's instantaneous velocity.

header: Connecting Tangent Slope and Instantaneous Velocity
content: As we have seen throughout this section, the slope of a tangent line to a function and instantaneous velocity are related concepts. Each is calculated by computing a derivative and each measures the instantaneous rate of change of a function, or the rate of change of a function at any point along the function.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the relationship between tangent lines and instantaneous velocity.

header: Definition of Instantaneous Rate of Change
content: The instantaneous rate of change of a function f(x) at a value a is its derivative f′(a).
summary: This section defines the concept of instantaneous rate of change in relation to derivatives.

Find the rate of change of profit when 10,000 games are produced. Should the toy company increase or decrease production?
summary: Introduction to the profit analysis based on sales pricing and manufacturing costs.

content: The profit P(x) earned by producing x gaming systems is R(x)−C(x), where R(x) is the revenue obtained from the sale of x games. Since the company can sell x games at p=−0.01x+400 per game, R(x)= xp= x(−0.01x+400)=−0.01x^2 +400x.
summary: Explanation on how to derive the revenue function based on selling price.

header: Profit Function Derived from Revenue and Costs
content: Consequently, P(x)=−0.01x^2 +300x−10,000.

header: Evaluating the Rate of Change of Profit
content: Therefore, evaluating the rate of change of profit gives P'(10,000) = lim (x→10,000) ((P(x)−P(10,000))/(x−10,000)), which simplifies to … = 100.
summary: Process to evaluate the rate of change of profit at a specific production level.

header: Decision Based on Profit Rate of Change
content: Since the rate of change of profit P′(10,000)>0 and P(10,000)>0, the company should increase production.
