content: In the previous exercise, explain why L(A) gets no smaller while U(A) gets no larger as the squares are subdivided into four boxes of equal area.
summary: Examines the effect of subdividing areas into smaller sections.

header: Fundamental Unit Circle Concepts in Integration
content: A unit circle is made up of n wedges equivalent to the inner wedge in the figure. The base of the inner triangle is 1 unit and its height is sin(π/n). The base of the outer triangle is B=cos(π/n)+sin(π/n)tan(π/n) and the height is H =Bsin(2π/n). Use this information to argue that the area of a unit circle is equal to π.
description: The document references a figure illustrating the unit circle and its inner and outer triangles, which is crucial for the discussion on areas.
summary: This chunk discusses the geometric properties of the unit circle and establishes a basis for proving its area.

header: Understanding the Definite Integral in Integration
content:
Learning Objectives
5.2.1 State the definition of the definite integral.
5.2.2 Explain the terms integrand, limits of integration, and variable of integration.
5.2.3 Explain when a function is integrable.
5.2.4 Describe the relationship between the definite integral and net area.
5.2.5 Use geometry and the properties of definite integrals to evaluate them.
5.2.6 Calculate the average value of a function.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to the definite integral concept.

header: Area Under a Curve: Riemann Sums and Generalization
content:
In the preceding section we defined the area under a curve in terms of Riemann sums:
n
A= lim ∑ f⎛x* ⎞Δx.
n→∞ ⎝ i ⎠
i=1
However, this definition came with restrictions. We required f(x) to be continuous and nonnegative. Unfortunately, real-world problems don’t always meet these restrictions. In this section, we look at how to apply the concept of the area under the curve to a broader set of functions through the use of the definite integral.
summary: This chunk discusses the limitations of the Riemann sums definition and introduces the definite integral as a broader tool.

content:
The definite integral generalizes the concept of the area under a curve. We lift the requirements that f(x) be continuous and nonnegative, and define the definite integral as follows.
Definition
If f(x) is a function defined on an interval ⎡a, b⎤, the definite integral of f from a to b is given by
⎣ ⎦
b n (5.8)
∫ f(x)dx= lim ∑ f⎛x* ⎞Δx,
n→∞ ⎝ i ⎠
a i=1
provided the limit exists. If this limit exists, the function f(x) is said to be integrable on ⎡a, b⎤, or is an integrable function.
summary: This section provides the formal definition of the definite integral and its criteria for integrability.

content: The integral symbol in the previous definition should look familiar. We have seen similar notation in the chapter on Applications of Derivatives, where we used the indefinite integral symbol (without the a and b above and below) to represent an antiderivative. Although the notation for indefinite integrals may look similar to the notation for a definite integral, they are not the same. A definite integral is a number. An indefinite integral is a family of functions. Later in this chapter we examine how these concepts are related. However, close attention should always be paid to notation so we know whether we’re working with a definite integral or an indefinite integral.
summary: This chunk distinguishes between definite and indefinite integrals based on notation and their meaning.

content: Integral notation goes back to the late seventeenth century and is one of the contributions of Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz, who is often considered to be the co-discoverer of calculus, along with Isaac Newton. The integration symbol ∫ is an elongated S, suggesting sigma or summation. On a definite integral, above and below the summation symbol are the boundaries of the interval, ⎡a, b⎤. The numbers a and b are x-values and are called the limits of integration; specifically, a is the lower limit and b is the upper limit.
summary: This section provides historical insight into the development of integral notation and its meanings.

header: Limits of Integration and Integrands Explained
content:
To clarify, we are using the word limit in two different ways in the context of the definite integral. First, we talk about the limit of a sum as n→∞. Second, the boundaries of the region are called the limits of integration. We call the function f(x) the integrand, and the dx indicates that f(x) is a function with respect to x, called the variable of integration. Note that, like the index in a sum, the variable of integration is a dummy variable and has no impact on the computation of the integral. We could use any variable we like as the variable of integration:
b b b
∫ f(x)dx=∫ f(t)dt=∫ f(u)du
a a a
summary: This chunk explains the concepts of limits of integration, integrands, and the nature of the variable of integration.

header: Integration Overview and Theorem on Continuity
content:
Previously, we discussed the fact that if f(x) is continuous on ⎡a, b⎤, then the limit lim ∑ f⎛x* ⎞Δx exists and is ⎣ ⎦ n→∞ ⎝ i ⎠ i=1 unique. This leads to the following theorem, which we state without proof.
Theorem 5.1:Continuous Functions Are Integrable
If f(x) is continuous on ⎡a, b⎤, then f is integrable on ⎡a, b⎤.
summary: This section introduces the basic principle that continuous functions can be integrated.

content: Functions that are not continuous on ⎡a, b⎤ may still be integrable, depending on the nature of the discontinuities. For example, functions with a finite number of jump discontinuities on a closed interval are integrable.
summary: This section discusses the conditions under which discontinuous functions can still be integrable.

content: It is also worth noting here that we have retained the use of a regular partition in the Riemann sums. This restriction is not strictly necessary. Any partition can be used to form a Riemann sum. However, if a non-regular partition is used to define the definite integral, it is not sufficient to take the limit as the number of subintervals goes to infinity. Instead, we must take the limit as the width of the largest subinterval goes to zero. This introduces a little more complex notation in our limits and makes the calculations more difficult without really gaining much additional insight, so we stick with regular partitions for the Riemann sums.
summary: This section emphasizes the significance of using regular partitions to simplify the computation of Riemann sums.

content:
Example 5.7
Evaluating an Integral Using the Definition
Use the definition of the definite integral to evaluate ∫ x² dx from 0 to 2. Use a right-endpoint approximation to generate the Riemann sum.
summary: This section presents an example problem that applies the definition of the definite integral and Riemann sums.

content:
Solution
We first want to set up a Riemann sum. Based on the limits of integration, we have a=0 and b=2. For i=0, 1, 2,…,n, let P={x} be a regular partition of [0, 2]. Then
Δx = (b-a)/n = (2-0)/n = 2/n. Since we are using a right-endpoint approximation to generate Riemann sums, for each i, we need to calculate the function value at the right endpoint of the interval [x_{i-1}, x_i]. The right endpoint of the interval is x_i, and since P is a regular partition, 
x_i = x_0 + iΔx = 0 + i(2/n) = 2i/n.
summary: This section outlines the procedure for establishing a Riemann sum based on a defined integral.

header: Calculating Function Values in Riemann Sums
content:
Thus, the function value at the right endpoint of the interval is f(x) = x² = (2i/n)² = 4i²/n². Then the Riemann sum takes the form
∑_{i=1}^{n} f(x_i)Δx = ∑_{i=1}^{n} 4i²/n² 
Δx = (2/n) = ∑_{i=1}^{n} 8i²/n². Using the summation formula for ∑ i², we have
∑_{i=1}^{n} i² = n(n + 1)(2n + 1)/6.
summary: This section computes the values of the function at the endpoints needed for the Riemann sum.
This section introduces the summation required to understand the integration process.

content: Now, to calculate the definite integral, we need to take the limit as n→∞. We get 2 n ∫ x 2 dx = lim ∑ f(x)Δx n→∞ i 0 i=1 ⎛ ⎞ 8 4 8 = lim + + n→∞⎝3 n 6n 2⎠ ⎛8⎞ ⎛4⎞ ⎛ 8 ⎞ = lim + lim + lim n→∞⎝3⎠ n→∞⎝n⎠ n→∞⎝ 6n 2⎠ 8 8 = +0+0= . 3 3
summary: This chunk explains the calculation of a definite integral through limits and summation.

summary: This section confirms the relationship between definite integrals and the area they represent.

header: Example of Using Geometric Formulas to Calculate Definite Integrals
content: Example 5.8 Using Geometric Formulas to Calculate Definite Integrals 6 Use the formula for the area of a circle to evaluate ∫ 9−(x−3)2 dx. 3 Solution The function describes a semicircle with radius 3.
description: This chunk references a geometric example that utilizes the area formula of a circle.
summary: An example demonstrates how to evaluate a definite integral using geometric area formulas.

content: ∫ 9−(x−3)2 dx, we want to find the area under the curve over the interval ⎡3, 6⎤. The formula for the area of a circle is A=πr². The area of a semicircle is just one-half the area of a circle, or A= ⎛1⎞ πr². The shaded area in Figure 5.16 covers one-half of the semicircle, or A= ⎛1⎞ πr². Thus, ∫ 9−(x−3)² = 1 π(3)².
description: Figure 5.16 illustrates the integral of the function f(x) over the interval ⎡3, 6⎤ as the area of the shaded region.
summary: This section focuses on the calculation of areas under curves using integration.

header: Evaluating the Definite Integral Using Trapezoids
content: Use the formula for the area of a trapezoid to evaluate ∫ (2x+3)dx.
summary: This chunk introduces using trapezoid area formulas for evaluating definite integrals.

content: When we defined the definite integral, we lifted the requirement that f(x) be non-negative. But how do we interpret 'the area under the curve' when f(x) is negative? Let us return to the Riemann sum. Consider, for example, the function f(x)=2−2x² (shown in Figure 5.17) on the interval [0, 2]. Use n=8 and choose x* as the left endpoint of each interval. Construct a rectangle on each subinterval of height f(x*) and width Δx. When f(x*) is positive, the product f(x*)Δx represents the area of the rectangle, as before. When f(x*) is negative, however, the product f(x*)Δx represents the negative of the area of the rectangle. The Riemann sum then becomes ∑ f(x*)Δx=⎛Area of rectangles above the x-axis⎞−⎛Area of rectangles below the x-axis⎞.
description: Figure 5.17 depicts the function f(x) = 2 - 2x², illustrating the concept of Riemann sums with negative values.
summary: This section explores the concept of net signed area and how to handle negative values in integration.

header: Understanding Riemann Sums and Net Signed Area in Integration
content: Figure 5.17 For a function that is partly negative, the Riemann sum is the area of the rectangles above the x-axis less the area of the rectangles below the x-axis.
description: Figure 5.17 illustrates how Riemann sums account for both positive and negative areas in a function's integration by visually representing the rectangles above and below the x-axis.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of Riemann sums and their relation to net signed area in integration.

header: Limiting Behavior of Riemann Sums and Net Area
content: Taking the limit as n→∞, the Riemann sum approaches the area between the curve above the x-axis and the x-axis, less the area between the curve below the x-axis and the x-axis, as shown in Figure 5.18.
description: This statement leads towards the mathematical understanding of integration as approaching net area as the number of rectangles increases indefinitely.
summary: This chunk discusses taking limits in Riemann sums to define the integral more precisely.

content: Then, 2 n∫ f(x)dx = lim ∑ f(c)Δx, as n→∞, where i=1 to n = A − A.
summary: This chunk presents the formal equation that defines the definite integral using Riemann sums.

header: Concept of Net Signed Area Explained
content: The quantity A − A is called the net signed area. Notice that net signed area can be positive, negative, or zero.
summary: This chunk explains what net signed area means in the context of integration.

content: If the area above the x-axis is larger, the net signed area is positive. If the area below the x-axis is larger, the net signed area is negative. If the areas above and below the x-axis are equal, the net signed area is zero.
summary: This chunk interprets the implications of the values of net signed area.

header: Example for Net Signed Area Calculation
content: Example 5.9 Finding the Net Signed Area
summary: This chunk introduces an example that will demonstrate the calculation of net signed area.

header: Integration of the Function f(x) = 2x - Finding Net Signed Area
content:
Find the net signed area between the curve of the function f(x)=2x and the x-axis over the interval [−3, 3].
Solution
The function produces a straight line that forms two triangles: one from x=−3 to x=0 and the other from x=0 to x=3 (Figure 5.19).
description: Figure 5.19 shows the area above the curve and below the x-axis, which equals the area below the curve and above the x-axis.
summary: The section outlines the steps to find the net signed area for the function f(x) = 2x over a specified interval.

header: Calculating Area of Triangles in Integration
content: Using the geometric formula for the area of a triangle, A= 1/2 bh, the area of triangle A1, above the axis, is A1 = 1/2 (3)(6) = 9, where 3 is the base and 2(3)=6 is the height. The area of triangle A2, below the axis, is A2 = 1/2 (3)(6)=9, where 3 is the base and 6 is the height.
summary: This section provides the calculations for the areas of triangles formed by the integration of the function.

content: Thus, the net area is 3∫ 2xdx= A1 − A2 = 9 − 9 = 0.
summary: The section states the final result of the net area calculation based on the areas of the triangles.

header: Analysis of Areas Above and Below the Axis
content: If A1 is the area above the x-axis and A2 is the area below the x-axis, then the net area is A1 − A2. Since the areas of the two triangles are equal, the net area is zero.
summary: This section analyzes the relationship between the areas above and below the x-axis, concluding that they cancel each other out.

content: Total Area One application of the definite integral is finding displacement when given a velocity function. If v(t) represents the...

header: Understanding Displacement with Constant Velocity - Integration
content: velocity of an object as a function of time, then the area under the curve tells us how far the object is from its original position. This is a very important application of the definite integral, and we examine it in more detail later in the chapter. For now, we’re just going to look at some basics to get a feel for how this works by studying constant velocities.
summary: This section introduces the concept of displacement in relation to definite integrals and constant velocities.

header: Calculating Distance for Constant Velocity - Integration
content: When velocity is constant, the area under the curve is just velocity times time. This idea is already very familiar. If a car travels away from its starting position in a straight line at a speed of 75mph for 2 hours, then it is 150 miles away from its original position.
description: Figure 5.20 illustrates how the area under the curve v(t)=75 represents the distance traveled by the car from its starting point over time.
summary: This chunk explains how to calculate distance using the area under the curve for a constant velocity scenario.

header: Integral Notation for Distance Calculation - Integration
content: Using integral notation, we have 2 ∫ 75 dt = 150.
summary: This section presents the integral notation used for calculating distance with constant velocity.

content: In the context of displacement, net signed area allows us to take direction into account. If a car travels straight north at a speed of 60mph for 2 hours, it is 120 miles north of its starting position. If the car then turns around and travels south at a speed of 40 mph for 3 hours, it will be back at its starting position.
description: Figure 5.21 depicts the displacement of the car as it travels north and then south, demonstrating how direction affects net displacement.
summary: This section discusses how to account for direction in displacement calculations.

content: Again, using integral notation, we have 2 5 ∫ 60 dt + ∫ -40 dt = 120 - 120 = 0.
summary: This chunk provides the integral calculation that shows the net displacement results in zero.

header: Understanding Total Area in Integration - Chapter 5
content: Suppose we want to know how far the car travels overall, regardless of direction. In this case, we want to know the area between the curve and the x-axis, regardless of whether that area is above or below the axis. This is called the total area.
summary: This section introduces the concept of total area in relation to a car's travel.

content:
Graphically, it is easiest to think of calculating total area by adding the areas above the axis and the areas below the axis (rather than subtracting the areas below the axis, as we did with net signed area). To accomplish this mathematically, we use the absolute value function. Thus, the total distance traveled by the car is 
∫ |60| dt + ∫ |−40| dt = ∫ 60 dt + ∫ 40 dt 
=120+120
=240.
summary: This section explains how to calculate total area using absolute values.

header: Defining Signed and Total Area - Chapter 5
content:
Bringing these ideas together formally, we state the following definitions. 
Let f(x) be an integrable function defined on an interval [a, b]. Let A1 represent the area between f(x) and the x-axis that lies above the axis and let A2 represent the area between f(x) and the x-axis that lies below the axis. Then, the net signed area between f(x) and the x-axis is given by 
∫ f(x) dx = A1 - A2.
summary: This section provides formal definitions for signed areas and total area.

content:
The total area between f(x) and the x-axis is given by 
∫ |f(x)| dx = A1 + A2.
summary: This section defines the total area in relation to the signed areas.

content: The integral of a difference is the difference of the integrals.

header: Integration of Constant Times a Function
content: The integral of the product of a constant and a function is equal to the constant multiplied by the integral of the function.
summary: Clarifies how to integrate a constant multiplied by a function.

content:
We oftentimes need to find the average of a set of numbers, such as an average test grade. Suppose you received the following test scores in your algebra class: 89, 90, 56, 78, 100, and 69. Your semester grade is your average of test scores and you want to know what grade to expect. We can find the average by adding all the scores and dividing by the number of scores. In this case, there are six test scores. Thus,
89+90+56+78+100+69 482
= ≈80.33.
6 6
Therefore, your average test grade is approximately 80.33, which translates to a B− at most schools.
summary: This section explains how to calculate the average of a set of test scores.

header: Average Value of a Function Explained
content: Suppose, however, that we have a function v(t) that gives us the speed of an object at any time t, and we want to find the object's average speed. The function v(t) takes on an infinite number of values, so we can’t use the process just described. Fortunately, we can use a definite integral to find the average value of a function such as this.
summary: This section introduces the concept of finding the average speed of an object using a definite integral.

header: Defining the Average Value Over an Interval
content: Let f(x) be continuous over the interval ⎡a, b⎤ and let ⎡a, b⎤ be divided into n subintervals of width Δx=(b−a)/n. Choose a representative x* in each subinterval and calculate f⎛x* ⎞ for i=1, 2,…,n. In other words, consider each f⎛x* ⎞ as a sampling of the function over each subinterval.

content:
5.3 | The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
Learning Objectives
5.3.1 Describe the meaning of the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals.
5.3.2 State the meaning of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1.
5.3.3 Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1, to evaluate derivatives of integrals.
5.3.4 State the meaning of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2.
5.3.5 Use the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2, to evaluate definite integrals.
5.3.6 Explain the relationship between differentiation and integration.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

content: In the previous two sections, we looked at the definite integral and its relationship to the area under the curve of a function. Unfortunately, so far, the only tools we have available to calculate the value of a definite integral are geometric area formulas and limits of Riemann sums, and both approaches are extremely cumbersome. In this section, we look at some more powerful and useful techniques for evaluating definite integrals.
summary: This chunk introduces the need for improved techniques for evaluating definite integrals.

content: These new techniques rely on the relationship between differentiation and integration. This relationship was discovered and explored by both Sir Isaac Newton and Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (among others) during the late 1600s and early 1700s, and it is codified in what we now call the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, which has two parts that we examine in this section. Its very name indicates how central this theorem is to the entire development of calculus.
summary: This chunk explains the historical development and significance of the relationship between differentiation and integration.

content: Isaac Newton’s contributions to mathematics and physics changed the way we look at the world. The relationships he discovered, codified as Newton’s laws and the law of universal gravitation, are still taught as foundational material in physics today, and his calculus has spawned entire fields of mathematics. To learn more, read a brief biography (http://www.openstax.org/l/20_newtonbio) of Newton with multimedia clips.
summary: This chunk discusses the lasting impact of Isaac Newton's contributions to mathematics and physics.

header: Introduction to the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals
content: Before we get to this crucial theorem, however, let’s examine another important theorem, the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals, which is needed to prove the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
summary: This chunk sets the stage for understanding the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals as a precursor to the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

header: Understanding the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals
content: The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals states that a continuous function on a closed interval takes on its average value at some point in that interval. The theorem guarantees that if f(x) is continuous, a point c exists in an interval [a, b] such that the value of the function at c is equal to the average value of f(x) over [a, b]. We state this theorem mathematically with the help of the formula for the average value of a function that we presented at the end of the preceding section.
summary: This chunk describes the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals and its significance regarding continuous functions.

header: Mathematical Definition of the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals
content:
Theorem 5.3: The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals
If f(x) is continuous over an interval [a, b], then there is at least one point c∈[a, b] such that
f(c)= 1/(b−a) * ∫[a, b] f(x)dx. This formula can also be stated as
∫[a, b] f(x)dx= f(c)(b−a).
summary: This chunk presents the formal statement of the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals along with its mathematical implications.

header: Proof of the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals
content:
Proof
Since f(x) is continuous on [a, b], by the extreme value theorem (see Maxima and Minima), it assumes minimum and maximum values—m and M, respectively—on [a, b]. Then, for all x in [a, b], we have m≤ f(x)≤M. Therefore, by the comparison theorem (see The Definite Integral), we have
m(b−a)≤∫[a, b] f(x)dx≤M(b−a).
summary: This chunk outlines the proof of the Mean Value Theorem for Integrals using the extreme value theorem and the comparison theorem.

header: Understanding the Mean Value Theorem in Integration
content:

Since 1 ∫ f(x)dx is a number between m and M, and since f(x) is continuous and assumes the values m and M over ⎡a, b⎤, by the Intermediate Value Theorem (see Continuity), there is a number c over ⎡a, b⎤ such that
b
f(c)= 1 ∫ f(x)dx,
b−a a
and the proof is complete.
summary: This section discusses the application of the Mean Value Theorem in finding average values in integration.

content: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 1: Integrals and Antiderivatives. As mentioned earlier, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus is an extremely powerful theorem that establishes the relationship between differentiation and integration, and gives us a way to evaluate definite integrals without using Riemann sums or calculating areas.
summary: This segment introduces the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and its significance.

header: Details of the First Part of the Theorem
content: The theorem is comprised of two parts, the first of which, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1, is stated here. Part 1 establishes the relationship between differentiation and integration.
summary: This part outlines the two segments of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

content: Theorem 5.4: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1. If f(x) is continuous over an interval ⎡a, b⎤, and the function F(x) is defined by F(x)=∫ f(t)dt from a to x, then F′(x)= f(x) over ⎡a, b⎤.
summary: This section contains the formal mathematical expression of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1.

header: Clarification on Notation and Function Definition
content: Before we delve into the proof, a couple of subtleties are worth mentioning here. First, a comment on the notation. Note that we have defined a function, F(x), as the definite integral of another function, f(t), from the point a to the point x. At first glance, this is confusing, because we have said several times that a definite integral is a number, and here it looks like it’s a function. The key here is to notice that for any particular value of x, the definite integral is a number. So the function F(x) returns a number (the value of the definite integral) for each value of x.
summary: This part clarifies notation used in the theorem and explains the nature of F(x) as a function derived from a definite integral.

header: Implications of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus - Integration
content: Second, it is worth commenting on some of the key implications of this theorem. There is a reason it is called the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. Not only does it establish a relationship between integration and differentiation, but also it guarantees that any integrable function has an antiderivative. Specifically, it guarantees that any continuous function has an antiderivative.
summary: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus links integration and differentiation while ensuring continuous functions have antiderivatives.

header: Proof of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus - Integration
content: Proof Applying the definition of the derivative, we have F(x+h)−F(x) F′(x) = lim h→0 h ⎡ x+h x ⎤ = lim 1⎢∫ f(t)dt−∫ f(t)dt⎥ h→0h⎣ a a ⎦ ⎡ x+h a ⎤ = lim 1⎢∫ f(t)dt+∫ f(t)dt⎥ h→0h⎣ a x ⎦ x+h = lim 1∫ f(t)dt. h→0h x x+h Looking carefully at this last expression, we see 1∫ f(t)dt is just the average value of the function f(x) over the h x interval ⎡x, x+h⎤. Therefore, by The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals, there is some number c in ⎡x, x+h⎤ such that x+h 1∫ f(x)dx= f(c). h x In addition, since c is between x and x+h, c approaches x as h approaches zero. Also, since f(x) is continuous, we have lim f(c)= lim f(c)= f(x). Putting all these pieces together, we have h→0 c→x x+h F′(x) = lim 1∫ f(x)dx h→0h x = lim f(c) h→0 = f(x), and the proof is complete. □
summary: The proof demonstrates how the Fundamental Theorem connects derivatives with integrals through average values and continuity.

content: Let F(x)=∫ sint dt. Find F′(x).
summary: An example illustrating the application of the Fundamental Theorem to find derivatives.

content: Letting u(x)= x, we have F(x)=∫ sint dt. Thus, by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus and the chain rule, F′(x) = sin(u(x)) · u'(x) = sin(x) · 1 = sin x.
summary: This section presents the solution to the derivative calculation example.

content: Let F(x)=∫ cost dt. Find F′(x).

header: Using Fundamental Theorem with Variable Limits of Integration
content: Let F(x)=∫ t^3 dt. Find F′(x).
summary: This section focuses on finding derivatives with variable limits of integration using the Fundamental Theorem.

header: The Impact of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2
content:
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2: The Evaluation Theorem
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2, is perhaps the most important theorem in calculus. After tireless efforts
bymathematiciansforapproximately500years,newtechniquesemergedthatprovidedscientistswiththenecessarytools
to explain many phenomena. Using calculus, astronomers could finally determine distances in space and map planetary
orbits.Everydayfinancialproblemssuchascalculatingmarginalcostsorpredictingtotalprofitcouldnowbehandledwith
simplicity and accuracy. Engineers could calculate the bending strength of materials or the three-dimensional motion of
objects. Our view of the world was forever changed with calculus.
summary: This section discusses the significance of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in advancing scientific and engineering fields.

content:
Analysis
Notice that we did not include the “+ C” term when we wrote the antiderivative. The reason is that, according to the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2, any antiderivative works. So, for convenience, we chose the antiderivative with C=0. If we had chosen another antiderivative, the constant term would have canceled out. This always happens when evaluating a definite integral.
summary: This analysis explains the omission of the constant term in the evaluation of the definite integral.

header: Understanding area under the curve and its implications
content: The region of the area we just calculated is depicted in Figure 5.28. Note that the region between the curve and the x-axis is all below the x-axis. Area is always positive, but a definite integral can still produce a negative number (a net signed area). For example, if this were a profit function, a negative number indicates the company is operating at a loss over the given interval.
description: Figure 5.28 illustrates the evaluation of a definite integral that can yield a negative value despite the area being positive.
summary: This section highlights the implications of a definite integral yielding a negative value in real-world contexts.

header: Understanding Free Fall and Terminal Velocity in Skydiving
content:
A Parachutist in Free Fall

Julie is an avid skydiver. She has more than 300 jumps under her belt and has mastered the art of making adjustments to her body position in the air to control how fast she falls. If she arches her back and points her belly toward the ground, she reaches a terminal velocity of approximately 120 mph (176 ft/sec). If, instead, she orients her body with her head straight down, she falls faster, reaching a terminal velocity of 150 mph (220 ft/sec).
summary: Julie employs body position adjustments to control her free fall velocities.

content: Since Julie will be moving (falling) in a downward direction, we assume the downward direction is positive to simplify our calculations. Julie executes her jumps from an altitude of 12,500 ft. After she exits the aircraft, she immediately starts falling at a velocity given by v(t) = 32t. She continues to accelerate according to this velocity function until she reaches terminal velocity. After she reaches terminal velocity, her speed remains constant until she pulls her ripcord and slows down to land.
summary: The downward velocity and acceleration over time are mathematically described.

header: Calculating Free Fall Time to Terminal Velocity
content: On her first jump of the day, Julie orients herself in the slower 'belly down' position (terminal velocity is 176 ft/sec). Using this information, answer the following questions. 1. How long after she exits the aircraft does Julie reach terminal velocity?
summary: Questions set to determine the time to reach terminal velocity.

header: Setting up Expressions for Distance Fallen
content: 2. Based on your answer to question 1, set up an expression involving one or more integrals that represent the distance Julie falls after 30 sec.
summary: An expression for distance fallen is to be constructed using integrals.

header: Free Fall Duration until Parachute Deployment
content: 3. If Julie pulls her ripcord at an altitude of 3000 ft, how long does she spend in a free fall?
summary: Determining Julie's free fall time until the parachute is deployed.

header: Understanding Parachute Opening Time and Speed Reduction
content: 4. Julie pulls her ripcord at 3000 ft. It takes 5 sec for her parachute to open completely and for her to slow down, during which time she falls another 400 ft. After her canopy is fully open, her speed is reduced to 16 ft/sec. Find the total time Julie spends in the air, from the time she leaves the airplane until the time her feet touch the ground.
summary: The total air time including parachute opening is calculated.

content: This OpenStax book is available for free at http://cnx.org/content/col11964/1.12
summary: Reference to the OpenStax resource for further reading.

header: Understanding Terminal Velocity in Freefall: Julie's Experience
content: On Julie’s second jump of the day, she decides she wants to fall a little faster and orients herself in the 'head down' position. Her terminal velocity in this position is 220 ft/sec. Answer these questions based on this velocity: 5. How long does it take Julie to reach terminal velocity in this case?
summary: This chunk discusses Julie's decision to change her position during free fall to increase her terminal velocity.

header: Preparing for Parachute Deployment: Body Positioning and Free Fall
content: 6. Before pulling her ripcord, Julie reorients her body in the 'belly down' position so she is not moving quite as fast when her parachute opens. If she begins this maneuver at an altitude of 4000 ft, how long does she spend in a free fall before beginning the reorientation?
summary: This section examines the impact of body positioning on Julie's descent speed prior to parachute deployment.

header: The Use of Wingsuits in Skydiving: Enhancing Free Fall
content: Some jumpers wear 'wingsuits'. These suits have fabric panels between the arms and legs and allow the wearer to glide around in a free fall, much like a flying squirrel. (Indeed, the suits are sometimes called 'flying squirrel suits.') When wearing these suits, terminal velocity can be reduced to about 30 mph (44 ft/sec), allowing the wearers a much longer time in the air. Wingsuit flyers still use parachutes to land; although the vertical velocities are within the margin of safety, horizontal velocities can exceed 70 mph, much too fast to land safely.
description: Figure 5.31: The fabric panels on the arms and legs of a wingsuit work to reduce the vertical velocity of a skydiver’s fall. (credit: Richard Schneider)
summary: This chunk explains the function and benefits of wingsuits for skydivers, including their impact on terminal velocity.

header: Calculating Gliding Time in a Wingsuit: Julie's Third Jump
content: 7. If Julie dons a wingsuit before her third jump of the day, and she pulls her ripcord at an altitude of 3000 ft, how long does she get to spend gliding around in the air?
summary: This section poses a problem related to the duration of gliding prior to parachute deployment while wearing a wingsuit.

header: Kepler's First Law and Motion of Planets in Elliptical Orbits
content: Kepler’s first law states that the planets move in elliptical orbits with the Sun at one focus. The closest point of a planetary orbit to the Sun is called the perihelion (for Earth, it currently occurs around January 3) and the farthest point is called the aphelion (for Earth, it currently occurs around July 4).
summary: Kepler's first law defines the shape of planetary orbits with respect to the Sun.

header: Kepler's Second Law and Equal Areas
content: Kepler’s second law states that planets sweep out equal areas of their elliptical orbits in equal times. Thus, the two arcs indicated in the following figure are swept out in equal times. At what time of year is Earth moving fastest in its orbit? When is it moving slowest?
description: The following figure illustrates the equal areas swept out by a planet in its elliptical orbit over equal time intervals.
summary: Kepler's second law addresses the speed variations of planets in their orbits.

header: Ellipse Coordinates and Distance to Focus
content: A point on an ellipse with major axis length 2a and minor axis length 2b has the coordinates (a*cos(θ), b*sin(θ)), 0≤θ≤2π. a. Show that the distance from this point to the focus at (−c, 0) is d(θ)=a+c*cos(θ), where c=√(a²−b²). b. Use these coordinates to show that the average distance d from a point on the ellipse to the focus at (−c, 0), with respect to angle θ, is a.
summary: The section examines the geometric properties of the ellipse and its relation to the focus.

header: Average Distance and Astronomical Unit Explained
content: As implied earlier, according to Kepler’s laws, Earth’s orbit is an ellipse with the Sun at one focus. The perihelion for Earth’s orbit around the Sun is 147,098,290 km and the aphelion is 152,098,232 km. a. By placing the major axis along the x-axis, find the average distance from Earth to the Sun. b. The classic definition of an astronomical unit (AU) is the distance from Earth to the Sun, and its value was computed as the average of the perihelion and aphelion distances. Is this definition justified?
summary: This part discusses the actual measurements of Earth's orbit and the concept of the astronomical unit.

content: The force of gravitational attraction between the Sun and a planet is F(θ) = G*m*M / r²(θ), where m is the mass of the planet, M is the mass of the Sun, G is a universal constant, and r(θ) is the distance between the Sun and the planet when the planet is at an angle θ with the major axis of its orbit. Assuming that M, m, and the ellipse parameters a and b (half-lengths of the major and minor axes) are given, set up—but do not evaluate—an integral that expresses in terms of G, m, M, a, b the average gravitational force between the Sun and the planet.
summary: This section formulates the gravitational force between the Sun and a planet in orbital mechanics.

content:
5.4 | Integration Formulas and the Net Change Theorem
Learning Objectives
5.4.1 Apply the basic integration formulas.
5.4.2 Explain the significance of the net change theorem.
5.4.3 Use the net change theorem to solve applied problems.
5.4.4 Apply the integrals of odd and even functions.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives related to integration formulas and the net change theorem.

content: In this section, we use some basic integration formulas studied previously to solve some key applied problems. It is important to note that these formulas are presented in terms of definite integrals. Although definite and indefinite integrals are closely related, there are some key differences to keep in mind. A definite integral is either a number (when the limits of integration are constants) or a single function (when one or both of the limits of integration are variables). An indefinite integral represents a family of functions, all of which differ by a constant. As you become more familiar with integration, you will get a feel for when to use definite integrals and when to use indefinite integrals. You will naturally select the correct approach for a given problem without thinking too much about it. However, until these concepts are cemented in your mind, think carefully about whether you need a definite integral or an indefinite integral and make sure you are using the proper notation based on your choice.
summary: This chunk explains the differences between definite and indefinite integrals and their implications for integration.

content:
Basic Integration Formulas
Recall the integration formulas given in the table in Antiderivatives and the rule on properties of definite integrals. Let’s look at a few examples of how to apply these rules.
summary: This section introduces the basic integration formulas and suggests examples for application.

header: Example of Integration Using Power Rule
content:
Example 5.23
Integrating a Function Using the Power Rule
Use the power rule to integrate the function ∫ t(1+t)dt.

Solution
The first step is to rewrite the function and simplify it so we can apply the power rule: 
∫ t(1+t)dt = ∫ t^(1/2) (1+t)dt
= ∫ (t^(1/2) + t^(3/2)) dt.
Now apply the power rule:
∫ (t^(1/2) + t^(3/2)) dt = (2/3 t^(3/2) + 2/5 t^(5/2)) | [1, 4]
= [ (2/3) * 4^(3/2) + (2/5) * 4^(5/2)] - [ (2/3) * 1^(3/2) + (2/5) * 1^(5/2)]
= (2/3 * 8 + 2/5 * 32) - (2/3 + 2/5)
= ... [continue as needed]
description: This chunk includes an example of integrating a function using the power rule.
summary: This section presents an example of applying the power rule to integrate a function.

header: Solving for Definite Integral of a Polynomial
content: 5.21 Find the definite integral of f(x)= x^2 −3x over the interval [1, 3].
summary: This problem involves finding the definite integral of a polynomial function over a specified interval.

content: The net change theorem considers the integral of a rate of change. It says that when a quantity changes, the new value equals the initial value plus the integral of the rate of change of that quantity. The formula can be expressed in two ways. The second is more familiar; it is simply the definite integral.
summary: Introduction to the net change theorem and its formulations.

header: Mathematical Representation of the Net Change Theorem
content: Theorem 5.6: Net Change Theorem The new value of a changing quantity equals the initial value plus the integral of the rate of change: (5.18) b F(b)=F(a)+∫ F'(x)dx a or b ∫ F'(x)dx=F(b)−F(a). a Subtracting F(a) from both sides of the first equation yields the second equation. Since they are equivalent formulas, which one we use depends on the application.
summary: Presentation of the mathematical formulas defining the net change theorem.

header: Significance and Applications of the Net Change Theorem
content: The significance of the net change theorem lies in the results. Net change can be applied to area, distance, and volume, to name only a few applications. Net change accounts for negative quantities automatically without having to write more than one integral.
summary: Exploration of the importance and various applications of the net change theorem.

header: Example Demonstrating the Net Change Theorem
content: To illustrate, let’s apply the net change theorem to a velocity function in which the result is displacement. We looked at a simple example of this in The Definite Integral. Suppose a car is moving due north (the positive direction) at 40 mph between 2 p.m. and 4 p.m., then the car moves south at 30 mph between 4 p.m. and 5 p.m. We can graph this motion as shown in Figure 5.32.
description: Figure 5.32 illustrates the speed versus time for the given motion of a car.
summary: Application of the net change theorem with a real-world example of a car's motion.

header: Calculating Net Displacement and Total Distance
content: Just as we did before, we can use definite integrals to calculate the net displacement as well as the total distance traveled. The net displacement is given by 5 5 4 ⌠ ∫ v(t)dt =∫ 40dt+ −30dt 2 2 ⌡ 4 =80−30 =50. Thus, at 5 p.m. the car is 50 mi north of its starting position. The total distance traveled is given by...
summary: Calculation of net displacement and total distance traveled using the given velocity functions.

= 110. Therefore, between 2 p.m. and 5 p.m., the car traveled a total of 110 mi.
summary: The total distance traveled by a car is calculated using integration of the velocity function.

header: Distinction Between Net Displacement and Total Distance
content: To summarize, net displacement may include both positive and negative values. In other words, the velocity function accounts for both forward distance and backward distance. To find net displacement, integrate the velocity function over the interval. Total distance traveled, on the other hand, is always positive. To find the total distance traveled by an object, regardless of direction, we need to integrate the absolute value of the velocity function.
summary: This section clarifies the difference between net displacement and total distance in terms of integration.

header: Calculation of Total Distance from Velocity Function
content:
Example 5.25
Finding the Total Distance Traveled
Use Example 5.24 to find the total distance traveled by a particle according to the velocity function v(t)=3t−5 m/sec over a time interval [0, 3].
summary: This example demonstrates how to calculate the total distance traveled using a given velocity function.

header: Methodology for Total Distance Calculation through Integration
content:
Solution
The total distance traveled includes both the positive and the negative values. Therefore, we must integrate the absolute value of the velocity function to find the total distance traveled.
summary: The solution describes the necessary integration of the absolute value of the velocity function to compute total distance.

header: Identifying Intervals for Velocity Function Integration
content: To continue with the example, use two integrals to find the total distance. First, find the t-intercept of the function, since that is where the division of the interval occurs. Set the equation equal to zero and solve for t. Thus, 3t−5 = 0, 3t = 5, t = 5/3.
summary: The method begins by identifying the t-intercept to establish the intervals for integration.

header: Defining Subintervals for Total Distance Calculation
content: The two subintervals are [0, 5/3] and [5/3, 3]. To find the total distance traveled, integrate the absolute value of the function. Since the function is negative over the interval [0, 5/3], we have |v(t)|=−v(t) over that interval. Over [5/3, 3], the function is positive, so |v(t)|=v(t).
summary: This chunk defines the subintervals required for calculating total distance using absolute values of the velocity function.
A mathematical derivation showing the application of integration on a velocity function to find total distance.

content: Integrating Even and Odd Functions We saw in Functions and Graphs that an even function is a function in which f(−x)= f(x) for all x in the domain—that is, the graph of the curve is unchanged when x is replaced with −x. The graphs of even functions are symmetric about the y-axis. An odd function is one in which f(−x)=−f(x) for all x in the domain, and the graph of the function is symmetric about the origin.
summary: This section defines even and odd functions and their graphical symmetry.

header: Integral Rules for Even and Odd Functions
content: Integrals of even functions, when the limits of integration are from −a to a, involve two equal areas, because they are symmetric about the y-axis. Integrals of odd functions, when the limits of integration are similarly [−a, a], evaluate to zero because the areas above and below the x-axis are equal.
summary: This section explains how integrals of even and odd functions behave in symmetric intervals.

header: Rules for Integrating Even and Odd Functions
content: Rule: Integrals of Even and Odd Functions For continuous even functions such that f(−x)= f(x), a a ⌠ f(x)dx=2∫ f(x)dx. ⌡ −a 0 For continuous odd functions such that f(−x)=−f(x), a ∫ f(x)dx=0. −a
summary: This section provides specific rules for integrating even and odd functions.

header: Integration of an Even Function - Example 5.28
content: Integrate the even function ∫ ⎛ 3x 8 −2 ⎞ dx and verify that the integration formula for even functions holds.
summary: This example demonstrates how to integrate an even function.

header: Solution to the Even Function Integration
content: The symmetry appears in the graphs in Figure 5.35. Graph (a) shows the region below the curve and above the x-axis. We have to zoom into this graph by a huge amount to see the region. Graph (b) shows the region above the curve and below the x-axis. The signed area of this region is negative. Both views illustrate the symmetry about the y-axis of an even function. We have ∫ 2 ⎛ 3x 8 −2 ⎞ dx = ⎛ x 9 −2x ⎞ | 2⎝ ⎠ ⎝3 ⎠ −2 −2.
description: Figure 5.35 contains two graphs: Graph (a) depicts the positive area between the curve and the x-axis, while Graph (b) illustrates the negative area between the curve and the x-axis, highlighting the even function's symmetry about the y-axis.
summary: This section provides the solution to the integration of an even function, including analysis of symmetry in graphs.

header: Calculation to Verify Integration Formula for Even Functions
content: We can calculate the integral from 0 to 2 and double it to verify the integration formula for even functions, then check to make sure we get the same answer. ∫ 2 ⎛ 3x 8 −2 ⎞ dx = ⎛ x 9 −2x ⎞ | 0 0.
summary: This calculation checks the validity of the integration formula for even functions.

content: Since 2· 500 = 1000, we have verified the formula for even functions in this particular example.
summary: This concluding statement confirms the successful verification of the even function integration formula.

content: 220. Write an integral that quantifies the increase in the volume of a cube when the side length doubles from s units to 2s units and evaluate the integral.

content: 222. Write an integral that quantifies the increase in the volume of a sphere as its radius doubles from R units to 2R units and evaluate the integral.

header: Displacement of a Particle with Linear Velocity
content: 223. Suppose that a particle moves along a straight line with velocity v(t)=4−2t, where 0≤t≤2 (in meters per second). Find the displacement at time t and the total distance traveled up to t=2.
summary: Calculating the displacement and total distance traveled by a particle moving with a specified linear velocity.

content: 224. Suppose that a particle moves along a straight line with velocity defined by v(t)=t²−3t−18, where 0≤t≤6 (in meters per second). Find the displacement at time t and the total distance traveled up to t=6.
summary: This problem involves finding the displacement of a particle given a quadratic velocity function.

content: 213. Write an integral that expresses the increase in the perimeter P(s) of a square when its side length s increases from 2 units to 4 units and evaluate the integral.
summary: An exercise focused on writing an integral for the change in the perimeter of a square as its side length increases.

content: 214. Write an integral that quantifies the change in the area A(s)=s² of a square when the side length doubles from S units to 2S units and evaluate the integral.
summary: This exercise requires calculating the change in area of a square as its side length is doubled.

content: 215. A regular N-gon (an N-sided polygon with sides that have equal length s, such as a pentagon or hexagon) has perimeter N*s. Write an integral that expresses the increase in perimeter of a regular N-gon when the length of each side increases from 1 unit to 2 units and evaluate the integral.
summary: Calculating the increase in perimeter for a regular N-gon as side lengths are doubled.

content: 216. The area of a regular pentagon with side length a>0 is 1/4 * √(5(5 + 2√5)) * a². The Pentagon in Washington, DC, has inner sides of length 360 ft and outer sides of length 920 ft. Write an integral to express the area of the roof of the Pentagon according to these dimensions and evaluate this area.
summary: An exercise requiring the calculation of the area of the Pentagon's roof based on provided dimensions.

content: 227. A ball is thrown upward from a height of 1.5 m at an initial speed of 40 m/sec. Acceleration resulting from gravity is -9.8 m/sec². Neglecting air resistance, solve for the velocity v(t) and the height h(t) of the ball t seconds after it is thrown and before it returns to the ground.
summary: This exercise deals with finding the velocity and height of a ball thrown upwards under gravitational effects.

content: 228. A ball is thrown upward from a height of 3 m at an initial speed of 60 m/sec. Acceleration resulting from gravity is -9.8 m/sec². Neglecting air resistance, solve for the velocity v(t) and the height h(t) of the ball t seconds after it is thrown and before it returns to the ground.
summary: Similar to previous problems, determining the trajectory of a ball under gravity with different initial conditions.

summary: Determining the change in radius of a circle based on its area growth over a specified time interval.

content: A spherical balloon is being inflated at a constant rate. If the volume of the balloon changes from 36π in³ to 288π in³ between time t=30 and t=60 seconds, find the net change in the radius of the balloon during that time.
summary: Discussion on the calculation of volume change related to the radius of an inflating balloon.

content: Newton’s law of gravity states that the gravitational force exerted by an object of mass M and one of mass m with centers that are separated by a distance r is F = G mM / r^2, with G an empirical constant G = 6.67 x 10^-11 m³/(kg·s²).

Find the corresponding distances if the surface is slippery wet asphalt.

content:
Learning Objectives
5.5.1 Use substitution to evaluate indefinite integrals.
5.5.2 Use substitution to evaluate definite integrals.
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus gave us a method to evaluate integrals without using Riemann sums. The drawback of this method, though, is that we must be able to find an antiderivative, and this is not always easy. In this section, we examine a technique called integration by substitution to help us find antiderivatives. Specifically, this method helps us find antiderivatives when the integrand is the result of a chain-rule derivative.
summary: This section introduces the substitution method in integration to find antiderivatives.

content: At first, the approach to the substitution procedure may not appear very obvious. However, it is primarily a visual task—that is, the integrand shows you what to do; it is a matter of recognizing the form of the function. So, what are we supposed to see? We are looking for an integrand of the form f[g(x)]g′(x)dx. For example, in the integral ⌠(x² - 3)²xdx, we have f(x) = x³, g(x) = x² - 3, and g'(x) = 2x. Then, f[g(x)]g′(x) = (x² - 3)(2x), and we see that our integrand is in the correct form.
summary: This chunk discusses recognizing the correct form of integrands suitable for substitution.

header: The Concept of Substitution in Integration
content: The method is called substitution because we substitute part of the integrand with the variable u and part of the integrand with du. It is also referred to as change of variables because we are changing variables to obtain an expression that is easier to work with for applying the integration rules.
summary: This chunk explains the rationale behind the substitution method in integration.

header: Theorem on Indefinite Integrals and Substitution
content:
Theorem 5.7: Substitution with Indefinite Integrals
Let u = g(x), where g′(x) is continuous over an interval, let f(x) be continuous over the corresponding range of g, and let F(x) be an antiderivative of f(x). Then, ∫ f[g(x)]g′(x)dx = ∫ f(u)du = F(u) + C = F[g(x)] + C.
summary: This chunk presents a formal theorem related to substitution for indefinite integrals.

content:
Proof
Let f, g, u, and F be as specified in the theorem. Then dF(g(x)) = F′(g(x)) g′(x) = f[g(x)] g′(x). Integrating both sides with respect to x, we see that ∫ f[g(x)]g′(x) dx = F[g(x)] + C. If we now substitute u = g(x), and du = g′(x) dx, we get ∫ f[g(x)]g′(x) dx = ∫ f(u) du = F(u) + C = F[g(x)] + C.
summary: This chunk provides a proof of the theorem concerning substitution in integrals.

Also, we have the option of replacing the original expression for u after we find the antiderivative, which means that we do not have to change the limits of integration.

content:
5.6 | Integrals Involving Exponential and Logarithmic Functions
Learning Objectives
5.6.1 Integrate functions involving exponential functions.
5.6.2 Integrate functions involving logarithmic functions.
Exponential and logarithmic functions are used to model population growth, cell growth, and financial growth, as well as depreciation, radioactive decay, and resource consumption, to name only a few applications. In this section, we explore integration involving exponential and logarithmic functions.
summary: Introduction to the use of exponential and logarithmic functions in integration.

header: Key Properties of Exponential Functions in Calculus
content:
Integrals of Exponential Functions
The exponential function is perhaps the most efficient function in terms of the operations of calculus. The exponential function, y=e^x, is its own derivative and its own integral.
summary: Exponential functions have unique properties that simplify their integration.

content:
Rule: Integrals of Exponential Functions
Exponential functions can be integrated using the following formulas.
∫e^x dx = e^x + C (5.21)
∫a^x dx = (a^x)/(ln a) + C
summary: Presentation of the main formulas for integrating exponential functions.

content:
Example 5.37
Finding an Antiderivative of an Exponential Function
Find the antiderivative of the exponential function e^{-x}.
Solution
Use substitution, setting u=-x, and then du=-1dx. Multiply the du equation by -1, so you now have -du=dx. Then,
∫e^{-x} dx = -∫e^u du = -e^u + C = -e^{-x} + C.
summary: A worked example illustrating how to find the antiderivative of an exponential function.

header: Common Mistakes in Integrating Exponential Expressions
content: Find the antiderivative of the function using substitution: x^2 e^{-2x}. A common mistake when dealing with exponential expressions is treating the exponent on e the same way we treat exponents in polynomial expressions. We cannot use the power rule for the exponent one. This can be especially confusing when we have both exponentials and polynomials in the same expression, as in the previous checkpoint. In these cases, we should always double-check to make sure we’re using the right rules for the functions we’re integrating.
summary: A cautionary note on common errors when integrating exponential functions along with polynomials.

content:
Example 5.38
Square Root of an Exponential Function
Find the antiderivative of the exponential function e x 1+e x .
Solution
First rewrite the problem using a rational exponent:
∫e x 1+e x dx=∫e x(1+e x) 1/2 dx.
Using substitution, choose u=1+e x .u=1+e x . Then, du=e x dx. We have
summary: This example demonstrates how to find the antiderivative of an exponential function using substitution.

header: Adjusting for Constant Multipliers in Substitution
content:
Again, du is off by a constant multiplier; the original function contains a factor of 3x2, not 6x2. Multiply both sides of the equation by 1 so that the integrand in u equals the integrand in x. Thus, 
∫3x 2 e 2x 3 dx= 1∫e u du.

header: Understanding Exponential Integration in Business Applications
content: Integrate the expression inu and then substitute the original expression in x back into the u integral: 1∫e^u du = 1/e^u + C = 1/e^(2x^3) + C. Evaluate the indefinite integral ∫2x^3 e^x dx.
summary: This section discusses the integration of exponential functions in business applications.

header: The Role of Price-Demand Functions in Economics
content: As mentioned at the beginning of this section, exponential functions are used in many real-life applications. The number e is often associated with compounded or accelerating growth, as we have seen in earlier sections about the derivative. Although the derivative represents a rate of change or a growth rate, the integral represents the total change or the total growth. Let’s look at an example in which integration of an exponential function solves a common business application.
summary: This section explains the significance of price-demand functions and exponential growth in economics.

content: A price–demand function tells us the relationship between the quantity of a product demanded and the price of the product. In general, price decreases as quantity demanded increases. The marginal price–demand function is the derivative of the price–demand function and it tells us how fast the price changes at a given level of production. These functions are used in business to determine the price–elasticity of demand, and to help companies determine whether changing production levels would be profitable.
summary: This example illustrates how price-demand functions are utilized in business to analyze demand and pricing strategies.

header: Finding a Price-Demand Equation for Toothpaste
content: Example 5.40 Finding a Price–Demand Equation Find the price–demand equation for a particular brand of toothpaste at a supermarket chain when the demand is 50 tubes per week at $2.35 per tube, given that the marginal price—demand function, p′(x), for x number of tubes per week, is given as −0.01x p'(x) = −0.015e^(-0.01x). If the supermarket chain sells 100 tubes per week, what price should it set?
summary: This example presents a case study on determining a price-demand equation for toothpaste in a supermarket setting.

header: Integration of the Marginal Price-Demand Function
content: To find the price–demand equation, integrate the marginal price–demand function. First find the antiderivative, then look at the particulars. Thus, p(x) = ∫−0.015e^(-0.01x) dx = −0.015∫e^(-0.01x) dx. Using substitution, let u = −0.01x and du = −0.01dx. Then, divide both sides of the du equation by −0.01.
summary: This section details the integration process to derive the price-demand equation from the marginal price-demand function.

header: Solving for the Constant in the Price-Demand Equation
content: This gives −0.015∫e^u du = 1.5∫e^u du −0.01 u = 1.5e^(-0.01x) + C. The next step is to solve for C. We know that when the price is $2.35 per tube, the demand is 50 tubes per week.
summary: In this section, the procedure to solve for the constant in the price-demand equation is explained.

content: −0.01(50) p(50) =1.5e +C =2.35. Now, just solve forC: −0.5 C =2.35−1.5e =2.35−0.91 =1.44. Thus, −0.01x p(x)=1.5e +1.44. If the supermarket sells 100 tubes of toothpaste per week, the price would be −0.01(100) −1 p(100)=1.5e +1.44=1.5e +1.44≈1.99. The supermarket should charge $1.99 per tube if it is selling 100 tubes per week.
summary: This section discusses how to calculate the price of toothpaste based on sales volume using integration.

header: Application of Integration in Bacterial Growth
content: Example 5.42 Growth of Bacteria in a Culture Suppose the rate of growth of bacteria in a Petridish is given by q(t) = 3t, where t is given in hours and q(t) is given in thousands of bacteria per hour. If a culture starts with 10,000 bacteria, find a function Q(t) that gives the number of bacteria in the Petri dish at any time t. How many bacteria are in the dish after 2 hours?
summary: This example demonstrates how to model bacterial growth using integration.

content:
Solution We have 
 Q(t) = ⌠ t 3 dt = ln(3) + C. 
 Then, at t = 0 we have Q(0) = 10 = 1 + C, so C ≈ 9.090 and we get 
 Q(t) = (3/ln(3))t + 9.090. 
 At time t = 2, we have 
 Q(2) = (3/ln(3)) * 2 + 9.090 = 17.282. 
 After 2 hours, there are 17,282 bacteria in the dish.
summary: This section details the computation of the bacterial population function and its evaluation at t = 2 hours.

header: Modified Growth Rate and Evaluation of Bacterial Population
content: 5.35 From Example 5.42, suppose the bacteria grow at a rate of q(t) = 2t. Assume the culture still starts with 10,000 bacteria. Find Q(t). How many bacteria are in the dish after 3 hours?
summary: This section presents a new scenario of bacterial growth rate and asks for the evaluation of Q(t) after 3 hours.

header: Integration: Fruit Fly Population Growth Explained
content:
Suppose a population of fruit flies increases at a rate of g(t) = 2e^(0.02t), in flies per day. If the initial population of fruit flies is 100 flies, how many flies are in the population after 10 days?
Solution
Let G(t) represent the number of flies in the population at time t. Applying the net change theorem, we have
G(10) = G(0) + ∫ from 0 to 10 2e^(0.02t) dt = 100 + [2e^(0.02t) / 0.02] | from 0 to 10
= 100 + [100e^(0.2) - 100] ≈ 122.
There are 122 flies in the population after 10 days.
summary: This chunk discusses the mathematical modeling of fruit fly population growth using integration.

content: Suppose the rate of growth of the fly population is given by g(t) = e^(0.01t), and the initial fly population is 100 flies. How many flies are in the population after 15 days?
summary: This section introduces a new rate of growth for the fly population and poses a follow-up question.

header: Integrating Logarithmic Functions - Key Integration Rules
content:
Integrals Involving Logarithmic Functions
Integrating functions of the form f(x)= x −1 result in the absolute value of the natural log function, as shown in the following rule. Integral formulas for other logarithmic functions, such as f(x)=lnx and f(x)=logax, are also included in the rule.
Rule: Integration Formulas Involving Logarithmic Functions
The following formulas can be used to evaluate integrals involving logarithmic functions.
∫x −1 dx = ln|x|+C (5.22)
∫lnxdx = xlnx−x+C= x(lnx−1)+C
∫logaxdx = x (lnx−1)+C

content: The following exercises are intended to derive the fundamental properties of the natural log starting from the basic principles.
summary: This section introduces exercises aimed at deriving the fundamental properties of natural logarithms.

header: Integration Concepts and Identities in Chapter 5
content: Compute the right endpoint estimates definition ln(x)=∫ dt , using properties of the definite integral and making no further assumptions.
summary: This chunk introduces the computation of right endpoint estimates using the natural logarithm.

content: Use the identity ln(x)=∫ dt to derive the identity ln(1/x) = -ln(x).
summary: This section focuses on deriving the logarithmic identity involving positive fractions.

content: Use a change of variable in the integral ∫ dt to show that ln(xy) = lnx + lny for x, y > 0.
summary: This part derives the identity that connects the logarithm of a product to the sum of logarithms.

header: Increasing Nature of the Natural Logarithm
content: Use the identity lnx = ∫ dt to show that ln(x) is an increasing function of x on [0, ∞), and use the previous exercises to show that the range of ln(x) is (-∞, ∞). Without any further assumptions, conclude that ln(x) has an inverse function defined on (-∞, ∞).
summary: This section establishes that the natural logarithm is an increasing function with a defined inverse.

header: Defining an Inverse Function for Logarithms
content: Pretend, for the moment, that we do not know that e^x is the inverse function of ln(x), but keep in mind that ln(x) has an inverse function defined on (-∞, ∞). Call it E. Use the identity ln(xy) = lnx + lny to deduce that E(a+b) = E(a)E(b) for any real numbers a, b.
summary: This section introduces a hypothetical inverse function E and its properties based on logarithmic identities.

header: Derivatives of the Inverse Function E
content: Show that E'(t) = E(t).
summary: This part examines the derivative of the hypothesized inverse function E.

content: The sine integral, defined as S(x) = ∫ sint dt, is an important quantity in engineering. Although it does not have a simple closed formula, it is possible to estimate its behavior for large x. Show that for k ≥ 1, |S(2πk) - S(2π(k+1))| ≤ k(2k + 1)π.
description: Estimation of the sine integral behavior is demonstrated.
summary: This chunk discusses the significance of the sine integral and provides an estimation method for larger values.

content: The normal distribution in probability is given by p(x) = (1 / (σ √(2π))) e^(-((x - μ)²)/(2σ²)), where σ is the standard deviation and μ is the average. The standard normal distribution in probability, p_s, corresponds to μ = 0 and σ = 1.
description: Explanation of the normal distribution and its standard form.
summary: This section provides a definition of the normal distribution and its parameters.

content: Compute the right endpoint estimates of the integral ∫ from -1 to 1 of (1 / (σ√(2π))) e^(-x²/2) dx.
summary: In this section, right endpoint estimates are computed for the normal distribution integral.

header: Integration of Inverse Trigonometric Functions - Learning Objectives
content: In this section we focus on integrals that result in inverse trigonometric functions. We have worked with these functions before. Recall from Functions and Graphs that trigonometric functions are not one-to-one unless the domains are restricted. When working with inverses of trigonometric functions, we always need to be careful to take these restrictions into account. Also in Derivatives, we developed formulas for derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions. The formulas developed there give rise directly to integration formulas involving inverse trigonometric functions.
summary: Overview of the focus on integrals resulting in inverse trigonometric functions.

content:
KEY TERMS
average value of a function (or f ) the average value of a function on an interval can be found by calculating the definite integral of the function and dividing that value by the length of the interval
change of variables the substitution of a variable, such as u, for an expression in the integrand
definite integral a primary operation of calculus; the area between the curve and the x-axis over a given interval is a definite integral
fundamental theorem of calculus the theorem, central to the entire development of calculus, that establishes the relationship between differentiation and integration
fundamental theorem of calculus, part 1 uses a definite integral to define an antiderivative of a function
fundamental theorem of calculus, part 2 (also, evaluation theorem) we can evaluate a definite integral by evaluating the antiderivative of the integrand at the endpoints of the interval and subtracting
integrable function a function is integrable if the limit defining the integral exists; in other words, if the limit of the Riemann sums as n goes to infinity exists
integrand the function to the right of the integration symbol; the integrand includes the function being integrated
integration by substitution a technique for integration that allows integration of functions that are the result of a chain-rule derivative
left-endpoint approximation an approximation of the area under a curve computed by using the left endpoint of each subinterval to calculate the height of the vertical sides of each rectangle
limits of integration these values appear near the top and bottom of the integral sign and define the interval over which the function should be integrated
lower sum a sum obtained by using the minimum value of f(x) on each subinterval
mean value theorem for integrals guarantees that a point c exists such that f(c) is equal to the average value of the function
net change theorem if we know the rate of change of a quantity, the net change theorem says the future quantity is equal to the initial quantity plus the integral of the rate of change of the quantity
net signed area the area between a function and the x-axis such that the area below the x-axis is subtracted from the area above the x-axis; the result is the same as the definite integral of the function
partition a set of points that divides an interval into subintervals
regular partition a partition in which the subintervals all have the same width
riemann sum an estimate of the area under the curve of the form A≈ ∑ f(x*)Δx
i=1
right-endpoint approximation the right-endpoint approximation is an approximation of the area of the rectangles under a curve using the right endpoint of each subinterval to construct the vertical sides of each rectangle
sigma notation (also, summation notation) the Greek letter sigma (Σ) indicates addition of the values; the values of the index above and below the sigma indicate where to begin the summation and where to end it
total area total area between a function and the x-axis is calculated by adding the area above the x-axis and the area below the x-axis; the result is the same as the definite integral of the absolute value of the function
upper sum a sum obtained by using the maximum value of f(x) on each subinterval
variable of integration indicates which variable you are integrating with respect to; if it is x, then the function in the integrand is followed by dx
summary: This section contains key terms and definitions related to calculus, particularly focusing on integrals and their evaluation.

header: Integration Concepts - Fundamental Theorem of Calculus Part 2
content: If f(x) is continuous over an interval ⎡a, b⎤, and the function F(x) is defined by F(x)=∫ f(t)dt, then F′(x)= f(x).

summary: The theorem explains the relationship between the integral of a function and its change over an interval.

header: Key Concepts in Integration - Approximating Areas
content: The use of sigma (summation) notation of the form ∑ a_i is useful for expressing long sums of values in compact form.
summary: Sigma notation is introduced as a method for compactly expressing sums in the context of area approximation.

content: For a continuous function defined over an interval ⎡a, b⎤, the process of dividing the interval into equal parts, extending a rectangle to the graph of the function, calculating the areas of the series of rectangles, and then summing the areas yields an approximation of the area of that region.
summary: This chunk describes the concept of Riemann sums as a method to estimate the area under a curve.

header: Calculating Rectangle Width in Riemann Sums
content: The width of each rectangle is Δx = (b - a) / n.
summary: This chunk provides the formula for calculating the width of rectangles used in Riemann sums.

content: Riemann sums are expressions of the form ∑ f(x*) Δx, and can be used to estimate the area under the curve y = f(x). Left-and-right-endpoint approximations are special kinds of Riemann sums where the values of x* are chosen to be the left or right endpoints of the subintervals, respectively.
summary: This chunk explains Riemann sums, particularly focusing on endpoint approximations.

content: Riemann sums allow for much flexibility in choosing the set of points x*, often with an eye to obtaining a lower sum or an upper sum.
summary: This chunk highlights the options available when selecting points for Riemann sums.

content: The definite integral can be used to calculate net signed area, which is the area above the x-axis less the area below the x-axis. Net signed area can be positive, negative, or zero.
summary: This chunk defines the concept of the definite integral and its significance in calculating net signed area.

content: The component parts of the definite integral are the integrand, the variable of integration, and the limits of integration.
summary: This chunk outlines the essential components that make up a definite integral.

content: Continuous functions on a closed interval are integrable. Functions that are not continuous may still be integrable, depending on the nature of the discontinuities.
summary: This chunk discusses the conditions under which functions are considered integrable.

content: The properties of definite integrals can be used to evaluate integrals. The area under the curve of many functions can be calculated using geometric formulas.
summary: This chunk explains how the properties of definite integrals facilitate their evaluation.

content: The average value of a function can be calculated using definite integrals.
summary: This chunk describes how to compute the average value of a function through integration.

content: The Mean Value Theorem for Integrals states that for a continuous function over a closed interval, there is a value c such that f(c) equals the average value of the function.
summary: This chunk describes the Mean Value Theorem for integrals and its implication on averaged function values.

content: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 1 shows the relationship between the derivative and the integral.
summary: This chunk reveals the connection outlined in Part 1 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

content: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Part 2 is a formula for evaluating a definite integral in terms of an antiderivative of its integrand. The total area under a curve can be found using this formula.
summary: This chunk provides insight into Part 2 of the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus concerning definite integrals.

content: The net change theorem states that when a quantity changes, the final value equals the initial value plus the integral of the rate of change. Net change can be a positive number, a negative number, or zero.
summary: This chunk details the Net Change Theorem and how it relates quantities to their rates of change.

header: Calculating Area with Even and Odd Functions
content: The area under an even function over an asymmetric interval can be calculated by doubling the area over the positive x-axis. For an odd function, the integral over a symmetric interval equals zero because half the area is negative.
summary: This chunk explains how to calculate areas for even and odd functions based on their symmetry.

content: Substitution is a technique that simplifies the integration of functions that are the result of a chain-rule derivative. The term ‘substitution’ refers to changing variables or substituting the variable u and du for appropriate expressions in the integrand.
summary: This chunk introduces the substitution technique, a simplification method used in integration.

header: Altering Limits of Integration with Substitution
content: When using substitution for a definite integral, we also have to change the limits of integration.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the necessity of adjusting limits of integration when applying the substitution technique.
