content: Integration is a fundamental concept in calculus that involves finding the total or accumulated value of a quantity. It can be approached as finding the area under a curve represented by a function.
summary: This chunk introduces the basic premise of integration in calculus.

content: There are various types of integration methods, including definite and indefinite integrals. Definite integrals calculate a number that represents the area under a curve between two points, while indefinite integrals provide a function that describes the accumulation of quantities.
summary: This chunk breaks down different categories of integration.

content: The definite integral of a function f from a to b is denoted by ∫[a, b] f(x) dx, which calculates the exact area under the curve f(x) from x = a to x = b.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the concept of definite integrals.

content: An indefinite integral, represented as ∫ f(x) dx, represents the family of functions whose derivative is f(x). It includes a constant of integration, C, to account for all possible antiderivatives.
summary: This chunk discusses the nature and significance of indefinite integrals.

content: Several techniques are employed to solve integrals, including substitution, integration by parts, and partial fractions. Each technique has its own applications based on the complexity of the function being integrated.
summary: This chunk identifies various methods used for solving integrals.

content: In substitution, we change the variable of integration to simplify the integral. If we let u = g(x), then the integral can be rewritten in terms of u, transforming the limits and the function accordingly.
summary: This chunk details the substitution technique used in integration.

content: The integration by parts technique is derived from the product rule of differentiation. It is given by the formula ∫ u dv = uv - ∫ v du, where u and v are chosen functions.
summary: This chunk explains the integration by parts method.

content: Partial fraction decomposition involves breaking down a complex rational function into simpler fractions that can be integrated individually. This technique is particularly useful when the integrand is a rational function.
summary: This chunk explores the method of partial fraction decomposition in integration.

content: Integration has numerous applications, including calculating areas, determining the volumes of solids of revolution, and solving problems in physics and engineering.
summary: This chunk addresses how integration is utilized in various real-world applications.

content: Overall, integration serves as a powerful tool in mathematical analysis, providing valuable insights across various fields of study. Its techniques allow for the resolution of complex problems that involve accumulation and area calculation.
summary: This chunk summarizes the importance and implications of integration.

header: Chapter 6 Overview on Applications of Integration
content: The Hoover Dam is an engineering marvel. When Lake Mead, the reservoir behind the dam, is full, the dam withstands a great deal of force. However, water levels in the lake vary considerably as a result of droughts and varying water demands. Later in this chapter, we use definite integrals to calculate the force exerted on the dam when the reservoir is full and we examine how changing water levels affect that force (see Example 6.28). Hydrostatic force is only one of the many applications of definite integrals we explore in this chapter. From geometric applications such as surface area and volume, to physical applications such as mass and work, to growth and decay models, definite integrals are a powerful tool to help us understand and model the world around us.
summary: Introduction to the significance of definite integrals and their applications.

header: Learning Objectives for Areas between Curves
content: 6.1 | Areas between Curves Learning Objectives 6.1.1 Determine the area of a region between two curves by integrating with respect to the independent variable. 6.1.2 Find the area of a compound region. 6.1.3 Determine the area of a region between two curves by integrating with respect to the dependent variable.
summary: Outline of learning objectives focusing on area calculations between curves.

header: Expanding the Concept of Area Under Curves
content: In Introduction to Integration, we developed the concept of the definite integral to calculate the area below a curve on a given interval. In this section, we expand that idea to calculate the area of more complex regions. We start by finding the area between two curves that are functions of x, beginning with the simple case in which one function value is always greater than the other. We then look at cases when the graphs of the functions cross. Last, we consider how to calculate the area between two curves that are functions of y.
summary: Introduction to calculating areas between two curves and complexity of regions.

header: Calculating the Area Between Two Curves
content: Area of a Region between Two Curves Let f(x) and g(x) be continuous functions over an interval [a, b] such that f(x) ≥ g(x) on [a, b]. We want to find the area between the graphs of the functions, as shown in the following figure.
summary: Introduction to finding the area between two graphs of continuous functions.

header: Visual Representation of Area Between Curves
content: As we did before, we are going to partition the interval on the x-axis and approximate the area between the graphs of the functions with rectangles. So, for i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of [a, b]. Then, for i=1, 2,…, n, choose a point x* ∈[xi−1, xi], and on each interval [xi−1, xi] construct a rectangle that extends vertically from g(x*) to f(x*).
description: Figure 6.2 shows the area between the graphs of two functions, f(x) and g(x), on the interval [a, b].

header: Area Approximation Between Curves in Chapter 6 Applications of Integration
content: We can approximate the area between the graphs of two functions, f(x) and g(x), with rectangles. The height of each individual rectangle is f(x* )−g(x* ) and the width of each rectangle is Δx. Adding the areas of all the rectangles, we see that the area between the curves is approximated by A≈ ∑ ⎡f(x* )−g(x* )⎤Δx. This is a Riemann sum, so we take the limit as n→∞ and we get A= lim ∑ ⎡f(x* )−g(x* )⎤Δx=∫ ⎡f(x)−g(x)⎤dx.
description: Figure 6.3 illustrates the approximation of the area between the graphs of two functions using rectangles.
summary: This chunk explains how to approximate the area between two curves using rectangles.

header: Theorem on Finding Area Between Two Curves
content: Theorem 6.1: Finding the Area between Two Curves Let f(x) and g(x) be continuous functions such that f(x)≥g(x) over an interval ⎡a, b⎤. Let R denote the region bounded above by the graph of f(x), below by the graph of g(x), and on the left and right by the lines x=a and x=b, respectively. Then, the area of R is given by A=∫ ⎡f(x)−g(x)⎤dx.

header: Understanding Volume Measurement in Three Dimensions
content:
Learning Objectives
6.2.1 Determine the volume of a solid by integrating a cross-section (the slicing method).
6.2.2 Find the volume of a solid of revolution using the disk method.
6.2.3 Find the volume of a solid of revolution with a cavity using the washer method.
In the preceding section, we used definite integrals to find the area between two curves. In this section, we use definite integrals to find volumes of three-dimensional solids. We consider three approaches—slicing, disks, and washers—for finding these volumes, depending on the characteristics of the solid.
summary: This section introduces the learning objectives related to finding volumes of solids using different methods.

header: Volume Calculation and Slicing Method Introduction
content:
Volume and the Slicing Method
Just as area is the numerical measure of a two-dimensional region, volume is the numerical measure of a three-dimensional solid. Most of us have computed volumes of solids by using basic geometric formulas. The volume of a rectangular solid, for example, can be computed by multiplying length, width, and height: V = lwh. The formulas for the volume of a sphere, V = (4/3) πr^3, a cone, V = (1/3) πr^2 h, and a pyramid, V = (1/3) Ah, have also been introduced. Although some of these formulas were derived using geometry alone, all these formulas can be obtained by using integration.
summary: This chunk outlines the concept of volume and how it can be expressed through geometric formulas.

header: Generalizing the Concept of a Cylinder
content: We can also calculate the volume of a cylinder. Although most of us think of a cylinder as having a circular base, such as a soup can or a metal rod, in mathematics, the word cylinder has a more general meaning. To discuss cylinders in this more general context, we first need to define some vocabulary. We define the cross-section of a solid to be the intersection of a plane with the solid. A cylinder is defined as any solid that can be generated by translating a plane region along a line perpendicular to the region, called the axis of the cylinder. Thus, all cross-sections perpendicular to the axis of a cylinder are identical.
summary: This section defines the cylinder in a mathematical context and introduces the concept of cross-sections.

content: The solid shown in Figure 6.11 is an example of a cylinder with a non-circular base. To calculate the volume of a cylinder, then, we simply multiply the area of the cross-section by the height of the cylinder: V = A·h. In the case of a right circular cylinder (soup can), this becomes V = πr^2 h.
description: Figure 6.11 illustrates a cylinder with a non-circular base, showing that all cross-sections are identical.
summary: This chunk explains how to calculate the volume of a cylinder based on its cross-sectional area.

content: If a solid does not have a constant cross-section (and it is not one of the other basic solids), we may not have a formula for its volume. In this case, we can use a definite integral to calculate the volume of the solid. We do this by slicing the solid into pieces, estimating the volume of each slice, and then adding those estimated volumes together. The slices should all be parallel to one another, and when we put all the slices together, we should get the whole solid.
summary: This chunk discusses the process of calculating the volume of irregular solids using the slicing method.

content: Consider, for example, the solid S shown in Figure 6.12, extending along the x-axis.
description: Figure 6.12 likely provides a visual representation of a solid being sliced along the x-axis for volume calculation.
summary: This section invites the reader to visualize an example of a solid being sliced for volume calculation.

header: Exploring the Concept of Slicing Solids in Integration
content: We want to divide S into slices perpendicular to the x-axis. As we see later in the chapter, there may be times when we want to slice the solid in some other direction—say, with slices perpendicular to the y-axis. The decision of which way to slice the solid is very important. If we make the wrong choice, the computations can get quite messy. Later in the chapter, we examine some of these situations in detail and look at how to decide which way to slice the solid. For the purposes of this section, however, we use slices perpendicular to the x-axis.
summary: This section introduces the concept of slicing solids for integration.

content: Because the cross-sectional area is not constant, we let A(x) represent the area of the cross-section at point x. Now let P = { x0, x1, ..., Xn } be a regular partition of [a, b], and for i = 1, 2, …, n, let Si represent the slice of S stretching from xi-1 to xi. The following figure shows the sliced solid with n=3.
description: Figure 6.12 A solid with a varying cross-section showing the division of the solid into slices.
summary: This section discusses the representation of cross-sectional areas and the partition of intervals.

content: Finally, for i = 1, 2, …, n, let x* be an arbitrary point in [xi-1, xi]. Then the volume of slice Si can be estimated by V(S) ≈ A(x*)Δx. Adding these approximations together, we see the volume of the entire solid S can be approximated by V(S) ≈ ∑ A(x*)Δx.
description: Figure 6.13 The solid S has been divided into three slices perpendicular to the x-axis, illustrating the sliced sections.
summary: This section estimates the volume of a solid using its cross-sectional areas.

content: By now, we can recognize this as a Riemann sum, and our next step is to take the limit as n→∞. Then we have V(S) = lim n→∞ ∑ A(x*)Δx = ∫ A(x)dx.
summary: This section concludes the estimation of volume by transitioning to a Riemann sum formulation.

header: Understanding the Slicing Method for Volume Calculation
content: The technique we have just described is called the slicing method. To apply it, we use the following strategy. Problem-Solving Strategy: Finding Volumes by the Slicing Method 1. Examine the solid and determine the shape of a cross-section of the solid. It is often helpful to draw a picture if one is not provided. 2. Determine a formula for the area of the cross-section. 3. Integrate the area formula over the appropriate interval to get the volume. Recall that in this section, we assume the slices are perpendicular to the x-axis. Therefore, the area formula is in terms of x and the limits of integration lie on the x-axis. However, the problem-solving strategy shown here is valid regardless of how we choose to slice the solid.
summary: This section outlines the slicing method strategy for calculating volumes.

header: Deriving Volume Formula for a Pyramid Using Slicing Method
content: Example 6.6 Deriving the Formula for the Volume of a Pyramid We know from geometry that the formula for the volume of a pyramid is V = (1/3)Ah. If the pyramid has a square base, this becomes V = (1/3)a²h, where a denotes the length of one side of the base. We are going to use the slicing method to derive this formula.
summary: The example demonstrates the derivation of a pyramid's volume using the slicing method.

header: Setting Up the Integral for Pyramid Volume Calculation
content: Solution We want to apply the slicing method to a pyramid with a square base. To set up the integral, consider the pyramid shown in Figure 6.14, oriented along the x-axis.
description: Figure 6.14 (a) A pyramid with a square base is oriented along the x-axis. (b) A two-dimensional view of the pyramid is seen from the side.
summary: This section details the setup for applying the slicing method to a pyramid.

header: Determining Cross-Sectional Area of the Pyramid
content: We first want to determine the shape of a cross-section of the pyramid. We know the base is a square, so the cross-sections are squares as well (step 1). Now we want to determine a formula for the area of one of these cross-sectional squares. Looking at Figure 6.14(b), and using a proportion, since these are similar triangles, we have sx/a = s/h or s = (a/h)x.
summary: This section explains how to determine the area of the pyramid's cross-section.

header: Introduction to the Disk Method - Chapter 6
content: When we use the slicing method with solids of revolution, it is often called the disk method because, for solids of revolution, the slices used to overapproximate the volume of the solid are disks. To see this, consider the solid of revolution generated by revolving the region between the graph of the function f(x)=(x−1)² +1 and the x-axis over the interval [−1, 3] around the x-axis.

header: Understanding Volume Calculation Through Disk Method
content: We already used the formal Riemann sum development of the volume formula when we developed the slicing method. We know that b V = ∫ A(x) dx. The only difference with the disk method is that we know the formula for the cross-sectional area ahead of time; it is the area of a circle. This gives the following rule.

header: Exploring the Washer Method for Complex Solids
content: Some solids of revolution have cavities in the middle; they are not solid all the way to the axis of revolution. Sometimes, this is just a result of the way the region of revolution is shaped with respect to the axis of revolution. In other cases, cavities arise when the region of revolution is defined as the region between the graphs of two functions. A third way this can happen is when an axis of revolution other than the x-axis or y-axis is selected.
summary: The section discusses conditions under which solids of revolution can have cavities.

header: The Concept of Washers in Volume Calculations
content: When the solid of revolution has a cavity in the middle, the slices used to approximate the volume are not disks, but washers (disks with holes in the center).
summary: This part elaborates on how cavities transform volume slices from disks to washers.

header: Example of Washer Method in Action
content: For example, consider the region bounded above by the graph of the function f(x)= x and below by the graph of the function g(x)=1 over the interval [1, 4]. When this region is revolved around the x-axis, the result is a solid with a cavity in the middle, and the slices are washers.
description: Figure 6.22(a) and (b) show the graph of the functions and a representative washer.
summary: This section provides a specific example illustrating the washer method.

(c) The region between the curves over the given interval. (d) The resulting solid of revolution. The cross-sectional area, then, is the area of the outer circle less the area of the inner circle. In this case, A(x)=π( x)2 −π(1)2 =π(x−1). Then the volume of the solid is V =∫ A(x)dx from a to b =∫ 4 π(x−1)dx=π ⎡ x 2 −x ⎤ | 4 = 9 πunits 3 ⎣2 ⎦ 1 2 1.

content: Generalizing this process gives the washer method. Rule: The Washer Method Suppose f(x) and g(x) are continuous, nonnegative functions such that f(x)≥g(x) over ⎡a, b⎤. Let R denote the region bounded above by the graph of f(x), below by the graph of g(x), on the left by the line x=a, and on the right by the line x=b.

header: Applying Washer Method Around the y-axis
content: As with the disk method, we can also apply the washer method to solids of revolution that result from revolving a region around the y-axis. In this case, the following rule applies.
summary: This chunk discusses the application of the washer method for rotations around the y-axis.

header: Understanding the Washer Method for Solids of Revolution - Applications of Integration
content: Suppose u(y) and v(y) are continuous, nonnegative functions such that v(y) ≤ u(y) for y ∈ [c, d]. Let Q denote the region bounded on the right by the graph of u(y), on the left by the graph of v(y), below by the line y = c, and above by the line y = d. Then, the volume of the solid of revolution formed by revolving Q around the y-axis is given by V = ∫ from c to d π [ (u(y))^2 - (v(y))^2 ] dy.
summary: This section introduces the Washer Method for calculating volumes of solids of revolution around the y-axis.

header: Application of Washer Method with Different Axis of Revolution - Applications of Integration
content: Rather than looking at an example of the washer method with the y-axis as the axis of revolution, we now consider an example in which the axis of revolution is a line other than one of the two coordinate axes. The same general method applies, but you may have to visualize just how to describe the cross-sectional area of the volume.
summary: This section discusses how the Washer Method can be applied when the axis of revolution is not one of the coordinate axes.

header: Understanding the Volume of Solids of Revolution - Integration Applications
content: coordinate axes. However, we still know that the area of the cross-section is the area of the outer circle less the area of the inner circle. Looking at the graph of the function, we see the radius of the outer circle is given by f(x)+2, which simplifies to f(x)+2=(4−x)+2=6−x. The radius of the inner circle is g(x)=2. Therefore, we have V =∫ π ⎡ (6−x)2 −(2)2⎤ dx =π∫ ⎛ x 2 −12x+32 ⎞ dx =π x −6x 2 +32x | = 160π units³.
summary: This chunk explains how to calculate the volume of a solid of revolution using integration.

content: In this section, we examine the method of cylindrical shells, the final method for finding the volume of a solid of revolution. We can use this method on the same kinds of solids as the disk method or the washer method; however, with the disk and washer methods, we integrate along the coordinate axis parallel to the axis of revolution. With the method of cylindrical shells, we integrate along the coordinate axis perpendicular to the axis of revolution. The ability to choose which variable of integration we want to use can be a significant advantage with more complicated functions. Also, the specific geometry of the solids sometimes makes the method of using cylindrical shells more appealing than using the washer method. In the last part of this section, we review all the methods for finding volume that we have studied and layout some guidelines to help you determine which method to use in a given situation.
summary: This section introduces the method of cylindrical shells for calculating volumes of solids of revolution.

content: Again, we are working with a solid of revolution. As before, we define a region R, bounded above by the graph of a function y = f(x), below by the x-axis, and on the left and right by the lines x = a and x = b, respectively, as shown in Figure 6.25(a). We then revolve this region around the y-axis, as shown in Figure 6.25(b). Note that this is different from what we have done before. Previously, regions defined in terms of functions of x were revolved around the x-axis or a line parallel to it.
description: Figure 6.25 shows (a) a region bounded by the graph of a function of x and (b) the solid of revolution formed when the region is revolved around the y-axis.
summary: This chunk describes the setup for applying the cylindrical shells method for solids of revolution.

header: Partitioning the Interval for Cylindrical Shells
content: As we have done many times before, partition the interval ⎡a, b⎤ using a regular partition, P = {x0, x1,…, xn} and, for i = 1, 2,…, n, choose a point x* ∈ [xi-1, xi]. Then, construct a rectangle over the interval [xi-1, xi] of height f(x*) and width Δx. A representative rectangle is shown in Figure 6.26(a). When that rectangle is revolved around the y-axis, instead of a disk or a washer, we get a cylindrical shell, as shown in the following figure.
description: Figure 6.26 shows a representative rectangle for the cylindrical shell method, which will form a cylindrical shell when revolved around the y-axis.
summary: This chunk illustrates how to partition the interval for the cylindrical shells method.

content: Figure 6.26 (a) A representative rectangle. (b) When this rectangle is revolved around the y-axis, the result is a cylindrical shell. (c) When we put all the shells together, we get an approximation of the original solid.
description: Figure 6.26 illustrates a rectangle that, when revolved around the y-axis, forms a cylindrical shell, subsequently showing how these shells can approximate the original solid shape.
summary: This chunk explains how a rectangle creates a cylindrical shell upon revolution.

content: To calculate the volume of this shell, consider Figure 6.27. The shell is a cylinder, so its volume is the cross-sectional area multiplied by the height of the cylinder. The cross-sections are annuli (ring-shaped regions—essentially, circles with a hole in the center), with outer radius x_i and inner radius x_{i-1}. Thus, the cross-sectional area is πx_i^2 − πx_{i-1}^2.
description: Figure 6.27 is referenced for calculating the volume of the shell, which involves understanding the cross-sectional area defined by the outer and inner radii.
summary: This chunk discusses how to find the volume of a cylindrical shell using cross-sectional areas.

header: Deriving the Volume Formula for Shells
content: The height of the cylinder is f(x*). Then the volume of the shell is V = f(x*)(πx_i^2 − πx_{i-1}^2). This simplifies to V = πf(x*)(x_i^2 − x_{i-1}^2) = πf(x*)(x_i + x_{i-1})(x_i − x_{i-1}). Note that x_i − x_{i-1} = Δx, so we have V = 2πf(x*)(Δx).
summary: This chunk elaborates on deriving the volume formula for cylindrical shells using a specific function.

header: Understanding Volume Calculation using Cylindrical Shells
content: V =2πf(x* ) i i−1 Δx. Furthermore, i i−1 is both the midpoint of the interval [x , x] and the average radius of the shell, and we can approximate this by x* . We then have V ≈2πf(x* )x* Δx.
summary: This section introduces the volume formula for cylindrical shells and how to approximate the average radius.

content: Another way to think of this is to think of making a vertical cut in the shell and then opening it up to form a flat plate (Figure 6.28).
description: Figure 6.28 consists of two parts: (a) showing a vertical cut in a representative shell, and (b) illustrating the flattened shell formed into a flat plate.
summary: This section describes a visual method to understand the volume calculation of cylindrical shells.

content: In reality, the outer radius of the shell is greater than the inner radius, and hence the back edge of the plate would be slightly longer than the front edge of the plate. However, we can approximate the flattened shell by a flat plate of height f(x*), width 2πx*, and thickness Δx (Figure 6.28). The volume of the shell, then, is approximately the volume of the flat plate. Multiplying the height, width, and depth of the plate, we get V ≈ f(x*)⎛2πx* ⎞Δx.
summary: This section approximates the volume of a cylindrical shell based on the dimensions of a corresponding flat plate.

header: Summing the Volumes of All Shells
content: To calculate the volume of the entire solid, we then add the volumes of all the shells and obtain n V ≈ ∑ ⎛2πx* f(x* )Δx⎞. i=1 Here we have another Riemann sum, this time for the function 2πxf(x). Taking the limit as n→∞ gives us n b V = lim ∑ ⎛2πx* f(x* )Δx⎞=∫ ⎛2πxf(x)⎞dx. n→∞ ⎝ i i ⎠ a ⎝ ⎠ i=1.
summary: This section discusses how to derive the volume of a solid of revolution by summing the volumes of all cylindrical shells.

header: The Method of Cylindrical Shells - Rule Definition
content: This leads to the following rule for the method of cylindrical shells. Rule: The Method of Cylindrical Shells Let f(x) be continuous and nonnegative. Define R as the region bounded above by the graph of f(x), below by the x-axis, on the left by the line x=a, and on the right by the line x=b. Then the volume of the solid of revolution...
summary: This section provides the formal rule for employing the method of cylindrical shells in volume calculations.

header: Applications of Integration: Rule for Solids of Revolution around the x-axis
content: The analogous rule for this type of solid is given here. Rule: The Method of Cylindrical Shells for Solids of Revolution around the x-axis. Let g(y) be continuous and nonnegative. Define Q as the region bounded on the right by the graph of g(y), on the left by the y-axis, below by the line y=c, and above by the line y=d. Then, the volume of the solid of revolution can be calculated similarly.
summary: This section states the rule for applying the method of cylindrical shells to solids of revolution around the x-axis.

content: The volume of the solid formed by revolving Q around the x-axis is given by dV = ∫ (2πyg(y)) dy.

content: Example 6.15 A Region of Revolution Revolved around a Line Define R as the region bounded above by the graph of f(x)= x and below by the x-axis over the interval [1, 2]. Find the volume of the solid of revolution formed by revolving R around the line x=−1. Solution First, graph the region R and the associated solid of revolution, as shown in the following figure.

header: Understanding Solids of Revolution in Integration Applications
content: Figure 6.32 (a) The region R between the graph of f(x) and the x-axis over the interval [1, 2]. (b) The solid of revolution generated by revolving R around the line x=−1. Note that the radius of a shell is given by x+1. Then the volume of the solid is given by V = ∫ (2π(x+1)f(x))dx from 1 to 2.
description: Figure 6.32 illustrates the region R between f(x) and the x-axis, and the solid formed by revolving this region around the line x = -1.
summary: This chunk discusses how to calculate the volume of solids of revolution generated from a specific region.

content: The volume of the solid is given by V = ∫ (2π(x+1)x)dx from 1 to 2 = 2π∫ (x^2 + x)dx from 1 to 2.
summary: This section continues the calculation of volume using integration techniques.

header: Final Volume Calculation for Solid of Revolution
content: Evaluating the integral, we find V = 2π [x^3/3 + x^2/2] evaluated from 1 to 2 = 2π [ (8/3 + 2) - (1/3 + 1/2) ] = 2π [23/6] = 23π/3 units.
summary: This part completes the volume calculation for the solid of revolution.

header: Integrating to Find Volumes - Applications of Integration
content: Looking at the region, if we want to integrate with respect to x, we would have to break the integral into two pieces, because we have different functions bounding the region over [0, 1] and [1, 2]. In this case, using the disk method, we would have V = ∫(πx^2)dx + ∫(π(2−x)^2)dx from 0 to 1.
summary: This section discusses how to set up integrals to find volumes using different methods.

header: Using the Shell Method for Volume Calculation - Applications of Integration
content: If we used the shell method instead, we would use functions of y to represent the curves, producing V = ∫(2πy[(2−y)−y])dy from 0 to 1 = ∫(2πy[2−2y])dy.

header: Comparing Volume Calculation Methods - Applications of Integration
content: Neither of these integrals is particularly onerous, but since the shell method requires only one integral, and the integrand requires less simplification, we should probably go with the shell method in this case.
summary: This section evaluates the advantages of using the shell method over the disk method for volume calculation.

header: Volume Approximation of Common Objects - Shell Method
content: For the following exercises, use the method of shells to approximate the volumes of some common objects, which are pictured in accompanying figures.

header: Volume Calculation Using Shell Method - Ellipse Example
content: Use the method of shells to find the volume of an ellipse (x^2/a^2) + (y^2/b^2) = 1 rotated around the x-axis.

header: Exploration of Solid Volume Generation through Integration - Applications of Integration
content: Consider the region enclosed by the graphs of y= f(x), y=1+ f(x), x=0, y=0, and x=a>0. What is the volume of the solid generated when this region is rotated around the y-axis? Assume that the function is defined over the interval [0, a].
summary: Determines volume generated by rotating a specific region around the y-axis.

header: Volume Calculation Techniques - Applications of Integration
content: Consider the function y= f(x), which decreases from f(0)=b to f(1)=0. Set up the integrals for determining the volume, using both the shell method and the disk method, of the solid generated when this region, with x=0 and y=0, is rotated around the y-axis.
summary: Introduces two methods to calculate volume generated by rotation.

header: Proving Volume Approximation Equality - Applications of Integration
content: Prove that both methods approximate the same volume. Which method is easier to apply? (Hint: Since f(x) is one-to-one, there exists an inverse f −1 (y).)
summary: Encourages proving equal volume results from both methods while hinting at the function's properties.

header: Overview of Arc Length Calculation in Chapter 6
content: In this section, we use definite integrals to find the arclength of a curve. We can think of arclength as the distance you would travel if you were walking along the path of the curve. Many real-world applications involve arclength. If a rocket is launched along a parabolic path, we might want to know how far the rocket travels. Or, if a curve on a map represents a road, we might want to know how far we have to drive to reach our destination. We begin by calculating the arclength of curves defined as functions of x, then we examine the same process for curves defined as functions of y. (The process is identical, with the roles of x and y reversed.) The techniques we use to find arc length can be extended to find the surface area of a surface of revolution, and we close the section with an examination of this concept.
summary: This section introduces the concept of arc length in the context of integration and its applications.

header: Arc Length Calculation for the Curve y = f(x)
content: In previous applications of integration, we required the function f(x) to be integrable, or at most continuous. However, for calculating arclength we have a more stringent requirement for f(x). Here, we require f(x) to be differentiable, and furthermore we require its derivative, f′(x), to be continuous. Functions like this, which have continuous derivatives, are called smooth. (This property comes up again in later chapters.) Let f(x) be a smooth function defined over ⎡a, b⎤. We want to calculate the length of the curve from the point ⎛a, f(a)⎞ to the point ⎛b, f(b)⎞. We start by using line segments to approximate the length of the curve.
summary: This chunk outlines the requirements for the function f(x) needed to calculate the arc length.

header: Constructing Line Segments for Arc Length Approximation
content: For i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of ⎡a, b⎤. Then, for i=1, 2,…, n, construct a line segment from the point ⎛x, f(x )⎞ to the point ⎛x, f(x)⎞. Although it might seem logical to use either horizontal or vertical line segments, we want our line segments to approximate the curve as closely as possible.
description: Figure 6.37 depicts the process of approximating the length of a curve by adding line segments.
summary: This section discusses the method of constructing line segments to approximate the curve during arc length calculation.

header: Understanding Vertical and Horizontal Distance Changes in Arc Length
content: To help us find the length of each line segment, we look at the change in vertical distance as well as the change in horizontal distance over each interval. Because we have used a regular partition, the change in horizontal distance over each interval is given by Δx. The change in vertical distance varies from interval to interval, though, so we use Δy = f(x)− f(x ) to represent the change in vertical distance over the interval [x , x]. Note that some (or all) Δy may be negative.
description: Figure 6.38 illustrates the concept of vertical and horizontal distance changes during the arc length calculation.
summary: This chunk explains how to calculate the changes in vertical and horizontal distances for arc length.

header: Understanding Arc Length Through Segments - Applications of Integration
content: By the Pythagorean theorem, the length of the line segment is (Δx)2 +⎛Δy⎞2. We can also write this as Δx 1+⎛⎛Δy⎞/(Δx)⎞2. Now, by the Mean Value Theorem, there is a point x* ∈[x_i−1, x_i] such that f′(x* )=⎛Δy⎞/(Δx). Then the length of the line segment is given by Δx 1+⎡f′(x* )⎤2.
description: Figure 6.38 shows a representative line segment that approximates the curve over the interval [x_i−1, x_i].
summary: This section discusses how to estimate the arc length of a curve using segments based on the Pythagorean theorem.

header: Approximating Arc Length with Riemann Sums - Applications of Integration
content: Adding up the lengths of all the line segments, we get n Arc Length ≈ ∑ 1+⎡f′(x* )⎤2 Δx. This is a Riemann sum. Taking the limit as n→∞, we have n b Arc Length= lim ∑ 1+⎡f′(x* )⎤2 Δx=∫ 1+⎡f′(x)⎤2 dx.
summary: This section explains how to express the arc length as a limit of Riemann sums.

header: Theorem on Arc Length of a Smooth Function - Applications of Integration
content: We summarize these findings in the following theorem. Theorem 6.4: Arc Length for y=f(x) Let f(x) be a smooth function over the interval ⎡a, b⎤. Then the arc length of the portion of the graph of f(x) from the point ⎛a, f(a)⎞ to the point ⎛b, f(b)⎞ is given by Arc Length=∫ 1+⎡f′(x)⎤2 dx.
summary: This section presents a theorem defining the arc length for smooth functions.

header: Example Calculation of Arc Length - Applications of Integration
content: Example 6.18 Calculating the Arc Length of a Function of x Let f(x)=2x^(3/2). Calculate the arc length of the graph of f(x) over the interval [0, 1]. Round the answer to three decimal places.
summary: This example demonstrates how to calculate the arc length of a specific function over a given interval.

content: We have f′(x)=3x 1/2 , so ⎡f′(x)⎤2 =9x. Then, the arc length is ⎣ ⎦ b Arc Length =∫ 1+⎡f′(x)⎤2 dx ⎣ ⎦ a 1 =∫ 1+9xdx. 0 Substitute u=1+9x. Then, du=9dx. When x=0, then u=1, and when x=1, then u=10.
summary: This chunk introduces the calculation of arc length using a derivative.
This chunk provides the detailed steps for evaluating the arc length integral.

header: Example Calculation - Arc Length of a Function
content: 6.18 Let f(x)=(4/3)x 3/2 . Calculate the arc length of the graph of f(x) over the interval [0, 1]. Round the answer to three decimal places. Although it is nice to have a formula for calculating arc length, this particular theorem can generate expressions that are difficult to integrate. We study some techniques for integration in Introduction to Techniques of Integration.
summary: This section presents a specific problem related to the calculation of arc length.

header: Using Technology for Arc Length Calculation
content: Example 6.19 Using a Computer or Calculator to Determine the Arc Length of a Function of x Let f(x)= x 2 . Calculate the arc length of the graph of f(x) over the interval [1, 3]. Solution We have f′(x)=2x, so ⎡f′(x)⎤2 =4x 2 . Then the arc length is given by ⎣ ⎦ b 3 Arc Length=∫ 1+⎡f′(x)⎤2 dx=∫ 1+4x 2 dx. ⎣ ⎦ a 1 Using a computer to approximate the value of this integral, we get 3 ∫ 1+4x 2 dx≈8.26815.
summary: This example illustrates the use of a computer to compute arc length.

header: Application Problem - Arc Length of Sine Function
content: 6.19 Let f(x)=sinx. Calculate the arc length of the graph of f(x) over the interval [0, π]. Use a computer or calculator to approximate the value of the integral.
summary: This problem challenges the reader to calculate the arc length of the sine function.

header: Arc Length of the Curve - Introduction to Integration Applications
content: We have just seen how to approximate the length of a curve with line segments. If we want to find the arc length of the graph of a function of y, we can repeat the same process, except we partition the y-axis instead of the x-axis. Figure 6.39 shows a representative line segment.
description: Figure 6.39 illustrates a representative line segment over the interval [y_{i-1}, y_{i}].
summary: This section introduces the concept of arc length for functions in terms of the y-axis.

content: Then the length of the line segment is ⎛Δy⎞² + ⎛Δx⎞², which can also be written as Δy √(1 + (⎛Δx⎞/⎛Δy⎞)²). If we now follow the same development we did earlier, we get a formula for arc length of a function x = g(y).
summary: This chunk derives the arc length formula based on line segments.

header: Theorem 6.5 - Arc Length for x = g(y)
content: Let g(y) be a smooth function over an interval [c, d]. Then, the arc length of the graph of g(y) from the point (c, g(c)) to the point (d, g(d)) is given by Arc Length = ∫ (1 + [g′(y)]²) dy from c to d.
summary: This theorem provides a formal definition of the arc length for functions in terms of calculus.

content: Let g(y) = 3y³. Calculate the arc length of the graph of g(y) over the interval [1, 2]. Solution: We have g′(y) = 9y², so [g′(y)]² = 81y⁴. Then the arc length is Arc Length = ∫ (1 + [g′(y)]²) dy = ∫ (1 + 81y⁴) dy from 1 to 2.
summary: This example demonstrates how to calculate the arc length of a specific function over a given interval.

content: Using a computer to approximate the value of this integral, we obtain Arc Length ≈ 21.0277.
summary: This section provides an approximation of the integral to find the arc length.

content: Let g(y)=1/y. Calculate the arc length of the graph of g(y) over the interval [1, 4]. Use a computer or calculator to approximate the value of the integral.
summary: This chunk discusses calculating the arc length of the function g(y) over a specified interval.

header: Understanding Surface Area of Revolution Concepts
content: The concepts we used to find the arc length of a curve can be extended to find the surface area of a surface of revolution. Surface area is the total area of the outer layer of an object. For objects such as cubes or bricks, the surface area of the object is the sum of the areas of all of its faces. For curved surfaces, the situation is a little more complex.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of surface area of revolution and its complexity compared to flat surfaces.

header: Finding Surface Area of Revolution with Functions
content: Let f(x) be a nonnegative smooth function over the interval [a, b]. We wish to find the surface area of the surface of revolution created by revolving the graph of y = f(x) around the x-axis as shown in the following figure.
description: Figure 6.40 (a) A curve representing the function f(x). (b) The surface of revolution formed by revolving the graph of f(x) around the x-axis.
summary: This chunk explains the surface area of revolution for a smooth function when revolved around the x-axis.

header: Approximating Surface Area with Line Segments
content: As we have done many times before, we are going to partition the interval [a, b] and approximate the surface area by calculating the surface area of simpler shapes. We start by using line segments to approximate the curve, as we did earlier in this section. For i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of [a, b]. Then, for i=1, 2,…, n, construct a line segment from the point (x_{i-1}, f(x_{i-1})) to the point (x_{i}, f(x_{i})).
summary: This chunk details how to approximate the surface area using line segments across a partitioned interval.

content: Now, revolve these line segments around the x-axis to generate an approximation of the surface of revolution as shown in the following figure.
description: Figure 6.41 (a) Approximating f(x) with line segments. (b) The surface of revolution formed by revolving the line segments around the x-axis.
summary: This chunk explains the process of revolving line segments around the x-axis to approximate the surface of revolution.

content: Notice that when each line segment is revolved around the axis, it produces a band. These bands are actually pieces of cones.
summary: This chunk describes the geometric interpretation of the bands formed when line segments are revolved.

header: Chapter 6 Applications of Integration - Introduction to Frustums
content: A frustum of a cone is a shape that resembles an ice cream cone with the pointy end cut off.
summary: This section introduces the concept of a frustum of a cone.

header: Calculating Surface Area of a Frustum - Lateral Surface Area
content: To find the surface area of the band, we need to find the lateral surface area, S, of the frustum (the area of just the slanted outside surface of the frustum, not including the areas of the top or bottom faces). Let r1 and r2 be the radii of the wide end and the narrow end of the frustum, respectively, and let l be the slant height of the frustum as shown in the following figure.
summary: This section describes how to calculate the lateral surface area of a frustum.

header: Figure Illustration - Frustum of a Cone
description: Figure 6.42 illustrates a frustum of a cone and how it can approximate a small part of surface area.
summary: This figure provides a visual representation of a frustum of a cone.

header: Lateral Surface Area Formula for Cones
content: We know the lateral surface area of a cone is given by Lateral Surface Area = πrs, where r is the radius of the base of the cone and s is the slant height (see the following figure).
summary: This section presents the formula for the lateral surface area of a cone.

header: Figure Illustration - Lateral Surface Area Formula
description: Figure 6.43 shows the lateral surface area of a cone, represented by the formula πrs.
summary: This figure illustrates the formula for the lateral surface area of a cone.

header: Surface Area Calculation for Frustums - Cone Subtraction
content: Since a frustum can be thought of as a piece of a cone, the lateral surface area of the frustum is given by the lateral surface area of the whole cone less the lateral surface area of the smaller cone (the pointy tip) that was cut off (see the following figure).
summary: This section explains how to calculate the lateral surface area of a frustum by subtracting the smaller cone's area.

header: Figures Illustration - Lateral Surface Area of the Frustum
description: The figure referenced shows the relationship between the whole cone and the smaller cone which is cut off.
summary: This figure visualizes the concept of subtracting the smaller cone's area from the whole cone's area to find the frustum's lateral surface area.

description: Figure 6.44 illustrates the calculation of the lateral surface area of a frustum of a cone.
summary: This section discusses the process for calculating the lateral surface area of a frustum using geometric relationships.

header: Summary of Surface Area Calculation Formula
content: Let’s now use this formula to calculate the surface area of each of the bands formed by revolving the line segments around the x-axis. A representative band is shown in the following figure.
description: No figures or tables are included in this part.
summary: This section introduces the application of the surface area formula to bands formed by revolving line segments.

header: Understanding Surface Area Calculation in Integration
content: Figure 6.45 A representative band used for determining surface area. Note that the slant height of this frustum is just the length of the lines segment used to generate it. So, applying the surface area formula, we have S = π(r₁ + r₂)l = π(f(xᵢ−1) + f(xᵢ))Δx + (Δy)².
description: Figure 6.45 illustrates a band used to calculate surface area, supporting the explanation of integration applications.
summary: This chunk introduces the calculation of surface area using integration.

header: Applying the Mean Value Theorem to Surface Area
content: Now, as we did in the development of the arc length formula, we apply the Mean Value Theorem to select x* ∈ [xᵢ−1, xᵢ] such that f′(x*) = (Δyᵢ)/(Δx). This gives us S = π(f(xᵢ−1) + f(xᵢ))Δx(1 + (f′(x*))²).
summary: This chunk details the application of the Mean Value Theorem in calculating surface area.

header: Understanding Continuous Functions and Intermediate Value Theorem
content: Furthermore, since f(x) is continuous, by the Intermediate Value Theorem, there is a point x** ∈ [xᵢ−1, xᵢ] such that f(x**) = (1/2)[f(xᵢ−1) + f(xᵢ)], so we get S = 2πf(x**)Δx(1 + (f′(x*))²).
summary: This chunk explains the importance of continuous functions in deriving the surface area formula.

header: Approximation of Surface Area of Revolution
content: Then the approximate surface area of the whole surface of revolution is given by n Surface Area ≈ ∑ 2πf(x**)Δx(1 + (f′(x*))²). This almost looks like a Riemann sum, except we have functions evaluated at two different points, x* and x**, over the interval [xᵢ−1, xᵢ].
summary: This chunk approximates the surface area using a summation approach and relates it to Riemann sums.

header: Limit and Final Surface Area Formula
content: Taking the limit as n→∞, we get Surface Area = lim (n→∞) ∑ 2πf(x**)Δx(1 + (f′(x*))²) = ∫(a to b) (2πf(x)(1 + (f′(x))²)) dx.
summary: This chunk defines the limit process that leads to the final surface area formula.

header: Surface Area of Revolution about the Y-axis
content: As with arc length, we can conduct a similar development for functions of y to get a formula for the surface area of surfaces of revolution about the y-axis. These findings are summarized in the following theorem.
summary: This chunk transitions to the application of the same principles for surfaces of revolution about the y-axis.

header: Understanding Surface Area of Revolution - Theorem 6.6
content: Let f(x) be a nonnegative smooth function over the interval [a, b]. Then, the surface area of the surface of revolution formed by revolving the graph of f(x) around the x-axis is given by Surface Area = ∫ from a to b (2πf(x) / √(1 + (f'(x))^2)) dx.
summary: This section presents the theorem for calculating the surface area of a surface of revolution around the x-axis.

header: Surface Area of Revolution Around the Y-Axis
content: Similarly, let g(y) be a nonnegative smooth function over the interval [c, d]. Then, the surface area of the surface of revolution formed by revolving the graph of g(y) around the y-axis is given by Surface Area = ∫ from c to d (2πg(y) / √(1 + (g'(y))^2)) dy.
summary: This section describes the formula for the surface area of revolution around the y-axis.

header: Practical Example of Surface Area Calculation
content: Example 6.21: Calculating the Surface Area of a Surface of Revolution 1 Let f(x) = x over the interval [1, 4]. Find the surface area of the surface generated by revolving the graph of f(x) around the x-axis. Round the answer to three decimal places.
description: Figure 6.46 (a) shows the graph of f(x), and (b) illustrates the surface of revolution generated by this graph.
summary: This example illustrates how to calculate the surface area of revolution using a specific function.

content: Solution: The graph of f(x) and the surface of rotation are shown in the following figure. We have f(x) = x. Then, f’(x) = 1/(2x) and (f’(x))^2 = 1/(4x).
summary: This section provides the calculations required to determine the surface area in the example.

content:

content:
6.5 | Physical Applications
Learning Objectives
6.5.1 Determine the mass of a one-dimensional object from its linear density function.
6.5.2 Determine the mass of a two-dimensional circular object from its radial density function.
6.5.3 Calculate the work done by a variable force acting along a line.
6.5.4 Calculate the work done in pumping a liquid from one height to another.
6.5.5 Find the hydrostatic force against a submerged vertical plate.
summary: This section outlines the learning objectives for exploring physical applications of integration.

header: Introduction to Physical Applications of Integration
content: In this section, we examine some physical applications of integration. Let's begin with a look at calculating mass from a density function. We then turn our attention to work, and close the section with a study of hydrostatic force.
summary: An introductory overview of the physical applications of integration.

header: Calculating Mass from a Density Function
content:
Mass and Density
We can use integration to develop a formula for calculating mass based on a density function. First we consider a thin rod or wire. Orient the rod so it aligns with the x-axis, with the left end of the rod at x=a and the right end of the rod at x=b.
summary: Introduction to the concept of calculating mass using a density function for a thin rod.

header: Visual Representation of a Thin Rod for Mass Calculation
content: Notethatalthoughwedepicttherodwithsomethicknessinthefigures,formathematicalpurposesweassumetherodisthin enoughtobecalculatedasone-dimensionalobject.
description: Figure 6.48 illustrates how to calculate the mass of a thin rod oriented along the x-axis by integrating its density function.
summary: Clarification on the mathematical treatment of a thin rod in mass calculations.

header: Mass of a Rod with Constant Density
content: If the rod has constant density ρ, given in terms of mass per unit length, then the mass of the rod is just the product of the density and the length of the rod: (b−a)ρ.
summary: Calculation of the mass of a rod with constant density.

content: If the density of the rod is not constant, however, the problem becomes a little more challenging. When the density of the rod varies from point to point, we use a linear density function, ρ(x), to denote the density of the rod at any point, x.
summary: Introduction to the complexities of calculating mass with variable density using a linear density function.

header: Constructing a Partition for Density Function Integration
content: Let ρ(x) be an integrable linear density function. Now, for i=0, 1, 2,…, n let P={x} be a regular partition of the interval ⎡a, b⎤, and for i=1, 2,…, n choose an arbitrary point x* ∈ [x_{i-1}, x_i].
summary: Setting up the necessary partition for integrating the density function.

content: The mass m of the segment of the rod from x_{i-1} to x_{i} is approximated by m ≈ ρ(x*)(x_{i}−x_{i-1}) = ρ(x*)(Δx).
summary: Mathematical approximation for the mass of segments of the rod using density function.

header: Total Mass of the Entire Rod
content: Adding the masses of all the segments gives us an approximation for the mass of the entire rod:
summary: Summation of segment masses to approximate the total mass of the entire rod.

header: Understanding Riemann Sums and Mass Calculation
content:
m= ∑ m ≈ ∑ ρ(x* )Δx.

i=1 i=1
This is a Riemann sum. Taking the limit as n→∞, we get an expression for the exact mass of the rod:
n b
m= lim ∑ ρ(x* )Δx=∫ ρ(x)dx.
n→∞ i a
i=1
summary: This section introduces the concept of Riemann sums used for calculating mass.

header: Theorem: Mass–Density Formula of a One-Dimensional Object
content:
We state this result in the following theorem.
Theorem 6.7: Mass–Density Formula of a One-Dimensional Object
Given a thin rod oriented along the x-axis over the interval ⎡a, b⎤, let ρ(x) denote a linear density function giving the density of the rod at a point x in the interval. Then the mass of the rod is given by
b (6.10)
m=∫ ρ(x)dx.
a
summary: The theorem provides a formula for calculating the mass of a one-dimensional object based on its density function.

header: Example Application of the Mass-Density Formula
content:
Example 6.23
Calculating Mass from Linear Density
Consider a thin rod oriented on the x-axis over the interval [π/2, π]. If the density of the rod is given by ρ(x)=sinx, what is the mass of the rod?
Solution
Applying Equation 6.10 directly, we have
b π

π/2

summary: This example illustrates the application of the mass-density theorem to a specific case of a rod with a sine density function.

header: Extending the Concept to Two-Dimensional Mass Calculation
content:
6.23 Consider a thin rod oriented on the x-axis over the interval [1, 3]. If the density of the rod is given by ρ(x)=2x^2 +3, what is the mass of the rod?
We now extend this concept to find the mass of a two-dimensional disk of radius r. As with the rod we looked at in the one-dimensional case, here we assume the disk is thin enough that, for mathematical purposes, we can treat it as a two-dimensional object.
summary: This segment explains how the concept of mass calculation extends from one-dimensional objects to two-dimensional disks.

header: Assumptions and Setup for Two-Dimensional Mass Calculation
content: We assume the density is given in terms of mass per unit area (called area density), and further assume the density varies only along the disk’s radius (called radial density). We orient the disk in the xy-plane, with the center at the origin. Then, the density of the disk can be treated as a function of x, denoted ρ(x). We assume ρ(x) is integrable.
summary: This section details the assumptions made for calculating the mass of a thin two-dimensional disk.

header: Partitioning and Analyzing the Disk for Mass Calculation
content: Because density is a function of x, we partition the interval from [0, r] along the x-axis. For i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of the interval [0, r], and for i=1, 2,…, n, choose an arbitrary point x* ∈[xi−1, xi].
summary: This chunk describes the process of partitioning the interval along the x-axis in preparation for mass calculation of the disk.

header: Visual Representation of the Disk and Washers
content: Now, use the partition to break up the disk into thin (two-dimensional) washers. A disk and a representative washer are depicted in the following figure.
description: This chunk refers to a figure illustrating a disk and the thin washers used in the partitioning method for mass calculation.
summary: This section introduces the concept of breaking the disk into thin washers for easier calculations.

header: Calculating Mass of a Washer in Integration Applications
content: We now approximate the density and area of the washer to calculate an approximate mass, m. Note that the area of the washer is given by A = π(x_i^2) − π(x_{i−1}^2) = π(x_i^2 − x_{i−1}^2) = π(x_i + x_{i−1})(x_i − x_{i−1}) = π(x_i + x_{i−1})Δx.
description: None
summary: The mass of a washer is calculated by determining its area based on its density.

header: Using Average Radius for Washer Mass Calculation
content: You may recall that we had an expression similar to this when we were computing volumes by shells. As we did there, we use x* ≈ (x_i + x_{i−1})/2 to approximate the average radius of the washer. We obtain A = π(x_i + x_{i−1})Δx ≈ 2πx*Δx.
description: None
summary: The average radius of the washer is utilized to refine the area calculation.

header: Mass Approximation of a Washer Using Density
content: Using ρ(x*) to approximate the density of the washer, we approximate the mass of the washer by m ≈ 2πx* ρ(x*)Δx.
description: None
summary: Density is incorporated into the calculation for the mass of the washer.

header: Total Mass Calculation from Washer Masses
content: Adding up the masses of the washers, we see the mass m of the entire disk is approximated by m = ∑ m_i ≈ ∑ 2πx* ρ(x*)Δx.
description: None
summary: The total mass of the disk is obtained by summing the masses of individual washers.

content: We again recognize this as a Riemann sum, and take the limit as n→∞. This gives us m = lim (n→∞) ∑ 2πx* ρ(x*)Δx = ∫ 2πxρ(x)dx.
description: None
summary: The process consolidates into a Riemann sum, leading to an integral expression.

header: Theorem: Mass–Density Formula for Circular Objects
content: We summarize these findings in the following theorem. Theorem 6.8: Mass–Density Formula of a Circular Object. Let ρ(x) be an integrable function representing the radial density of a disk of radius r. Then the mass of the disk is given by m = ∫_0^r 2πxρ(x)dx.
description: None
summary: The mass-density relationship for circular objects is formally presented in a theorem.

summary: This section details how to calculate the mass of a disk using radial density.

header: Applications of Integration: Additional Mass Calculation Example
content: Let ρ(x)=3x+2 represent the radial density of a disk. Calculate the mass of a disk of radius 2.
summary: This example continues the mass calculation theme using a different density function.

header: Physics Concept: Work Related to Force
content: We now consider work. In physics, work is related to force, which is often intuitively defined as a push or pull on an object. When a force moves an object, we say the force does work on the object. In other words, work can be thought of as the amount of energy it takes to move an object. According to physics, when we have a constant force, work can be expressed as the product of force and distance.
summary: This chunk introduces the concept of work in relation to force in physics.

header: Units of Work and Force in Different Systems
content: In the English system, the unit of force is the pound and the unit of distance is the foot, so work is given in foot-pounds. In the metric system, kilograms and meters are used. One newton is the force needed to accelerate 1 kilogram of mass at the rate of 1 m/sec2. Thus, the most common unit of work is thenewton-meter. This same unit is also called the joule. Both are defined as kilograms times meters squared over seconds squared (kg·m²/s²).
summary: This section explains the units used for work and force in different measurement systems.

content: When we have a constant force, things are pretty easy. It is rare, however, for a force to be constant. The work done to compress (or elongate) a spring, for example, varies depending on how far the spring has already been compressed (or stretched). We look at springs in more detail later in this section.
summary: This chunk discusses the complications of calculating work when dealing with variable forces.

header: Calculating Work Done by Variable Force
content: Suppose we have a variable force F(x) that moves an object in a positive direction along the x-axis from point a to point b. To calculate the work done, we partition the interval [a, b] and estimate the work done over each subinterval. So, for i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of the interval [a, b], and for i=1, 2,…, n, choose an arbitrary point x* ∈ [x_i−1, x_i].
summary: This section outlines the methodology to calculate work done by a variable force.

content: To calculate the work done to move an object from point x_i−1 to point x_i, we assume the force is roughly constant over the interval, and use F(x*) to approximate the force. The work done over the interval [x_i−1, x_i], then, is given by W ≈ F(x*)(x_i - x_i−1) = F(x*)Δx.
summary: This chunk discusses how to approximate work done using constant force assumptions over small intervals.

content: Therefore, the work done over the interval [a, b] is approximately W = ∑ W_i ≈ ∑ F(x*)Δx from i=1 to n.
summary: This section describes how to sum the work calculations over the entire interval.

header: Limit of Work Calculation for Exact Value
content: Taking the limit of this expression as n→∞ gives us the exact value for work.
summary: This chunk explains how to reach the exact value of work through limits in calculus.

header: Understanding Work in Integration - Work Definition
content: W = lim ∑ F(x* )Δx=∫ F(x)dx. Thus, we can define work as follows. Definition If a variable force F(x) moves an object in a positive direction along the x-axis from point a to point b, then the work done on the object is W = ∫ F(x)dx.
summary: This section defines work done by a variable force in integration.

content: Note that if F is constant, the integral evaluates to F·(b−a)=F·d, which is the formula we stated at the beginning of this section.
summary: This details the evaluation of work when force is constant.

header: Real-world Example: Spring Compression and Elongation
content: Now let’s look at the specific example of the work done to compress or elongate a spring. Consider a block attached to a horizontal spring. The block moves back and forth as the spring stretches and compresses.
summary: This chunk introduces the example of a block and spring to illustrate work.

header: Friction and Equilibrium in Spring Systems
content: Although in the real world we would have to account for the force of friction between the block and the surface on which it is resting, we ignore friction here and assume the block is resting on a frictionless surface. When the spring is at its natural length (at rest), the system is said to be at equilibrium.
summary: This section discusses the neglect of friction and the concept of equilibrium in the spring system.

content: In this state, the spring is neither elongated nor compressed, and in this equilibrium position the block does not move until some force is introduced. We orient the system such that x=0 corresponds to the equilibrium position (see the following figure).
description: Figure 6.51 A block attached to a horizontal spring at equilibrium, compressed, and elongated.
summary: This describes the equilibrium position of the spring and the block.

content: According to Hooke’s law, the force required to compress or stretch a spring from an equilibrium position is given by F(x)=kx, for some constant k. The value of k depends on the physical characteristics of the spring. The constant k is called the spring constant and is always positive.
summary: This section introduces Hooke's law and the spring constant.

header: Calculating Work on a Spring - Example Introduction
content: We can use this information to calculate the work done to compress or elongate a spring, as shown in the following example.
summary: This prepares the reader for an example calculating work on a spring.

header: Spring Work Calculation - Compression and Stretching
content: Suppose it takes a force of 10 N (in the negative direction) to compress a spring 0.2 m from the equilibrium position. How much work is done to stretch the spring 0.5 m from the equilibrium position? Solution First find the spring constant, k. When x = -0.2, we know F(x) = -10, so F(x) = kx -10 = k(-0.2) k = 50 and F(x) = 50x.
summary: This section discusses how to calculate the work done in stretching a spring using the spring constant.

header: Calculating the Work Done on a Spring
content: Then, to calculate work, we integrate the force function, obtaining W = ∫ F(x)dx = ∫ 50xdx = 25x^2|_0^0.5. The work done to stretch the spring is 6.25 J.
summary: The section provides the steps to calculate work done on the spring through integration.

header: Work Calculation for Spring Stretching Example
content: Suppose it takes a force of 8 lb to stretch a spring 6 in. from the equilibrium position. How much work is done to stretch the spring 1 ft from the equilibrium position?
summary: This section presents a new example for calculating work done in stretching a spring.

content: Consider the work done to pump water (or some other liquid) out of a tank. Pumping problems are a little more complicated than spring problems because many of the calculations depend on the shape and size of the tank. In addition, instead of being concerned about the work done to move a single mass, we are looking at the work done to move a volume of water, and it takes more work to move the water from the bottom of the tank than it does to move the water from the top of the tank.
summary: This section introduces the concept of calculating work for pumping liquids, emphasizing the complexity involved.

header: Work Calculation for a Cylindrical Tank
content: We examine the process in the context of a cylindrical tank, then look at a couple of examples using tanks of different shapes. Assume a cylindrical tank of radius 4 m and height 10 m is filled to a depth of 8 m. How much work does it take to pump all the water over the top edge of the tank?
summary: This section sets up a problem for calculating work needed to pump water from a cylindrical tank.

header: Defining the Frame of Reference for Pumping
content: The first thing we need to do is define a frame of reference. We let x represent the vertical distance below the top of the tank. That is, we orient the x-axis vertically, with the origin at the top of the tank and the downward direction being positive.
description: Figure illustrating the frame of reference for the cylindrical tank pumping problem.
summary: This section describes how to set up the coordinate system for analyzing work done in pumping water.

header: Introduction to Work Calculation in Integration Applications
content: Figure 6.52 How much work is needed to empty a tank partially filled with water? Using this coordinate system, the water extends from x=2 to x=10. Therefore, we partition the interval [2, 10] and look at the work required to lift each individual “layer” of water. So, for i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of the interval [2, 10], and for i=1, 2,…, n, choose an arbitrary point x* ∈[x , x].
description: Figure 6.52 illustrates the question of how much work is required to empty a tank that is partially filled with water, providing a visual representation of the tank and water level.
summary: This section introduces the calculation of work needed to empty a tank using integration.

header: Understanding the Representational Layer in Water Tank Applications
content: Figure 6.53 shows a representative layer.
description: Figure 6.53 presents a representative layer of water, highlighting how layers are analyzed for lifting calculations.
summary: This chunk describes a visual representation that supports understanding of the water lifting calculation.

header: Force Calculation for Water Lifting in Tank Problems
content: In pumping problems, the force required to lift the water to the top of the tank is the force required to overcome gravity, so it is equal to the weight of the water. Given that the weight-density of water is 9800 N/m3, or 62.4 lb/ft3, calculating the volume of each layer gives us the weight. In this case, we have 2 V = π(4) Δx = 16πΔx. Then, the force needed to lift each layer is F = 9800·16πΔx = 156,800πΔx.
summary: This section focuses on calculating the force needed to lift water layers within the tank.

header: Considerations for Non-Cylindrical Tanks in Work Calculation
content: Note that this step becomes a little more difficult if we have a non-cylindrical tank. We look at a non-cylindrical tank in the next example. We also need to know the distance the water must be lifted. Based on our choice of coordinate systems, we can use x* as an approximation of the distance the layer must be lifted.
summary: This chunk addresses the complexities involved when dealing with non-cylindrical tanks.

header: Work Calculation for the Whole Tank Using Integration
content: Then the work to lift the ith layer of water W is approximately W ≈ 156,800πx* Δx. Adding the work for each layer, we see the approximate work to empty the tank is given by...
summary: Here we summarize how to calculate the total work required to empty the tank by lifting each water layer.

content:
For pumping problems, the calculations vary depending on the shape of the tank or container. The following problem-solving strategy lays out a step-by-step process for solving pumping problems.
Problem-Solving Strategy: Solving Pumping Problems
1. Sketch a picture of the tank and select an appropriate frame of reference.
2. Calculate the volume of a representative layer of water.
3. Multiply the volume by the weight-density of water to get the force.
4. Calculate the distance the layer of water must be lifted.
5. Multiply the force and distance to get an estimate of the work needed to lift the layer of water.
6. Sum the work required to lift all the layers. This expression is an estimate of the work required to pump out the desired amount of water, and it is in the form of a Riemann sum.
7. Take the limit as n→∞ and evaluate the resulting integral to get the exact work required to pump out the desired amount of water.
summary: This section details a problem-solving strategy tailored for calculating work in pumping scenarios.

header: Example of a Pumping Problem with a Noncylindrical Tank
content:
We now apply this problem-solving strategy in an example with a noncylindrical tank.
Example 6.26
A Pumping Problem with a Noncylindrical Tank
Assume a tank in the shape of an inverted cone, with height 12 ft and base radius 4 ft. The tank is full to start with, and water is pumped over the upper edge of the tank until the height of the water remaining in the tank is 4 ft. How much work is required to pump out that amount of water?
summary: An example illustrates the application of the previously described strategy to a noncylindrical tank.

content:
Solution
The tank is depicted in Figure 6.54. As we did in the example with the cylindrical tank, we orient the x-axis vertically, with the origin at the top of the tank and the downward direction being positive (step 1).

content: A water tank in the shape of an inverted cone. The tank starts out full and ends with 4 ft of water left, so, based on our chosen frame of reference, we need to partition the interval [0, 8]. Then, for i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of the interval [0, 8], and for i=1, 2,…, n, choose an arbitrary point x* ∈[x_i-1, x_i].

How much work is required to pump out that amount of water?

header: Understanding Hydrostatic Force and Pressure within Integration Applications
content: In this last section, we look at the force and pressure exerted on an object submerged in a liquid. In the English system, force is measured in pounds. In the metric system, it is measured in newtons. Pressure is force per unit area, so in the English system we have pounds per square foot (or, perhaps more commonly, pounds per square inch, denoted psi). In the metric system, we have newtons per square meter, also called pascals.
summary: This section introduces the concepts of hydrostatic force and pressure relevant to fluids.

content: Let’s begin with the simple case of a plate of area A submerged horizontally in water at a depth s (Figure 6.56). Then, the force exerted on the plate is simply the weight of the water above it, which is given by F = ρAs, where ρ is the weight density of water (weight per unit volume). To find the hydrostatic pressure—that is, the pressure exerted by water on a submerged object—we divide the force by the area. So the pressure is p = F/A = ρs.
description: Figure 6.56 shows a plate submerged horizontally in water.
summary: This chunk discusses how to calculate hydrostatic pressure using force and area.

header: Principle of Pascal and Its Application
content: By Pascal’s principle, the pressure at a given depth is the same in all directions, so it does not matter if the plate is submerged horizontally or vertically. So, as long as we know the depth, we know the pressure. We can apply Pascal’s principle to find the force exerted on surfaces, such as dams, that are oriented vertically.
summary: This chunk explains the implications of Pascal's principle for submerged objects.

header: Calculating Force on Vertical Surfaces using Integrals
content: We cannot apply the formula F = ρAs directly because the depth varies from point to point on a vertically oriented surface. So, as we have done many times before, we form a partition, a Riemann sum, and, ultimately, a definite integral to calculate the force.
summary: This section prepares for the calculation of force on vertically oriented surfaces.

header: Defining the Reference Frame for Depth and Width
content: Suppose a thin plate is submerged in water. We choose our frame of reference such that the x-axis is oriented vertically, with the downward direction being positive, and point x=0 corresponding to a logical reference point. Let s(x) denote the depth at point x. Note we often let x=0 correspond to the surface of the water.
summary: This chunk sets up the reference frame for depth and width in calculating forces.

header: General Case of Calculating Force on a Submerged Plate
content: However, in some cases we may want to select a different reference point for x=0, so we proceed with the development in the more general case. Last, let w(x) denote the width of the plate at the point x. Assume the top edge of the plate is at point x=a and the bottom edge of the plate is at point x=b. Then, for i=0, 1, 2,…, n, let P={x} be a regular partition of the interval ⎡a, b⎤, and for i=1, 2,…, n, choose an arbitrary point x* ∈[x_i, x_{i-1}].
summary: This section explains the general case scenario for calculating the force on a submerged plate.

header: Visual Aid for Understanding Force Distribution
content: The partition divides the plate into several thin, rectangular strips (see the following figure).
description: The following figure illustrates how the partition divides the plate into thin rectangular strips.
summary: This chunk describes the visualization of the partition-ing of the submerged plate.

header: Hydrostatic Force Estimation in Fluid Mechanics
content: Let’s now estimate the force on a representative strip. If the strip is thin enough, we can treat it as if it is at a constant depth, s(x*). We then have F = ρAs = ρ[w(x*)Δx]s(x*). Adding the forces, we get an estimate for the force on the plate: F ≈ ∑ F = ∑ ρ[w(x*)Δx]s(x*). This is a Riemann sum, so taking the limit gives us the exact force. We obtain F = lim ∑ ρ[w(x*)Δx]s(x*) = ∫ ρw(x)s(x)dx.
summary: This section describes the estimation of hydrostatic force on a submerged strip using integration.

content: Evaluating this integral gives us the force on the plate. We summarize this in the following problem-solving strategy.
summary: This continues the discussion on calculating the force on the submerged plate using integration.

content: Problem-Solving Strategy: Finding Hydrostatic Force 1. Sketch a picture and select an appropriate frame of reference. (Note that if we select a frame of reference other than the one used earlier, we may have to adjust Equation 6.13 accordingly.) 2. Determine the depth and width functions, s(x) and w(x). 3. Determine the weight-density of whatever liquid with which you are working. The weight-density of water is 62.4 lb/ft3, or 9800 N/m3. 4. Use the equation to calculate the total force.
summary: This section outlines a systematic approach to finding hydrostatic forces on submerged objects.
